Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses 240 annotated 3D CT scans (typically 228 for training, 12 for testing), exceeding the >50 training-subject threshold.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset clearly described as the 'Computational Anatomy' shared dataset from Tokushima University Hospital; acquisition context and scan types provided.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Primary split described (random 5% test from torso/chest/abdomen; 228 train/12 test) and repeated 3 times; also reports 4-fold cross-validation (75/25).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual annotations by multiple groups, then validated/refined by an expert committee; mentions semiautomatic support tool for annotation.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset beyond the shared dataset used for training/testing.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: FCN (VGG16-based) architecture and 3D-2D-3D voting pipeline described; includes FCN variants (32s/16s/8s/4s/2s), skip connections, and deconvolution details.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides key hyperparameters (optimizer choices, LR=1e-4, batch size=20, momentum=0.9, weight decay reported as '2-4' likely 2e-4, dropout/LCN settings, iterations). However, some details are incomplete/ambiguous (exact loss name, LR schedule, data sampling specifics, and final chosen FCN variant for main results).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss described as 'sum of pixel-wise losses' between prediction and label map (implies softmax cross-entropy), but not explicitly specified or justified.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing described: no normalization; 12-bit CT mapped linearly to 8-bit 0–255 and duplicated to RGB channels; multi-view slice extraction; postprocessing voting and tie-break rule described.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release; only states implementation in Caffe based on reference FCN code.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports IoU (Jaccard) per organ plus mean voxel accuracy, mean IoU, and frequency-weighted IoU.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean and standard deviation of IoU per organ across test cases and repeats; no confidence intervals or hypothesis testing.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against conventional multi-organ method (Okada et al.) on same split for abdomen subset; also compares to single 3D U-Net (ROI-sized) and Cascade U-Nets (hierarchical 3D FCNs) on same train/test split; includes optimizer and architecture ablations.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides per-organ IoU means and SDs for 19 targets (training and testing).

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations: poor performance on small/tubular structures, limited resolution, dependence on transfer learning, and inability to run full-volume 3D FCN/3D U-Net due to GPU memory.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient inclusion is described at dataset level (200 patients, multiple scans per some patients) and test sampling is random with constraint that multiple scans from same patient only used for training, but detailed inclusion/exclusion criteria and lesion distribution are not specified; potential correlation/heterogeneity remains.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - Outcomes assessed consistently using the same ground-truth annotations and the same evaluation metrics across methods/splits; majority voting and tie-break rules are fixed.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Reports many metrics and per-organ IoU, but lacks Dice/HD and does not provide full details for all compared methods (e.g., exact settings for 3D U-Net/Cascade U-Nets) and no external validation; some negative results are reported (training from scratch fails), which reduces but does not eliminate reporting concerns.

detailed_extraction.title: Deep learning of the sectional appearances of 3D CT images for anatomical structure segmentation based on an FCN voting method

method.architecture_name: 2D FCN (VGG16-based) + 3D multi-view majority voting (3D-2D-3D)
key_innovations: Reformulates 3D multi-organ CT segmentation as multi-view 2D semantic segmentation followed by voxel-wise 3D label fusion via majority voting.
Uses whole 2D slice as receptive field (FCN) to leverage global context and handle arbitrary scan ranges and image sizes.
Applies transfer learning from ImageNet-pretrained VGG16 and progressive fine-tuning from coarse-to-fine FCN variants (FCN32s→16s→8s→4s→2s).
Tie-breaking rule for 3-way voting conflicts using a largest-organ-volume prior.
loss_functions: Sum of pixel-wise losses between prediction and ground-truth label map (not explicitly named; consistent with softmax cross-entropy in FCN/Caffe)
method.epochs: Not reported (training described by iterations: 80k/160k/550k; main FCN8s trained for 160k iterations)

names: Computational Anatomy project shared dataset (Tokushima University Hospital)
dataset.total_subjects: 200 patients (240 annotated CT scans used; from a larger pool of 640 CT scans)
dataset.ct_scanner: Toshiba Aquilion multislice CT
dataset.slice_thickness: 1.0 mm slice thickness; in-plane resolution 0.625–1.148 mm (reported as distributed); variable protocols/kernels; 155/240 contrast-enhanced
dataset.image_size: Variable; example 512×512×221; maximum mentioned up to 512×512×1141

segmented_organs: Portal vein / splenic vein / superior mesenteric vein region

results.inference_time: 3 minutes 43 seconds per CT scan (example size 512×512×221)
results.gpu_memory: 12 GB (GPU model: TITAN X; memory limitation discussed for 3D FCN/3D U-Net)

comparison_results.Okada et al. (conventional) comparison: Compared on 7 contrast-enhanced abdomen CT test cases; proposed method better for 6/7 organs and worse for gallbladder (exact per-organ values not fully available in provided text).
comparison_results.Cascade U-Nets (hierarchical 3D FCNs): Frequency-weighted IoU comparable (80.5% vs 80.3% on same 12 test scans); proposed better for 9 targets, worse for 9 small/tubular targets (as described).

implementation.gpu: NVIDIA GeForce GTX TITAN X (12 GB)
implementation.training_time: Approximately 3 days

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a practical multi-organ CT segmentation pipeline that uses a single 2D FCN applied to orthogonal slices and fuses predictions into a 3D segmentation via voxel-wise majority voting.
contribution_summary.novelty: At the time, it demonstrated scale-/scan-range-flexible multi-organ segmentation (up to 19 targets, including rarely reported regions like stomach lumen/contents) using a single FCN model with 3D reconstruction by multi-view voting, avoiding full 3D CNN memory constraints.
strengths: Large annotated dataset for 2017 (240 CT scans) with expert-validated labels across varied scan ranges (torso/chest/abdomen/pelvis) and protocols.
Clear, simple 3D-2D-3D design that is computationally feasible on 12 GB GPUs and supports arbitrary volume sizes via FCN.
Per-organ quantitative reporting (mean and SD IoU) and multiple global semantic-segmentation metrics (voxel accuracy, mean IoU, frequency-weighted IoU).
Ablations/analyses: transfer learning necessity, optimizer comparison (Adam vs SGD), iteration count, FCN stride variants, and effect of voting.
weaknesses: No external validation; testing is small (12 scans per repeat) and no patient-level stratification details beyond avoiding same-patient leakage into test.
Performance is poor for small/tubular structures (e.g., esophagus, small vessels, pancreas) and for some pelvic organs; method uses only 3 orthogonal views.
Loss function not explicitly specified/justified; limited training reproducibility details (e.g., LR schedule, augmentation absent).
No public code/model release.
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: runtime (~3.7 min/scan) and ability to handle variable scan ranges are clinically relevant for pre-processing and workflow support, but low accuracy on several clinically important small structures and lack of external validation limit immediate deployment without further refinement and validation.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training uses 261 CT volumes aggregated from multiple public sources (>50 subjects).

Q2_dataset_source.justification: All sources are explicitly named: MICCAI 2015 HaN challenge train/test, TCIA Head-Neck Cetuximab, and TCIA Quebec (4 institutions).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Clear split: datasets 1–3 combined for training (261); dataset 4 (MICCAI 2015 test, n=10) used for final testing; they state datasets are nonoverlapping.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Ground truth is described as manually delineated OAR masks; they describe RT-structure-to-mask conversion and label-name harmonization, but provide limited detail on annotator expertise, inter-rater variability, or annotation protocol.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Primary evaluation is on MICCAI 2015 test set (external to their training aggregation). They also show qualitative results on a small internal/holdout set, but no quantitative external multi-center validation beyond the MICCAI test benchmark.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is well specified: modified 3D U-Net with one downsampling, 3D SE-residual blocks, concatenation skip connections, and 10-class softmax output; SE equations and design choices are provided.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides framework (PyTorch), GPU (Tesla P40), batch size=1, optimizer sequence (RMSprop then SGD+momentum), learning rates, epochs, and augmentations. However, some details are missing/unclear (exact data normalization, cropping specifics, weight decay, LR schedule, random seeds, validation split definition).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Hybrid Dice + focal loss is mathematically defined, motivated by extreme class imbalance and small-structure segmentation; includes masking/weighting for missing labels and reports tuning of k.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Describes multi-source curation steps (name mapping, CT-RT correspondence, RT conversion, chest removal/cropping) and on-the-fly augmentation (affine + elastic). Postprocessing is minimal/none.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is indicated.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice as primary metric and additionally reports 95th percentile Hausdorff distance (HD95) per organ.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean ± standard deviation for Dice/HD95 on the test set, but no confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, or statistical significance analysis.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against MICCAI 2015 challenge best results and multiple prior methods (atlas-based and CNN-based), with tabulated results.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides per-organ Dice and per-organ HD95; also includes ablations per organ (downsampling schemes, blocks, loss functions).

Q15_limitations.justification: Explicitly discusses limitations: lack of shape/connectivity priors, outlier false positives affecting surface metrics, annotation inconsistency across sources, and metric relevance to clinical practice.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient selection is indirectly defined by inclusion of specific public datasets; however, inclusion/exclusion criteria and patient characteristics are not detailed, and training data are heterogeneous across institutions.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Outcomes are assessed consistently on a fixed benchmark (MICCAI 2015 test) with standard metrics; however, missing annotations in training and heterogeneous annotation standards may introduce systematic performance differences across organs.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - Per-organ results and ablations are reported, but external quantitative validation beyond the MICCAI test set is limited and some implementation details are insufficient for full replication.

detailed_extraction.title: AnatomyNet: Deep learning for fast and fully automated whole-volume segmentation of head and neck anatomy

method.architecture_name: AnatomyNet (modified 3D U-Net with 3D SE-residual blocks)
key_innovations: Whole-volume (one-shot) 3D segmentation with only one downsampling layer to preserve small-structure detail under GPU memory constraints
Integration of 3D squeeze-and-excitation (SE) residual blocks for channel-wise feature recalibration in 3D segmentation
Hybrid loss combining Dice loss and focal loss to address extreme voxel imbalance for small OARs
Masked and class-weighted loss to train with missing annotations across multi-source datasets
loss_functions: Masked & weighted focal loss (gamma implied by (1-p)^2 term)
Hybrid: Dice + k * focal (k tuned; best reported k=0.5 in loss-function experiments; k searched in {0.1,0.5,1})
method.learning_rate: RMSprop lr=0.002 (150 epochs), then SGD lr=0.001 with momentum=0.9 (50 epochs)

names: MICCAI Head and Neck Auto Segmentation Challenge 2015 (training set)
TCIA Head-Neck Cetuximab collection
TCIA Quebec head & neck RT dataset (4 institutions)
MICCAI Head and Neck Auto Segmentation Challenge 2015 (test set)
dataset.image_size: Typical HaN CT around 178×512×512; inference example volume 178×302×225; variable sizes due to cropping/settings

results.inference_time: 0.12 s per CT volume (example 178×302×225)

comparison_results.MICCAI 2015 best (average DSC): 0.76 (reported as best challenge average across organs; note label-definition differences for paired organs)
comparison_results.Ren et al. (selected organs): e.g., chiasm 0.58±0.17; optic nerves 0.72±0.08 and 0.70±0.09 (as tabulated)
comparison_results.Wang et al. (selected organs): e.g., brain stem 0.90±0.04; mandible 0.94±0.01; parotids 0.83±0.06 (as tabulated)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Proposes AnatomyNet, an end-to-end whole-volume 3D CNN for fast multi-OAR head-and-neck CT segmentation that improves MICCAI 2015 benchmark Dice while running in ~0.12 s/volume.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines (i) a memory-aware 3D U-Net variant with only one downsampling for small-structure preservation, (ii) 3D SE-residual blocks for improved representation, and (iii) a masked/weighted hybrid Dice+focal loss to address extreme class imbalance and missing labels in multi-source RT datasets.
strengths: Large aggregated multi-institution training set (261 volumes) and evaluation on a standard public benchmark test set (MICCAI 2015, n=10)
Clear ablation studies (downsampling depth, skip fusion strategy, residual vs SE-residual blocks, loss functions)
Addresses practical RT dataset issues (missing annotations) via masked and class-weighted loss
Very fast inference with whole-volume, one-pass multi-organ output
weaknesses: No public code/model release; some preprocessing and training details are insufficient for exact replication
Limited quantitative external validation beyond the MICCAI 2015 test set; additional holdout results are mainly qualitative
Surface-distance performance (HD95) can be worse than ROI/cascade methods due to outlier predictions; limited explicit mechanisms for shape/connectivity priors
Training data annotation inconsistency across sources is acknowledged and can degrade performance for some organs
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High potential for RT planning workflows due to fully automated multi-OAR segmentation and very low inference time; however, clinical deployment would require stronger validation across diverse scanners/institutions, robustness checks for outliers, and evaluation with clinically relevant endpoints beyond Dice (e.g., dosimetric impact).

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training/validation uses 81 subjects (>50), with 92 additional independent test subjects; total 173 CTU cases.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Single-institution dataset from University of Michigan Abdominal Imaging Division; acquisition protocol and scanner vendor line provided (GE LightSpeed MDCT; 120 kVp, 120–280 mA; excretory phase ~12 min post-contrast).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Clear split: 81 training/validation and 92 independent test; additionally 7-case validation subset and 74-case training subset used for model selection, then retrained on all 81.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Experienced radiologist provided 3D hand outlines by slice-by-slice boundary tracing; masks generated from these outlines; first/last bladder slice also marked.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an external multi-institution or publicly available independent dataset; only internal independent test split.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: 2D and 3D U-Net variants described with kernel sizes, padding, down/up paths, concatenation strategy, activations, and sigmoid output; figures included.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Reports optimizer (Adam), loss (binary cross-entropy), learning rate (1e-4), epochs (100 for 2D, 400 for 3D), batch sizes (2D=4; 3D=1 or 2 depending on input), weight initialization (N(0,0.02)), input sizes, threshold selection procedure, and GPU model.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Binary cross-entropy is stated but not substantively justified against alternatives (e.g., Dice loss); rationale is minimal.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is clearly described: exclude non-bladder slices using radiologist-marked first/last slice; optional downsampling 512->256 via 2x2 box filter + stride 2; automated center cropping; 3D fixed 192-slice stack selection centered between first/last slice; postprocessing threshold=0.2 + largest connected component.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release reported.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses multiple metrics: AVI, AVE, AAVE, AMD, AHD, and AJI (Jaccard).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports statistical significance (P < 0.001; P < 0.006) for comparisons, but does not provide confidence intervals or detailed statistical test description.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against their prior DCNN-LS and two non-DL level-set methods (CLASS-LCR, LS-HF). Does not benchmark against broader contemporary public SOTA on shared datasets.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Single-organ study (bladder only), so per-organ reporting is inherently satisfied but not applicable to multi-organ segmentation.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations: need for user marking first/last slice; GPU memory constraints preventing full-volume 3D input; dataset still small for DL; notes suboptimal cases and possible reasons 3D underperforms 2D.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient inclusion criteria beyond being collected at one institution and undergoing cystoscopy/biopsy are not fully specified; split was 'balanced by difficulty' but exact procedure not detailed; single-center cohort may limit representativeness.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Reference standard is consistent (single experienced radiologist hand outlines), but reliance on radiologist-marked first/last slice introduces user interaction that could vary; evaluation metrics are consistently applied across methods.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - Reports multiple metrics and comparisons and mentions failure counts; however, lacks external validation and does not report some commonly expected segmentation metrics (e.g., Dice) and does not provide full distributional statistics beyond mean±SD and histograms.

detailed_extraction.title: U-Net based deep learning bladder segmentation in CT urography

method.architecture_name: Modified 2D U-Net and modified 3D U-Net (U-DL)
key_innovations: Automated bladder segmentation in CT urography without user-input bounding box and without level-set refinement (compared to prior DCNN-LS pipeline).
Systematic comparison of 2D vs 3D U-Net-style models, input resolution (512 vs 256), and automated center-cropping to trade off accuracy vs compute.
Simple postprocessing (threshold + largest connected component) and empirical threshold selection on a validation subset.

names: University of Michigan CT urography (CTU) dataset (institutional)
dataset.ct_scanner: GE Healthcare LightSpeed MDCT scanners
dataset.slice_thickness: Reconstruction interval 1.25 mm or 0.625 mm
dataset.image_size: 2D inputs: 512x512 (optionally downsampled to 256x256; cropped to 256x256 from 512 or 128x128 from 256). 3D inputs: 256x256x192 or 128x128x192 (fixed 192-slice stack).

challenging_organs: bladder (variable opacification; unclear wall boundaries; apex/base partial volume; advanced cancer invasion; poor image quality/metal artifacts)

results.primary_metric: AJI (3D Jaccard index) and AVI (volume intersection ratio) (Dice not reported)

hausdorff_distance.bladder: AHD 9.7 ± 7.6 mm (best 2D U-DL, test); 11.4 ± 10.0 mm (best 3D U-DL, test)

results.gpu_memory: 3D 256x256x192 model ~11 GB GPU memory; 512x512x192 too large for 12 GB GPU

implementation.training_time: Per-epoch: 2D 512x512 with cropping 18.5 min (without cropping 70.0 min); 3D 512x512 with cropping 32.5 min; 3D 256x256 with cropping 10 min (Table IV).

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces and validates a U-Net-based bladder segmentation approach for CT urography that removes the need for a user-defined bounding box and level-set refinement, improving accuracy over prior DCNN-LS and classical level-set baselines on an independent test set.
contribution_summary.novelty: Compared to their earlier DCNN+level-set method requiring a user bounding box, this work demonstrates a more automated U-Net-style pipeline (2D and 3D variants) with empirical design choices (resolution, cropping, thresholding) and statistically significant improvements on a relatively large internal test cohort for CTU bladder segmentation.
strengths: Reasonably sized dataset for a single-organ CT segmentation study (173 subjects) with an independent test set (92).
Clear description of model variants (2D vs 3D), preprocessing, and postprocessing; includes compute/memory constraints and training-time comparisons.
Multiple evaluation metrics including overlap- and distance-based measures; statistical significance reported for key comparisons.
Clinically relevant cohort with variable bladder opacification and pathology (masses, wall thickening, normal).
weaknesses: No external validation; single-center data limits generalizability.
Still requires user input (marking first/last bladder slice) for both training/testing workflow, so not fully automated.
No public code/model; limited reproducibility beyond textual description.
Does not report Dice score (common in segmentation literature), making cross-paper comparison harder.
3D model constrained by GPU memory and uses a fixed 192-slice stack, potentially limiting robustness.
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate to high for CT urography workflows: improved bladder segmentation accuracy and reduced interaction compared with bounding-box-based methods could support downstream CAD for bladder cancer detection/response assessment; however, remaining user interaction (first/last slice marking) and lack of external validation are barriers to immediate deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses 45 DECT volumes total with 5-fold CV (per fold: 27 train / 9 val / 9 test). Training set per fold is <50 subjects, so not adequate by the stated threshold, but still a non-trivial clinical dataset for DECT.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Source and acquisition are clearly described: clinical thorax/abdomen DECT from University Hospital Erlangen, scanned on Siemens SOMATOM Definition Force with detailed protocol (70 kV and Sn150 kV, contrast agent, reconstruction kernel).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Split methodology is clearly described: manifold-learning (LLE) + k-means clustering to reduce selection bias, then stratified random selection with 3:1:1 ratio within clusters; 5-fold cross-validation.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States that six organs were manually segmented and 'ground truth was generated manually by experts', but lacks details such as number of annotators, inter-rater agreement, annotation tools, or quality control.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset; robustness is shown only via a few special-case examples (protocol change, pathology, missing organ, truncation) without a separate cohort.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Four DECT fusion architectures are described with diagrams and equations (LC-net, MC-net, MI-net, late fusion), plus residual-block variant design (full pre-activation, dimension matching).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides many training details: patch size (32^3) with overlap 16, downsampling factor 2, batch sizes (train 6/val 3), optimizer (Adam), initial LR 5e-5 with step-down by 0.1 every 10 epochs until convergence, augmentations list, postprocessing (largest CC). Framework is not specified.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss is fully specified (weighted multi-class Dice) with explicit class-weighting formula combining median frequency balancing and boundary compensation; includes discussion/ablation on balance weight k.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing and postprocessing are clearly described: downsampling, patch extraction strategy, extensive augmentation, and largest connected component filtering.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is indicated.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice as primary metric and additionally AUC and Hausdorff distance; also reports training performance metrics (parameters, epochs, training time).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports p-values for some comparisons (residual vs non-residual) but does not provide confidence intervals, effect sizes, or detailed statistical testing protocol across all comparisons.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against single-energy baselines (low-energy-only and high-energy-only 3D U-Net) and compares multiple fusion strategies; does not benchmark against broader external SOTA multi-organ CT segmentation methods on shared public datasets.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides per-organ Dice, AUC, and Hausdorff distance for six organs.

Q15_limitations.justification: Explicit limitations section: small dataset, patch-wise artifacts and spatial relationship issues, patch size constraints, sensitivity to loss hyperparameter k; includes qualitative failure example.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient inclusion criteria are not fully specified; however, acquisition protocol is consistent and they attempt to reduce split bias using manifold-learning-based stratified sampling rather than purely random splits.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - outcomes are assessed consistently with fixed metrics and 5-fold CV; manual ground truth is expert-made but annotation protocol and inter-rater variability are not described.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - reports multiple metrics and per-organ results and discusses limitations; however, no external validation and no code release limit transparency and generalizability.

detailed_extraction.title: Automatic multi-organ segmentation in dual-energy CT (DECT) with dedicated 3D fully convolutional DECT networks

method.architecture_name: Dedicated 3D U-Net-based FCNs for DECT fusion (LC-net, MC-net, MI-net, Late fusion) with optional residual blocks
key_innovations: Systematic comparison of four DECT fusion strategies embedded within 3D FCN/U-Net-style segmentation networks (image-level, early feature-level, late feature-level, and output-level fusion).
Trainable fusion parameters: LC-net learns alpha blending; late fusion learns per-class output fusion weights.
Two-branch encoder (MI-net) to extract energy-specific features separately and fuse at bottleneck/skip connections.
Weighted multi-class Dice loss combining median frequency balancing with boundary compensation to address class imbalance and boundary accuracy.
Residual-block integration tailored for U-Net with dimension matching and full pre-activation.
loss_functions: Weighted multi-class Dice loss with median frequency balancing
Boundary compensation term controlled by hyperparameter k
method.learning_rate: Initial 5e-5; reduced by factor 0.1 every 10 epochs until convergence
method.epochs: Early stopping; examples reported: 146–191 epochs (non-residual) and 179–240 epochs (residual), depending on architecture

names: 45 clinical thorax/abdomen dual-energy CT (DECT) datasets (single-center clinical cohort)
dataset.ct_scanner: Siemens SOMATOM Definition Force (dual-source)
dataset.slice_thickness: 0.6 mm (z spacing reported as 0.6 mm; in-plane spacing 0.6895–0.9766 mm)
dataset.image_size: 512×512 pixels; 992–1290 slices per volume

challenging_organs: spleen (noted with low-Dice failure example and patch-edge artifacts)
kidneys (discussed as benefiting from higher boundary weighting k)

results.overall_dice: Not reported as a single aggregate across organs; best per-organ Dice reported up to ~0.98 (lungs).

dice_per_organ.right lung: 0.98 (best reported; MI-net/MI-Res around 97.6% in tables; best summary reports 98%)
dice_per_organ.left lung: 0.98 (best reported; MI-Res 97.8% in Table II; best summary reports 98%)

hausdorff_distance.right lung: best ~3.82 (MI-Res or Late fusion Res depending on table; MI-Res reported 3.82±0.97)

results.gpu_memory: 12 GB (GPU model specified; memory implied by TITAN Xp 12GB)

comparison_results.MI-net (k=1): Dice: RL 97.6, LL 97.5, Liver 95.3, Spleen 91.4, RK 94.3, LK 92.7
comparison_results.LC-net (k=1): Dice: RL 97.3, LL 97.1, Liver 95.4, Spleen 90.9, RK 94.1, LK 91.0
comparison_results.MC-net (k=1): Dice: RL 97.5, LL 97.3, Liver 95.5, Spleen 91.2, RK 93.2, LK 91.0
comparison_results.Late fusion (k=1): Dice: RL 95.2, LL 95.3, Liver 93.2, Spleen 88.1, RK 93.2, LK 92.1
comparison_results.Single-energy baseline (low energy): Dice: RL 95.7, LL 95.8, Liver 91.6, Spleen 85.6, RK 90.0, LK 91.4
comparison_results.Single-energy baseline (high energy): Dice: RL 95.8, LL 95.9, Liver 91.8, Spleen 87.8, RK 89.8, LK 90.2
comparison_results.Opt-k MI-net: Dice: RL 97.6, LL 97.5, Liver 96.2, Spleen 91.4, RK 94.5, LK 93.7

implementation.training_time: Reported per architecture (examples): MC-net ~94 h 8 min; LC-net ~103 h 20 min; MI-net ~115 h 15 min; Late fusion ~106 h 32 min; residual variants up to ~186 h 32 min

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces and systematically evaluates four dedicated 3D CNN fusion strategies to exploit dual-energy CT information for automatic multi-organ segmentation, showing consistent gains over single-energy baselines.
contribution_summary.novelty: Rather than treating DECT as a pre-fused virtual monoenergetic image or segmenting each energy independently, the paper embeds DECT fusion inside the network at different stages (image/feature/output) with trainable fusion, and analyzes which fusion level is most effective (MI-net feature fusion performing best overall).
strengths: Clear architectural variants enabling controlled study of DECT fusion strategies.
Detailed acquisition protocol and consistent scanning settings for the main cohort.
Multiple metrics (Dice, AUC, Hausdorff) and per-organ reporting.
Attempts to mitigate small-cohort split bias using manifold-learning-based stratified sampling.
Explicit limitations and qualitative robustness demonstrations (protocol change, pathology, missing organ, truncation).
weaknesses: Small dataset (45 volumes) with no external validation; generalizability across sites/scanners/protocols remains uncertain.
Annotation process lacks detail (number of experts, consensus, inter-rater variability).
Patch-wise training introduces visible patch-edge artifacts; receptive field vs patch size mismatch discussed but not fully resolved.
No public code/model; framework not specified, limiting reproducibility.
Comparisons are mainly internal (fusion variants and single-energy baselines) rather than broad SOTA benchmarking on public datasets.
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: targets clinically relevant organs (lungs, liver, spleen, kidneys) and demonstrates robustness on several special cases, but absence of external validation and limited cohort size mean further multi-center testing is needed before clinical deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses 100 pancreatic cancer CT simulation patients; per fold 80 for training and 20 for testing in 5-fold cross-validation, exceeding the >50 training-subject guideline.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Institutional cohort from Emory University; acquisition years (2015–2019), clinical context (pancreatic radiotherapy CT simulation), and scanner models are described.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Fivefold cross-validation clearly described with 20 patients per fold held out for testing and 80 for training; repeated so all patients are tested once.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Contours were physician-delineated for treatment planning; two expert physicians reviewed all contours and reached a consensus set per patient (single consensus label set).

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset; only internal 5-fold cross-validation.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described (3D U-Net with attention gates on skip connections, deep supervision at decoder stages, 3 down/3 up blocks, softmax multi-class output, patch fusion).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides key training details: patch size/stride, augmentation scheme, optimizer (Adam), learning rate (2e-4), batch size (20), epochs (200), framework (TF 1.14), GPU (V100 32GB). Some details (e.g., weight decay, exact channel counts) are not fully specified but overall reproducible.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Compound loss (weighted cross-entropy + Dice) with deep supervision is defined mathematically; balancing weights (ct=0.8^(t-1), b=0.5) and motivation (limited data, convergence, feature learning) are provided.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Patch-based sliding window extraction (512×512×32, step 16) and extensive augmentation (rotations, flips, scaling, affine warp) are clearly described; postprocessing includes overlap-averaging fusion.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release reported.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses six metrics: DSC, sensitivity, specificity, HD95, MSD, RMSD.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Performs paired two-tailed t-tests with reported p-values comparing ablation variants (U-Net, DS, compound loss, attention gate).

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Includes ablation comparisons against internal baselines (U-Net variants). Mentions comparison to state-of-the-art in discussion, but does not provide a direct head-to-head evaluation on a shared public benchmark against external SOTA methods.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Reports per-organ DSC and boundary metrics (HD95/MSD/RMSD) for all eight organs.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses outliers and failure causes (contrast/no-contrast differences, artifacts/noise, missing manual contours at scan extremes, superior/inferior slice issues) and notes need for human inspection.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - explanation: Cohort is institutional and retrospective; inclusion criteria beyond 'pancreatic cancer patients treated at three clinical sites' are not fully specified (potential unreported selection filters).
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - explanation: Outcomes assessed consistently using the same consensus contour set and standardized metrics across 5-fold CV; however, no multi-institution external test to assess protocol variability.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - explanation: Reports multiple metrics and per-organ results plus ablation and p-values; but lacks external validation and does not report some implementation specifics (e.g., full layer/channel configuration), which may limit complete transparency.

detailed_extraction.title: CT-based multi-organ segmentation using a 3D self-attention U-net network for pancreatic radiotherapy

method.architecture_name: 3D Deep Attention U-Net (U-Net + attention gates + deep supervision)
key_innovations: 3D attention-gated U-Net tailored for upper-abdominal OAR segmentation in pancreatic radiotherapy CT simulation scans
Deep supervision applied at multiple decoder stages to improve convergence and feature learning for low-contrast organ boundaries
Deep-supervised compound loss combining weighted cross-entropy and Dice loss across decoder stages
Patch-based 3D inference with overlap-averaging fusion to reconstruct full-volume multi-organ probability maps
loss_functions: Deep-supervised weighted sum across decoder stages (ct=0.8^(t-1), t=1..3; balance b=0.5)

names: Emory University institutional pancreatic radiotherapy CT simulation dataset
dataset.ct_scanner: SOMATOM Definition AS; SOMATOM Definition Edge (Siemens); Brilliance Big Bore (Philips); LightSpeed RT (GE)
dataset.image_size: In-plane: 512×512; in-plane voxel size ~1 mm (0.90–1.56 mm); scan length 216–712.5 mm; training patches 512×512×32 with step 16

challenging_organs: spinal cord at superior/inferior extremes when manual contours are missing

results.gpu_memory: 29 GB GPU memory used per batch optimization (reported); GPU is 32 GB V100

comparison_results.U-Net (baseline) DSC: Reported in Table II per organ (e.g., large bowel 0.88, small bowel 0.85, duodenum 0.82, left kidney 0.94, right kidney 0.94, liver 0.95, spinal cord 0.84, stomach 0.90)
comparison_results.U-Net + deep supervision (Unet-DS) DSC: Reported in Table II per organ (e.g., large bowel 0.90, small bowel 0.86, duodenum 0.83, left kidney 0.93, right kidney 0.94, liver 0.96, spinal cord 0.86, stomach 0.91)
comparison_results.Unet-DS + compound loss (Unet-DS-CL) DSC: Reported in Table II per organ (e.g., large bowel 0.89, small bowel 0.88, duodenum 0.83, left kidney 0.94, right kidney 0.95, liver 0.96, spinal cord 0.86, stomach 0.91)
comparison_results.Unet-DS-CL + attention gate (proposed) D: Reported in Table II per organ (large bowel 0.91, small bowel 0.89, duodenum 0.86, left kidney 0.95, right kidney 0.95, liver 0.96, spinal cord 0.87, stomach 0.93)

implementation.training_time: Not reported (training stopped after 200 epochs).

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces and validates a 3D attention-gated, deeply supervised U-Net with a compound Dice+cross-entropy loss for fast, simultaneous segmentation of eight abdominal OARs on pancreatic radiotherapy CT simulation scans.
contribution_summary.novelty: Applies a 3D attention U-Net with deep supervision and a staged compound loss to the specific pancreatic radiotherapy CT simulation setting (multi-scanner, variable contrast), demonstrating improved performance over U-Net baselines via ablation and statistical testing.
strengths: Clinically relevant target (pancreatic RT OARs) with fast inference (<1 s) suitable for workflow acceleration
Reasonable dataset size for a single-institution RT study (100 patients) with clear 5-fold CV
Consensus contour review by two expert physicians
Comprehensive evaluation metrics including overlap and surface distances
Ablation study with statistical significance testing to support design choices
weaknesses: No external validation; generalizability to other institutions/protocols remains uncertain
No public code/model release
Patch-based approach with fusion may be sensitive to scan extent and missing/variable manual contouring at superior/inferior slices
Some implementation details (e.g., exact channel numbers, full normalization/regularization settings) are not fully specified
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High potential for pancreatic radiotherapy planning and adaptive workflows due to multi-organ, single-pass segmentation and sub-second inference; however, lack of external validation and reported outliers/failure modes imply it should be used with clinician review and would benefit from multi-institution testing before deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training/validation uses 66 CT exams (from 64 individuals), exceeding the >50-subject criterion.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Internal Mayo Clinic dataset described: RCC abdomen/pelvis CT exams; demographics and clinical context provided.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Train/validation combined count (66 exams) and test set (18 exams) are clearly stated, but the exact train vs validation partitioning procedure is not specified.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Annotation workflow described in detail: 10 analysts, physician review/correction (4 radiologists), tool (RIL-Contour), interpolation strategy, use of priors, and additional manual-only subset; STAPLE used for interobserver analysis.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset; only internal test set.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: 3D U-Net variant described with residual pre-activation blocks, dropout, instance norm, RReLU, stride-2 conv downsampling, nearest-neighbor upsampling + conv, skip concatenations, and input size 256×256×128.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Some key training details provided (Adam parameters, LR=1e-3, Xavier init, early stopping on validation Dice, augmentation types). However, batch size, number of epochs/steps, exact validation split, and some implementation specifics are missing.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Weighted Dice loss is described and motivated by class imbalance (weights inversely proportional to prevalence), but there is limited deeper justification/ablation versus alternatives.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Clear preprocessing: resampling to 256×256×128 (cubic), HU windowing (WW=1000, WL=100) and scaling to [0,1]; masks resampled with nearest neighbor; augmentation described.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release reported.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice plus Jaccard, TPF, FPF, and volume/volume error.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean and standard deviation across test cases and box/whisker plots; no confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, or formal statistical significance testing.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple published multi-organ methods (e.g., Zhou, Gibson, Roth, Hu, Cerrolaza) and some single-organ references (Table 3).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ Dice and other metrics reported (Table 1), with some bilateral structures averaged and individual values in supplement.

Q15_limitations.justification: Limitations and failure modes discussed: RCC pathology, missing organs, truncation, Siemens-only acquisition, downsampling effects on small organs, potential annotation bias from machine priors.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient cohort is clearly RCC-based and internal, but inclusion/exclusion criteria and consecutive sampling strategy are not fully detailed; organ absence/truncation introduces potential selection/coverage issues.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - Ground truth created with semi-automated priors and interpolation; outcomes assessed consistently, but priors may bias annotations and reduce apparent variability; evaluation performed on downsampled volumes.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low - Reports multiple metrics, per-organ results, and limitations; however, no external validation and some missing training details are acknowledged rather than selectively omitted.

detailed_extraction.title: Complete Abdomen and Pelvis Segmentation using U-Net Variant Architecture

method.architecture_name: 3D U-Net (variant with residual context modules)
key_innovations: Single end-to-end 3D model for dense whole abdomen/pelvis labeling with 33 structures including body composition tissues (SAT/VAT/muscle/bone) and a catch-all soft-tissue class
Residual pre-activation blocks in U-Net context modules with instance normalization and RReLU to improve training stability/regularization
Weighted multi-class Dice loss with inverse-prevalence weighting to address extreme class imbalance across organs
Reports human interobserver variability using STAPLE and compares model performance to human performance
loss_functions: Weighted Dice loss (per-class weights inversely proportional to class prevalence)
method.learning_rate: 1e-3 (initial)

names: Internal Mayo Clinic RCC abdomen/pelvis CT dataset (manually annotated 33 labels)
dataset.ct_scanner: Siemens scanners (manufacturer mentioned; specific models not provided)
dataset.slice_thickness: Typical 3–5 mm; some scans ≤1 mm (then downsampling occurred in cranial-caudal after resampling to 128 slices)
dataset.image_size: Resampled to 256×256×128 voxels (~1.5 mm × 1.5 mm × 3 mm approximate resolution after resampling)

segmented_organs: intervertebral discs (T12/S1 range; multiple disc labels)
other soft tissue/connective tissue & small vessels/ureters (lumped label)
background (model output; not reported in results)
challenging_organs: uterus/prostate when absent or scan truncated

results.gpu_memory: 32GB GPU used (Volta architecture); memory usage not quantified

comparison_results.Zhou et al. (multi-planar co-training): Reported per-organ Dice in Table 3; generally higher for several organs; notably adrenals much lower (0.36) than this work (0.69).
comparison_results.Gibson et al. (Dense V-Net): Reported per-organ Dice in Table 3; often higher for major organs; small intestine comparison uses duodenum proxy.
comparison_results.Roth et al. (hierarchical 3D FCN): Reported per-organ Dice in Table 3; higher for several organs including pancreas.
comparison_results.Human (semiautomated, STAPLE reference): Dice values in Table 2; e.g., kidneys 0.979, liver 0.975, spleen 0.969; renal arteries 0.549.

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces and evaluates an end-to-end 3D U-Net variant that performs dense whole abdomen/pelvis CT segmentation into 33 structures, with performance contextualized against human interobserver variability and prior multi-organ methods.
contribution_summary.novelty: At the time of writing, targets an unusually large set of abdominal/pelvic structures (33) in a single 3D model, including body composition tissues and a catch-all soft-tissue class, and reports human variability (STAPLE) to contextualize model accuracy.
strengths: Large label set (33) including tissues often omitted (SAT/VAT/muscle/bone/connective tissue)
Clear description of architecture and preprocessing; practical end-to-end 3D approach
Addresses class imbalance via inverse-prevalence weighted Dice loss
Rich evaluation: multiple metrics (Dice/Jaccard/TPF/FPF/volume error) and per-organ reporting
Includes human interobserver variability analysis (STAPLE) and discussion of organ absence/truncation
weaknesses: No external validation; generalizability limited (single institution, Siemens-only acquisitions, RCC cohort)
Downsampling to 256×256×128 likely harms small-structure accuracy (e.g., renal arteries, adrenals)
Train/validation split details, batch size, and training duration not fully specified; code not released
Ground truth creation uses machine-generated priors and interpolation, which may bias annotations and inflate consistency
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High potential relevance for radiotherapy planning and dose-to-organs-at-risk estimation due to broad coverage of abdominal tissues; however, deployment would require validation on multi-vendor, multi-site cohorts and higher-resolution handling for small critical structures (e.g., vessels, adrenals) and robust handling of absent organs/truncated scans.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training sizes are generally adequate for deep learning segmentation: G-MP 204, G-HN 65, C-ABD 58, C-MP 50 (borderline but meets >50 only for some models). Overall, multiple models exceed 50 training subjects and the study includes several datasets totaling >300 training CTs across sites.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset sources are described: MICCAI 2015 HN (41 cases), additional multi-institutional HN (24), multi-institutional MP (204), and single-institution custom datasets (MP 50, HN 47, ABD 58).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Independent test sets are clearly stated per model (e.g., 50/10/13 test cases), but validation split methodology is not explicitly detailed (no explicit val set size or cross-validation description).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Custom dataset ground truths were manually created using consensus guidelines and independently checked by two experienced clinicians per site; this process is described. (Less detail for public/general datasets, but overall annotation process is addressed.)

Q5_external_validation.justification: The 'general' models are trained on multi-institutional data and tested on independent cases chosen to mimic variability, but there is no explicit external validation on a fully independent named external dataset/site held out for final evaluation (beyond internal independent test sets).

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described as a modified ResUnet3D (3D U-Net with residual blocks and long/short skip connections) with schematic; organ-based strategies ASR and PSE are explained.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key training details are provided (optimizer Adam, weighted cross-entropy, LR=0.002 with linear decay after half epochs, weight decay 0.0005, momentum 0.9, 200–400 epochs, HU clipping/normalization, elastic deformation augmentation, GPU V100). However, some reproducibility details are missing (batch size, exact patch/volume sampling strategy, full network hyperparameters, validation protocol).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Weighted cross-entropy is specified, but the paper provides limited justification for this choice versus Dice/compound losses, especially for class imbalance and small organs.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is clearly described: HU range clipping per site and linear normalization to [-1,1]; augmentation via online random 3D elastic deformation; post-processing/organ-based handling via ASR and PSE is described.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release; models integrated into a research tool (ADMIRE, Elekta) and data shared only upon request.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses Dice similarity coefficient (DSC) and mean distance to agreement (MDA), plus time efficiency and qualitative visual inspection.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean ± standard deviation and boxplots; no confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, or formal statistical significance testing between models/datasets.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against recent multi-site/multi-organ works (Chen et al. WBNet; Isensee et al. nnU-Net; and other combined published results) with per-organ DSC table.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ DSC and MDA are reported (Table 2) and additional per-organ comparisons (Table 4); also discusses organ-specific failure modes.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations and failure modes: small/low-contrast organs (lens/optic nerves), artifacts (dental/metal), pancreas/bowels variability, scan-length issues, and need for manual edits; includes examples and analysis.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient selection criteria and inclusion/exclusion rules are not fully specified; test cases were 'purposely chosen' to mimic variability, which may introduce subjective selection even though they are independent from training.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Outcomes (DSC/MDA) are computed consistently against ground truth; however, qualitative review/edit time estimation may vary by reviewer and is not fully standardized or blinded.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low - Per-organ results and multiple metrics are reported across sites; limitations and poorer-performing organs are explicitly discussed, reducing risk of selective reporting.

detailed_extraction.title: General and custom deep learning autosegmentation models for organs in head and neck, abdomen, and male pelvis

method.architecture_name: Modified ResUnet3D (3D U-Net with residual blocks)
key_innovations: General vs custom training strategy comparison (multi-institutional diversified vs single-institution well-controlled datasets) across multiple anatomical sites
Adaptive Spatial Resolution (ASR): higher in-plane resolution subregion processing (1×1×2 mm3) for small/narrow organs to improve segmentation
Pseudo Scan Extension (PSE): synthetic extension of scan length by repeating end slices with elastic contour transformations to mitigate missing superior/inferior coverage
method.learning_rate: 0.002 initial; linear decay after half of training epochs

names: MICCAI 2015 Head and Neck Auto-Segmentation Challenge dataset (41 HN cases)
Multi-institutional general datasets for male pelvis (204) and head & neck (additional 24 beyond MICCAI)
Single-institution custom datasets: male pelvis (50), head & neck (47), abdomen (58)
dataset.total_subjects: Training totals across models: MP general 204; HN general 65 (41 MICCAI2015 + 24 multi-institution); MP custom 50; HN custom 47; ABD custom 58. Testing: G-MP 50; C-MP 10; G-HN 50; C-HN 10; C-ABD 13.
dataset.train_subjects: Per model: G-MP 204; C-MP 50; G-HN 65; C-HN 47; C-ABD 58
dataset.test_subjects: Per model: G-MP 50; C-MP 10; G-HN 50; C-HN 10; C-ABD 13
dataset.ct_scanner: Not reported (multi-institutional variability mentioned)
dataset.slice_thickness: Not reported
dataset.image_size: Example network tensor sizes shown (e.g., 320×320×32 input mentioned as typical); default resolution example 2×2×2 mm3; ASR uses 1×1×2 mm3 in subregions

segmented_organs: Male pelvis: Bladder, Prostate, Rectum, SeminalVes, PenileBulb, Sigmoid, Femure_L, Femure_R, Pelvis_L, Pelvis_R, Patient external

Head & neck: Brain, Brainstem, Bone_Mandible, Esophagus, Eye_L, Eye_R, InnerEar_L, InnerEar_R, Larynx, Lens_L, Lens_R, OpticNerve_L, OpticNerve_R, Parotid_L, Parotid_R, Glnd_Submand_L, Glnd_Submand_R, SpinalCord, Patient external
Abdomen: A_Aorta, Bowel_Large, Bowel_Small, Duodenum, Kidney_L, Kidney_R, Liver, Pancreas, SpinalCord, Spleen, Stomach, Patient external

results.overall_dice: Reported as high accuracy (DSC 0.8–0.98) for 74% of OARs; average DSC across compared OARs reported as 0.863 in Table 4 comparison context
results.inference_time: Approx. 30 s (MP), 120 s (HN), 70 s (ABD) per case for all OARs (CPU execution reported)
results.gpu_memory: Not reported

implementation.framework: Not explicitly stated (TensorFlow cited; models integrated into ADMIRE research tool)
implementation.training_time: Not reported

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Develops and clinically evaluates multi-site 3D deep-learning CT autosegmentation models (head & neck, abdomen, male pelvis) with organ-specific strategies (ASR, PSE) and compares general multi-institutional vs custom single-institution training.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines (i) multi-anatomical-site, multi-organ 3D autosegmentation in a unified study with (ii) explicit organ-targeted mechanisms for small organs (adaptive spatial resolution) and insufficient scan coverage (pseudo scan extension), plus (iii) a practical clinical time-efficiency evaluation including review/edit time.
strengths: Broad scope: 42 OARs across three major RT sites with per-organ reporting
Practical organ-based improvements (ASR markedly improves lens/optic nerve/inner ear performance; PSE improves bowel completeness)
Clinically oriented evaluation: TG-132 acceptability criteria, qualitative review, and time savings quantified
Comparison to strong baselines in literature (WBNet, nnU-Net) with competitive or better performance for many organs
weaknesses: No public code/model release; limited reproducibility details (batch size, validation protocol, full implementation specifics)
No explicit external validation on a fully independent held-out institution/dataset for final claims
Limited statistical testing (no CIs/p-values) and small test sets for custom models (10 cases for C-MP and C-HN; 13 for C-ABD)
Persistent lower performance for difficult organs (penile bulb, pancreas, bowels/duodenum; MDA often >2 mm for bowels)
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High practical relevance for radiation therapy planning and online adaptive workflows: models reduce contouring time substantially (reported 65–88% reduction) and achieve clinically acceptable accuracy for most OARs, but still require manual review/editing for low-contrast or highly variable organs (notably pancreas and bowels).

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training set size is below the >50-subject threshold: total dataset is 47 CT scans (2-fold CV; also 4-fold CV reported for proposed method).

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset source and acquisition context are described: 47 patients with normal organs, late arterial phase CT, obtained at the same hospital; voxel sizes and slice counts are reported.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Evaluation uses 2-fold cross-validation (and an additional 4-fold CV experiment), but exact fold composition/subject allocation details are not fully specified beyond the CV scheme.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual annotation/ground-truth creation process (who annotated, protocol, inter-rater checks) is not described in the paper text provided.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No external/independent dataset validation is reported; all results are on the same 47-case in-house dataset via cross-validation.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture and pipeline are clearly described: 3D U-Net backbone, two-phase training, transfer to 8 organ-specific expert 3D U-Nets, PA-guided inference region, and largest connected component post-processing.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Some training details are provided (TensorFlow, epochs=30, lr=1e-4, batch size=3, dropout at bottom layer, tile sizes, hardware), but key items are missing (optimizer type, loss function, weight initialization details beyond last layer randomization, intensity normalization, exact sampling strategy).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss function is not specified or justified in the provided paper text.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing/tiling strategy is described: 2D downsampling to 256x256; 3D tiles 132x132x116 with valid convolutions yielding 44x44x28 outputs; non-overlapping output voxels to reduce computation; PA used to focus inference region.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is indicated; only implementation framework (TensorFlow) is mentioned.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice plus recall and precision per organ.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Paired t-tests are reported between the proposed method and others; additionally a p-value is reported for pancreas improvement in 4-fold vs 2-fold CV (p=0.012).

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple baselines (S-CSL-UI, 2D U-Net, 3D U-Net, mixture without transfer) and includes a comparison table to Roth et al. hierarchical 3D FCN (reported metrics).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ Dice/recall/precision are reported for all 8 organs.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations: small dataset size; PA used only as non-zero mask due to discrete probabilities; suggests larger dataset and arithmetic use of PA probabilities in future.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient cohort is described only broadly (47 patients, normal organs, late arterial phase, single hospital) without explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria or consecutive sampling details.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - evaluation is consistent across methods with cross-validation and common post-processing, but ground-truth generation and blinding are not described; PA is built from training folds which is appropriate but details are limited.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - key methodological details (loss, optimizer, annotation protocol) are missing; however, per-organ outcomes and multiple metrics are reported and comparisons are provided.

detailed_extraction.title: 3D U-JAPA-Net: Mixture of Convolutional Networks for Abdominal Multi-organ CT Segmentation

method.architecture_name: 3D U-JAPA-Net (3D U-Net + probabilistic atlas + mixture of experts with transfer learning)
key_innovations: Judgement Assisted by Probabilistic Atlas (PA) to inject organ location priors into a 3D U-Net-based segmentation pipeline
Two-phase training with transfer learning: train a multi-class 3D U-Net then transfer weights to organ-specific expert 3D U-Nets (binary segmentation per organ)
Mixture-of-experts inference restricted to PA-nonzero regions to reduce misclassification from purely local context
Largest connected component post-processing to remove debris

names: In-house abdominal CT dataset (late arterial phase, normal organs)
dataset.train_subjects: Not explicitly stated (2-fold CV implies ~23/24 per fold; 4-fold CV implies ~35 training per fold, but exact counts per fold not provided)
dataset.val_subjects: Not reported (no explicit validation set; cross-validation used)
dataset.test_subjects: Not explicitly stated (2-fold CV implies ~23/24 per fold; exact counts not provided)
dataset.ct_scanner: Not reported
dataset.slice_thickness: Varies by subset: 1.25 mm for 9 cases; 0.625 mm for most others (voxel sizes reported as 0.625x0.625x1.25, 0.781x0.781x0.625, and 0.674x0.674x0.625 mm^3)
dataset.image_size: In-plane 512x512; slices: 159 for 9 cases; 305-409 for 37 cases; 345 for 1 case

results.overall_dice: Not reported as a single aggregate; best per-organ Dice up to 0.984 (left kidney)
results.inference_time: Not reported
results.gpu_memory: 11 GB (GPU model indicates 11 GB; explicit memory usage not reported)

implementation.training_time: 2D U-Net: 2.5 h; 3D U-Net (all organs): 16 h; expert 3D U-Nets: 40-130 min each except liver 9.5 h

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces 3D U-JAPA-Net, a PA-assisted, transfer-learned mixture-of-expert 3D U-Nets that improves abdominal multi-organ CT segmentation over standard 2D/3D U-Net baselines on an in-house dataset.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines a probabilistic atlas for organ-location guidance with a two-stage transfer learning scheme that initializes organ-specific expert 3D U-Nets from a multi-class 3D U-Net, using PA to focus inference regions and reduce local-context misclassification.
strengths: Clear, practical two-stage training recipe (multi-class pretraining then organ-specific experts) demonstrating transfer learning benefit vs training experts from scratch
Uses explicit location prior (PA) to address a known weakness of patch/tile-based 3D U-Nets (limited global context)
Reports per-organ Dice/precision/recall and includes statistical testing (paired t-tests; p-value for pancreas improvement with more training data)
Shows improvements particularly on difficult organs (pancreas, gallbladder, IVC)
weaknesses: Small single-center dataset (47 cases) with no external validation; limited generalizability
Ground-truth annotation protocol not described
Key training details missing for reproducibility (loss function, optimizer, intensity normalization, full sampling strategy)
No data augmentation (may limit robustness) and no inference-time reporting
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: targets clinically relevant abdominal organ segmentation and improves difficult structures, but evidence is limited by small in-house data and lack of external validation/annotation detail; further multi-center testing is needed before clinical deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training set includes 309 patients (618 kidneys), well above the >50-subject threshold; total cohort 441 patients plus an additional 35-patient small-tumor test group.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Single-institution retrospective cohort from Peking University First Hospital; acquisition period (July 2017–April 2019) and inclusion criteria are described; scanners and protocol noted.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Random split into training/validation/test with explicit counts and ratio (7:1.5:1.5): 309/66/66 patients; additional independent small-tumor group (35 patients) used for extra testing.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual voxel-wise annotations for kidney, renal tumor, and simple cyst on corticomedullary phase CTU by two experienced radiologists using a specified software platform; small-tumor group annotated for tumor only.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No external multi-institution or publicly sourced dataset validation; the additional small-tumor group is from the same institution (later time period), not an independent external dataset.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described at a high level (3D U-Net; cascaded two-stage kidney model; single 3D U-Net multi-label for mass), but key architectural/training parameters are largely deferred to Supplementary/Table S2 and not fully detailed in the main text.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Core training hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, batch size, epochs, augmentation, hardware, framework) are not reported in the provided main text; details are referenced to supplementary material but not present here.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss function is not stated in the provided text and no justification is given (referenced indirectly via supplementary material).

key_innovations: Two-stage cascaded 3D U-Net kidney pipeline: low-resolution global localization followed by high-resolution ROI fine segmentation
Renal mass segmentation via a single 3D U-Net trained with multi-label learning (kidney/tumor/cyst) using kidney ROI from the kidney model
Simple detection strategy derived from segmentation: maximum 3D VOI volume thresholding per kidney with ROC/AUC evaluation (Youden index threshold selection)

Renal cyst (small cysts; cyst attenuation similar to renal medulla in corticomedullary phase)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a fully automated CTU corticomedullary-phase pipeline using cascaded 3D U-Nets for kidney localization/segmentation and a multi-label 3D U-Net for renal tumor/cyst segmentation, with lesion detection derived from segmentation via maximum-VOI thresholding.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines (i) a practical cascaded kidney ROI strategy tailored to CTU and (ii) a unified multi-label renal mass segmentation (tumor + cyst) with a simple, clinically interpretable detection rule (max 3D VOI volume) evaluated by ROC/AUC, on a relatively large single-center CTU cohort.
strengths: Large training cohort for this specific CTU task (309 patients for training; 441 total) and additional small-tumor testing set
Strong kidney segmentation (test DSC ~0.974) and solid tumor segmentation (test DSC ~0.844) with detailed subgroup analyses (location/appearance effects)
Reports both segmentation and detection performance with AUC and 95% CIs; includes false-positive/false-negative characterization and example failure cases
weaknesses: No external multi-center validation; single-institution protocol and single CTU phase limit generalizability
Key training/implementation details (loss, optimizer, LR schedule, augmentation, compute) not provided in the available text; no public code

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Primary training set contains 281 CT volumes (>50) with an additional 50-volume validation set; multiple additional datasets are also used (e.g., 240-case, 82-case TCIA).

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Data sources are clearly described: in-house Nagoya University Hospital portal venous phase CTs (331 total), an external hospital test set (150), TCIA Pancreas-CT (82), Visceral Challenge (20), and an unseen torso test set (10).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Explicit random split of 281/50 for train/validation on the 331-case dataset; separate external test set of 150 from a different hospital; 4-fold CV specified for TCIA; 228/12 split specified for the 240-case comparison dataset.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual labels are stated for the 331-case dataset (7 structures) but the detailed annotation protocol (number of annotators, QA, inter-rater variability) is not fully described. For the 150-case external test set, pancreas/liver/spleen were semi-automatically delineated by three trained researchers and confirmed by a clinician.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Yes—models trained on the 331-case in-house dataset are evaluated on a completely unseen external hospital dataset of 150 CT scans; additional cross-dataset transfer learning experiments are also reported.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: 3D U-Net/3D FCN architecture is described in detail (4 resolution levels, 3x3x3 convs, 2x2x2 pooling/up-conv, skip connections, 1x1x1 output conv, batch norm, parameter count, fixed input/output tile sizes). Cascade candidate regions C1/C2 are defined.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Many training details are provided (SGD, iterations 200k/115k, augmentation ranges, input size, downsampling factor, dilation radius r=3, tiling strategy, GPU model, approximate training/inference time). However, key hyperparameters such as learning rate, momentum, weight decay, batch size, and LR schedule are not fully specified in the provided text.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Weighted voxel-wise softmax cross-entropy is mathematically defined and motivated by severe class imbalance; class weights are specified based on voxel counts within candidate regions.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing and inference procedures are clearly described: downsample by factor 2, body mask via thresholding + morphological operations, candidate region generation via dilation of stage-1 predictions, tiling/sliding-window with optional overlap and probability averaging.

Q10_code_availability.justification: Code and trained models are publicly released (GitHub link provided in the paper).

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Dice is the primary metric; additional evaluation includes sensitivity/false-positive-rate analysis for candidate region dilation and runtime reporting. However, standard boundary metrics (e.g., Hausdorff/ASSD) are not reported.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Statistical significance testing is reported for the direct comparison to 2D FCN voting (Wilcoxon signed-rank test with p-values).

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple prior methods (atlas/registration/ML baselines, 2D FCN pancreas methods, and a direct re-implementation comparison to a 2D FCN voting approach on the same dataset).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ Dice results are reported for validation (7 labels), external test (3 organs), 18-class dataset comparison, and transfer learning experiments.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations including GPU memory/tiling constraints, varying benefit across organ sizes, transfer learning failure cases (pancreas), and challenges in cross-paper comparisons; also notes future directions.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient cohorts are described (clinical CTs for gastric surgery planning; external gastric cancer cohort) and splits are specified, but inclusion/exclusion criteria and consecutive vs convenience sampling are not detailed.

However, some hyperparameters and some negative results (e.g., full per-organ external test beyond 3 organs) are inherently limited by available labels, and boundary metrics are not reported.

detailed_extraction.title: An application of cascaded 3D fully convolutional networks for medical image segmentation

method.architecture_name: Cascaded 3D U-Net (3D FCN)
key_innovations: Two-stage coarse-to-fine cascade using candidate regions to reduce class imbalance and focus stage-2 on boundaries/small structures
Candidate region C1 from simple body mask (~40% voxels) and C2 from dilated stage-1 predictions (~10% voxels)
Demonstration that a single multi-class 3D FCN can segment heterogeneous structures (large organs + thin vessels) without organ-specific models
Optional overlapping-tile inference with probability averaging to improve accuracy

Random translations (-20 to 20 voxels in each direction)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces and validates a two-stage cascaded 3D U-Net (3D FCN) coarse-to-fine framework that uses candidate regions to substantially improve multi-organ (including vessels) CT segmentation, with strong external generalization.
contribution_summary.novelty: Rather than organ-specific models or 2D cascades with bounding boxes, the paper proposes a simple candidate-region cascade (body mask then dilated stage-1 predictions) for multi-class 3D FCN training/inference, reducing voxel search space (~40% then ~10%) and improving small/thin-structure accuracy without post-processing.
strengths: Large in-house training dataset (331) and evaluation on a truly external unseen hospital test set (150), supporting generalization claims
Clear architectural and pipeline description (candidate regions, dilation radius selection, tiling/overlap strategy)
Demonstrated gains particularly for small/thin structures (arteries, pancreas) and improved mean performance in an 18-class direct comparison
Public code and models released, improving transparency and reuse
Includes statistical testing (Wilcoxon) for the direct 2D vs 3D comparison
weaknesses: Incomplete reporting of some training hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, momentum, batch size), limiting exact reproducibility
Primary evaluation relies heavily on Dice; limited boundary/clinical metrics (e.g., Hausdorff/ASSD) and limited error analysis beyond qualitative examples
External test labels cover only 3 organs, so external generalization for vessels/other organs is not directly verified
Transfer learning results show failure/outliers for pancreas on torso dataset, indicating sensitivity to contrast protocol/domain shift
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High potential: achieves strong accuracy on clinically acquired contrast-enhanced CT and runs in minutes per case on a single GPU, with external validation across hospitals. However, robustness to protocol variation (especially for pancreas) and lack of boundary metrics/clinical task validation may limit immediate deployment without further domain adaptation and prospective testing.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against their classic 3D U-Net baseline and cites/compares to prior ROI-based methods; also reports KiTS19 challenge ranking and compares to top leaderboard methods.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses failure cases (missed small tumors, including a case with tumor Dice=0), receptive field limitations, and generalization/domain shift concerns across vendors/settings.

detailed_extraction.title: MSS U-Net: 3D segmentation of kidneys and tumors from CT images with a multi-scale supervised U-Net

method.architecture_name: MSS U-Net (multi-scale supervised 3D U-Net; nnU-Net-inspired)
key_innovations: Deep supervision / multi-scale supervision applied to decoder outputs of a 3D U-Net to improve gradient flow and low-resolution prediction quality
Exponential logarithmic loss applied to Soft Dice (with kidney/tumor weighting) combined with weighted cross-entropy to address class imbalance and difficult samples
Connected-component-based postprocessing using anatomical priors (1–2 kidneys; tumors attached to kidneys) to remove disconnected false positives
loss_functions: Weighted Cross-Entropy (class weights: background 0.28, kidney 0.28, tumor 0.44)
Exponential logarithmic Soft Dice (exponent 0.3) with kidney/tumor weights 0.4/0.6
Multi-scale weighted sum of per-layer losses (decoder layer weights: 0.4, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1, 0.1)
method.learning_rate: Initial 3e-4; reduce by factor 0.2 if training loss not improved for 30 epochs; stop if no improvement for 50 epochs
method.epochs: Not explicitly fixed; training uses 250 iterations per epoch and stops by plateau criterion (training time ~5 days)

contribution_summary.novelty: Rather than adding complex architectural modules, the paper improves a strong 3D U-Net baseline via (i) multi-scale/deep supervision across decoder outputs and (ii) exponential logarithmic Soft Dice (plus weighted CE) to better handle class imbalance and difficult tumor voxels, complemented by anatomy-informed connected-component postprocessing.
strengths: End-to-end segmentation on raw CT volumes (not ROI-only), improving clinical realism
Clear description of architecture, loss design, preprocessing, augmentation, and postprocessing

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Some training details provided (epochs for stage 1/2, loss, dropout, voxel spacing, bounding-box sizes, hardware, inference/training time). Key hyperparameters are missing (optimizer, learning rate schedule, batch size, weight decay, augmentation details).

detailed_extraction.title: Dual-energy CT for automatic organs-at-risk segmentation in brain-tumor patients using a multi-atlas and deep-learning approach

Two-step 3D U-Net pipeline: stage 1 predicts per-organ 3D bounding boxes; stage 2 trains a dedicated 3D U-Net per organ within fixed-size boxes.
Practical handling of very small structures (eye lenses) via heuristic postprocessing (hole detection) rather than direct network training at downsampled resolution.
Combined quantitative (DSC, HD95, ΔCoM) and qualitative clinical acceptability scoring by multiple experts.

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Evaluates how DECT-derived pseudo-monoenergetic CT energy affects automatic OAR segmentation in brain-tumor radiotherapy, comparing an in-house multi-atlas pipeline to a two-step 3D U-Net approach with both quantitative metrics and expert clinical scoring.
contribution_summary.novelty: Systematic comparison of segmentation performance across multiple DECT pseudo-monoenergetic energies (40–170 keV) and demonstration that optimal energy may differ by method (MA benefits from low-energy PMI-40, while DL performs slightly better on PMI-70).
strengths: Clear description of DECT acquisition and PMI generation across multiple energies.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports P-values for comparisons (U-Net vs atlas; U-Net vs inter-observer) across metrics and organs.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against an atlas-based commercial method and inter-observer manual segmentation; does not benchmark against contemporary deep learning SOTA on public datasets.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses weaker performance for stomach/duodenum, limited organ coverage (upper abdomen only), reliance on manual contours as gold standard, potential lack of outlier cases, and limited efficiency endpoints.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient selection is described (HCC radiotherapy patients between July 2017 and March 2018 at one center), but inclusion/exclusion criteria and consecutive sampling details are not fully specified; single-disease cohort may limit representativeness.
