detailed_extraction.title: Abdominal multi-organ auto-segmentation using 3D-patch-based deep convolutional neural network

method.architecture_name: 3D patch-based U-Net
key_innovations: 3D patch-based multi-label U-Net for abdominal organ-at-risk segmentation in radiotherapy CT simulation
Graph-cut post-processing to remove false positives and refine boundaries using intensity discontinuity/gradient-based boundary term
Clinical workflow evaluation via time-to-contour reduction (manual contouring vs manual refinement after auto-segmentation)
augmentation: Not reported (random patch sampling is used, but explicit augmentation such as flips/rotations is not described)

names: Asan Medical Center internal radiotherapy CT simulation dataset (hepatocellular carcinoma; 4D CT end-expiratory phase)

dataset.image_size: Not reported (processed as 64×64×64 patches; resampled to 2 mm isotropic resolution)

results.gpu_memory: 24 GB (RTX Titan)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: A clinically oriented abdominal multi-organ CT auto-segmentation pipeline using a 3D patch-based U-Net with graph-cut refinement, demonstrating improved accuracy over atlas-based segmentation and substantial contouring time reduction in radiotherapy planning.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines a 3D patch-based multi-label U-Net with explicit graph-cut post-processing for boundary refinement/false-positive removal and evaluates both accuracy (vs atlas and inter-observer) and real-world time savings in a radiotherapy CT simulation setting.
strengths: Reasonable dataset size for a single-center radiotherapy study (n=120) with a clear train/val/test split
Per-organ reporting with multiple geometric metrics (DSC, HD, MSD) and statistical testing
Clinically relevant comparator (commercial atlas-based method) and inter-observer variability assessment
Includes workflow efficiency evaluation (time-to-contour reduction)
weaknesses: No external validation; single-center, single-disease cohort (HCC) limits generalizability
Architecture details and data augmentation are not fully specified; inference time not reported
Loss choice (cross-entropy) not justified and may be suboptimal for class imbalance/small organs (e.g., duodenum)
Performance remains notably below inter-observer for stomach and duodenum
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High practical relevance for radiotherapy planning: the method reduced mean contouring time from 22.6 to 7.1 minutes for five organs and achieved near inter-observer accuracy for liver and kidneys; however, stomach/duodenum still require substantial manual refinement and broader validation is needed before general deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Large internal dataset (n=1006 CT scans/patients) used for model development and internal validation; exceeds >50 training-subject threshold.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Internal dataset source (Gil Medical Center, 2016–2019) and external dataset source (TCIA/NIH Pancreas-CT, n=82) are clearly described, including scanner and acquisition parameters for internal data.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: States 4-fold cross-validation for internal data; for TCIA mentions a 10:5:5 split for 4-fold CV, but exact per-fold counts and whether splits are patient-level (assumed) are not fully detailed.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Gold standard created by manual delineation in 2D axial plane using ImageJ; described as manual segmentation prior to reconstruction.

Q5_external_validation.justification: External validation performed on independent public dataset (TCIA/NIH Pancreas-CT, n=82) with reported metrics.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: 3D U-Net family described with skip connections, batch norm, 4 resolution steps, 3×3×3 convs, ReLU, dilation rate 1, and simple upsampling; four variants (basic/dense/residual/residual-dense) and parameter counts reported; figure provided.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides optimizer (Adam), learning rate (0.001), batch size (2), epochs (500), frameworks and GPU; however, key details like weight initialization, learning-rate schedule, regularization, exact data split seeds, and augmentation are not specified.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Uses Dice loss (appropriate for class imbalance) but provides little explicit justification/ablation for the loss choice.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is detailed: resampling to (3mm,1mm,1mm), reconstruction, ROI cropping based on reconstructed mask with margin and z:y:x ratio, resizing to (64,128,256,1), windowing (WL=60, WW=400), CLAHE, restoration to original spacing for evaluation, thresholding at 0.5.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release reported; internal dataset not publicly available (available upon reasonable request).

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice, precision, recall; additionally evaluates volumetry with regression (R2, correlation R) and ICC, plus Bland–Altman analysis.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Performs paired t-tests comparing residual-dense U-Net to other variants (p<0.05) and reports p-values for correlation/regression; uses Bland–Altman and ICC.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares four 3D U-Net variants (basic/dense/residual/residual-dense) and discusses prior reported DSCs from literature, but does not implement/benchmark against external state-of-the-art pancreas segmentation methods (e.g., cascaded/coarse-to-fine) on the same splits.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Single-organ study (pancreas only), so per-organ reporting is inherently satisfied but limited; also reports performance stratified by pancreas volume ranges.

Q15_limitations.justification: Explicitly discusses limitations: semi-automated ROI cropping based on manual delineation due to memory limits, reduced external performance due to slice thickness differences, blurry boundaries, and limited population diversity.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - inclusion criteria are defined (no resection, no tumor) and cohort described, but it is a single-center retrospective health-checkup population (healthy Koreans), which may limit representativeness and introduces potential spectrum bias.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - outcomes are assessed consistently against manual delineations, but the pipeline uses manual mask-based ROI cropping (information leakage/assisted setting) which can inflate apparent segmentation performance versus a fully automated workflow.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - key segmentation and volumetry outcomes are reported for internal and external validation with multiple metrics; however, limited reporting of failure-case examples and no full distributional reporting (e.g., CIs) or benchmarking against external SOTA may omit relevant comparative outcomes.

Systematic comparison of four 3D U-Net block designs (basic vs dense vs residual vs residual-dense) under matched depth/width settings
Pancreas volumetry evaluation from segmentation outputs using regression, ICC, and Bland–Altman analysis

contribution_summary.novelty: Primary novelty is scale and validation: a large internal dataset for pancreas segmentation (rare in prior work) plus external validation, and a controlled comparison of U-Net block variants (dense/residual/residual-dense) with volumetry reliability analysis (ICC/Bland–Altman).
strengths: Very large internal dataset for training/validation relative to typical pancreas CT segmentation studies
External validation on a public independent dataset (TCIA/NIH Pancreas-CT)
Multiple evaluation perspectives: segmentation (DSC/precision/recall) and clinical proxy task (pancreas volume estimation with ICC and Bland–Altman)
Statistical testing (paired t-tests) for architecture comparisons
Clear preprocessing description (resampling, windowing, CLAHE) and hardware/software reporting
weaknesses: Semi-automated pipeline: ROI cropping uses manual delineation-derived masks, limiting real-world automation and potentially inflating performance
No public code/model release; internal dataset not publicly available
Limited benchmarking against external state-of-the-art pancreas segmentation methods on the same data/splits
Generalizability concerns: internal cohort is healthy, single-center, and demographically narrow (healthy Koreans); performance drops notably on external data
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: the volumetry analysis suggests potential utility for quantitative follow-up, but the current workflow depends on manual ROI cropping and shows reduced performance on external data; further work is needed for fully automated deployment and broader multi-site validation.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Single-institution dataset clearly described: 136 prostate cancer patients from UT Southwestern Medical Center; scanner model and acquisition parameters provided.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Clear 80/20 train-test split; training further uses 4-fold leave-one-out style cross-validation with one fold as validation and model averaging across 4 models for test prediction.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States organs were contoured by experienced radiation oncologists, but does not detail number of annotators, contouring protocol, QA, or inter-observer variability measurement.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset; all results are from the same UTSW cohort.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Both stages described (5-channel 2D U-Net localization; 3D U-Net with ResNeXt blocks in encoder) with kernel sizes, pooling/upsampling strides, activations, and appendices listing layer/feature details.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides optimizer (Adam), learning rate (1e-2 with decay 0.1 on plateau), regularization (dropout, batch norm, early stopping), epochs per organ (~170–400), and framework/GPU; however key details like batch size, exact decay schedule/criteria, weight initialization, and augmentation are not specified.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Defines and motivates organ-weighted Dice for localization (class imbalance) and boundary-weighted Dice for segmentation (fuzzy boundaries), and includes comparison to weighted BCE.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple U-Net variants trained on the same dataset and provides a literature comparison table with prior published methods/metrics (noting direct comparison limitations).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Reports per-organ Dice (and ASD/HD for prostate/bladder/rectum) rather than only an overall average.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations including imperfect ground truth due to inter-observer variability, rectum variability/balloon inserts, and need for larger datasets; proposes future work.

detailed_extraction.title: Fully automated organ segmentation in male pelvic CT images

ResNeXt (aggregated residual transformations) integrated into the encoder of a 3D U-Net for improved accuracy/efficiency
Custom organ-weighted Dice loss for multi-organ localization to address organ size imbalance
Boundary-weighted Dice loss for segmentation to emphasize fuzzy/uncertain boundaries in CT

dataset.image_size: 512x512; voxel size 1.17mm x 1.17mm x 2mm

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a fully automated two-stage deep learning pipeline (2D localization + 3D ResNeXt-augmented U-Net) for multi-organ male pelvic CT segmentation for radiotherapy planning.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines automatic organ localization (removing manual slice selection) with a 3D U-Net whose encoder uses ResNeXt aggregated residual blocks, and employs boundary-weighted Dice to improve delineation under low-contrast CT boundaries.
strengths: End-to-end automated workflow including localization, enabling practical deployment without manual pre-selection of slices/ROIs
Solid internal evaluation with per-organ Dice and surface metrics (ASD, HD) and mean±SD reporting
Includes statistical testing demonstrating benefit of weighted Dice over weighted BCE for several organs
Ablation/architecture comparison across multiple U-Net variants on the same dataset

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training dataset D1 contains 90 CT scans (public), exceeding the >50-subject threshold; interactive validation uses an additional 10 clinical scans (D2).

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Sources are clearly described: D1 from a public reference standard dataset (Gibson et al.) drawn from TCIA Pancreas-CT and BTCV Abdomen; D2 from Radboud UMC 2015 late venous phase cohort (1905 scans) with selection/exclusion criteria.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Automatic baseline comparison uses explicit 5-fold cross-validation on D1 with random division into 5 folds; interactive validation protocol on D2 is described (10 cases split into two subsets of 5, alternated per reader/method).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: D1 includes 'reference segmentation' from a published reference standard dataset, but the annotation procedure is not detailed in this paper. For D2, a consensus reference is created by two readers on full-resolution images, but detailed annotation guidelines and adjudication steps are limited.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Interactive evaluation is performed on an independent clinical dataset (D2) from a different institution/source than the public training data (D1), serving as external validation (though small n=10 and not publicly available).

Q6_architecture_description.justification: 3D U-Net-based iUnet is described with a diagram (feature map sizes) and explanation of which layers are retrained during interaction (last layer + a deep/latent layer).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key training details are provided: Keras + TensorFlow 1.12, Adam optimizer, learning rate 1e-4, batch size 8, 2000 iterations (~12h), augmentation ranges (translations, rotations, Gaussian noise), and preprocessing steps.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Baseline uses differentiable Dice loss (Milletari/V-Net style) with formula; interactive loss includes voxel-weighted Dice and a volume-difference regularization term with lambda, but the choice is only partially justified (intuitive rationale; lambda value not clearly specified in the provided text).

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is described in detail: Gaussian smoothing (sigma=0.75), HU windowing (-160 to 240) scaled to [-1,1], clipping, cropping using a bounding box derived from reference segmentation with 5% margin, and resampling to 64x64x24.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is provided; only implementation stack and a custom GUI description are mentioned.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Dice is the primary segmentation metric; time-to-segmentation is also reported for manual vs interactive. No boundary metrics (e.g., Hausdorff/ASSD) are reported.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Wilcoxon signed-rank tests are reported for key comparisons (e.g., manual vs interactive DSC p=0.017; time reduction p<0.001; initial vs refined p<0.001). A 95% CI is reported for inter-observer DSC.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against Cicek et al. 3D U-Net and Wang et al. iFCN (dilated FCN interactive framework) using 5-fold CV on D1.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Single-organ study (pancreas); results are reported specifically for pancreas rather than only an aggregate multi-organ average.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations including low contrast/uncinate process difficulty, low-resolution/partial volume artifacts, sensitivity to number of retrained layers (risk of divergence/under-segmentation), need for larger training sets, and reliance on ground-truth-derived bounding boxes.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - D2 selection is described as random from a large cohort with exclusion of pancreas pathology, but only 10 cases are used and the selection process details (e.g., randomization procedure) are limited; D1 is public but heterogeneous and derived from two sources.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - Manual segmentations use ITK-SNAP with full tools while interactive uses a custom tool and includes an initial automatic step; readers received 2 practice cases and were instructed to focus on global features, which may affect consistency. Consensus reference is created by two readers, but blinding and adjudication details are limited.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low - Main outcomes (DSC, time) and statistical tests are reported; however, lack of additional segmentation metrics and absence of inference-time reporting slightly limits completeness.

detailed_extraction.title: Interactive 3D U-net for the segmentation of the pancreas in computed tomography scans

method.architecture_name: Interactive 3D U-Net (iUnet)
key_innovations: Interactive extension of a 3D U-Net via image-specific fine-tuning using sparse user scribbles
Freezing most layers and retraining only selected layers (final layer plus one deep/latent layer) to enable fast adaptation and reduce overfitting risk
Scribble-driven voxel-weighted Dice loss with an additional volume-difference regularization term to stabilize interactive updates
Custom interactive segmentation workflow/GUI integrating iterative user corrections and rapid retraining

Interactive: voxel-weighted Dice loss + volume difference penalty (lambda term)

augmentation: Random translations (-3 to +3 voxels, trilinear interpolation)
Additive Gaussian noise (sigma uniformly sampled 0 to 3 HU)
Applied with '1000 fold augmentation' described as random augmentation each epoch

D2: Radboud UMC clinical late venous phase abdominal CT cohort (2015) subset

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Proposes an interactive 3D U-Net (iUnet) that performs rapid image-specific fine-tuning from sparse user scribbles to achieve near-expert pancreas CT segmentation in substantially less time than full manual delineation.
contribution_summary.novelty: Adapts interactive fine-tuning (previously demonstrated with an FCN framework) to a 3D U-Net and introduces a practical layer-freezing strategy (retrain last + deep layer) with a scribble-weighted Dice plus volume-regularization loss to make interactive updates effective for low-contrast pancreas CT without organ-specific post-processing.
strengths: Demonstrates improved automatic baseline over iFCN and standard 3D U-Net on public data via 5-fold CV
Includes an independent clinical reader study (3 observers) evaluating both segmentation quality and time
Uses statistical testing (Wilcoxon) and reports inter-observer agreement with confidence interval
Clear description of preprocessing, augmentation, and interactive training mechanism
weaknesses: Heavy preprocessing includes cropping using a bounding box derived from the reference segmentation (not available in real deployment), risking optimistic performance
Validation dataset for interactive evaluation is small (n=10) and not publicly available
Limited evaluation metrics (primarily Dice and time; no boundary/surface metrics)
No public code/model release, limiting reproducibility and adoption
Resampling to low fixed resolution (64x64x24) may limit anatomical detail and generalizability
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Clinically relevant for reducing radiologist time in pancreas delineation (reported ~48% median time reduction) while maintaining near-expert Dice; however, real-world deployment would require replacing ground-truth-derived cropping with user-provided or automatic localization, validating on larger multi-center cohorts (including pancreas pathology), and reporting additional safety/failure analyses and boundary accuracy metrics.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses SegTHOR 2019 with 32 training subjects (plus 8 validation, 20 test). Training set is <50 subjects, though a public benchmark dataset is used.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset is clearly identified as SegTHOR 2019 (public Grand Challenge dataset) with basic acquisition characteristics (in-plane size, resolution range, slice thickness range).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Ground truth is referred to as 'manually provided segmentation map/masks' from the dataset, but the paper does not describe who annotated, how many annotators, or quality control.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset beyond SegTHOR 2019.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described with encoder-decoder DenseNet-style blocks, skip connections, downsampling/upsampling operations, and a detailed 3D-ASPP module (branches, dilation rates, pooling, concatenation, 1x1x1 conv).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides framework (PyTorch), optimizer (Adam), learning rate (0.0008), batch size (2), epochs (10000), GPU model; however, key details are missing/unclear (patch/volume sampling strategy details, exact layer/channel configuration, dropout rate, weight decay, LR schedule, normalization of intensities).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Uses Dice loss and provides formula, but offers limited justification beyond common usage for overlap/imbalance; no ablation or rationale vs alternatives.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: States downsampling to 256x256 and selecting slices with lesion/masks plus 5 empty slices at start/end; does not fully specify intensity preprocessing (HU windowing/normalization), resampling in z, cropping strategy, or augmentation pipeline.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release reported.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice and multiple additional metrics (Jaccard, sensitivity, specificity, VOE, RVD, ASSD, Hausdorff/MSD).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance analysis reported.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against reimplemented 3D V-Net and 3D Attention U-Net and references/compares with other SegTHOR 2019 state-of-the-art methods (Table 1).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: The task is multi-organ (heart, aorta, trachea, esophagus) and the paper indicates organ-wise evaluation in the comparison table; however, the provided text excerpt does not explicitly list per-organ Dice values in-line, and reporting appears to emphasize an overall Dice (97.89%).

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses general limitations (overfitting risk with small data, computational/memory constraints, low contrast/ambiguous boundaries, small organs harder), but provides limited concrete failure-case analysis or qualitative error breakdown.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Uses a public benchmark with a fixed train/test partition, but inclusion/exclusion criteria and patient selection details are not described beyond dataset summary; no demographic/clinical distribution reporting.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Outcomes are assessed using standard segmentation metrics on the same dataset; however, preprocessing choices (slice selection around masks) may advantage evaluation if not applied identically across methods, and details are insufficient to fully verify consistency.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Many metrics are defined, but the paper highlights a single best overall Dice (97.89%) and does not clearly provide complete per-organ metric tables in the excerpt; no statistical uncertainty is reported.

detailed_extraction.title: Automatic Segmentation Using a Hybrid Dense Network Integrated With an 3D-Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling Module for Computed Tomography (CT) Imaging

dataset.image_size: Coarse-stage patches: 64×128×176; fine-stage max input: 48×144×192; in-plane capped at 128×176 after resampling

results.inference_time: <25 s (average time consumption; stage breakdown not provided)
results.gpu_memory: 1.91 GB reported for proposed method; 5.77 GB for baseline 3D U-Net

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a two-stage coarse-to-fine 3D CNN framework (Tiny-CED Net) for 13-organ abdominal CT segmentation that achieves competitive accuracy while keeping GPU memory usage under 2GB.
contribution_summary.novelty: The primary novelty is the explicit design for low-memory 3D multi-organ segmentation via aggressive low-resolution coarse segmentation followed by ROI-based per-organ fine segmentation using a lightweight 3D U-Net-like model.
strengths: Targets a practical constraint (low GPU memory) and quantifies memory usage (1.91GB) alongside accuracy
Clear two-stage pipeline description with concrete resampling and patch/ROI sizes
Reports multiple metrics (DSC, ASSD, SSD) and per-organ results
Includes a baseline comparison to a standard 3D U-Net with higher memory usage
weaknesses: Small labeled dataset and small test set (10 cases) with no validation set or cross-validation
No external validation; generalizability across scanners/sites is unknown
Limited reproducibility details (no batch size, epochs, augmentation, λ value, class weights, ROI localization specifics)
No statistical testing or uncertainty estimates
Performance remains relatively low for small/complex organs (e.g., duodenum, adrenal glands, pancreas)
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: the low-memory requirement and <25s inference suggest feasibility for deployment on modest hardware, but limited validation (single dataset, small test set, no external sites) and weaker performance on small organs constrain immediate clinical reliability without further multi-center evaluation and robustness testing.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training uses multiple retrospective cohorts with model-specific training sizes n=307 (whole liver), n=170 (Couinaud segments/veins), n=492 (lesions), all >50 subjects.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Data sources are clearly described as two hospitals (Hospital-1 for development; Hospital-2 for comparative analysis/external validation) with time ranges and inclusion/exclusion criteria.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: External validation cohorts are clearly defined (cohort-1 n=146, cohort-2 n=32), but the internal split strategy (train/val/test within Hospital-1) is not described; only that CT images were 'randomly selected' for training.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual measurements by human doctors are used as reference for FLR/FLR% evaluation, and networks are trained with 'manual annotations', but the annotation protocol (number of annotators, expertise, inter-rater agreement, tools) is not detailed in the main text (likely in Appendix E1).

Q5_external_validation.justification: Spatial external validation is performed on an independent hospital dataset (Hospital-2) with two designed cohorts (n=178 total).

Q6_architecture_description.justification: States use of 3D U-Net (Çiçek et al.) for all five segmentation tasks and describes a 4-stage pipeline, but does not provide architectural specifics (depth, channels, patch size) in the main text.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key training details (optimizer, learning rate, batch size, epochs, augmentation, hardware, framework) are not reported in the provided main text.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss function is not reported or justified in the provided text.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Some data/scan constraints are specified (contrast-enhanced CT, slice thickness <=2 mm, clear portal/hepatic veins; acquisition details referenced to Appendix E1), but concrete preprocessing steps (normalization, resampling, cropping) and postprocessing are not described in the main text.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release; data available only on reasonable request.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses multiple metrics/analyses beyond a single score: Spearman correlation, Bland-Altman agreement, ROC/AUC, sensitivity/specificity, McNemar test; however, segmentation Dice is not reported.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Includes Shapiro–Wilk, Levene’s test, Kruskal–Wallis, Mann–Whitney with Bonferroni correction, McNemar’s test, ROC with 95% CI, DeLong test with Bonferroni correction.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against a commonly used clinical semi-automatic tool (hepatic VCAR) and human doctors, but does not benchmark against modern deep learning SOTA segmentation methods (e.g., nnU-Net/transformers).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: No per-structure segmentation performance (e.g., Dice for liver/segments/vessels/lesions) is reported; evaluation focuses on downstream volumetry and candidate classification.

Q15_limitations.justification: Explicit limitations discussed: reference standard is manual volumetry not post-resection remnant; no functional MRI; no benchmarking vs contemporary architectures.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - explanation: Inclusion/exclusion criteria and hospital/time windows are described, but training set construction is 'randomly selected' from a larger pool without detailing sampling strategy; cohort-2 includes only patients who underwent major hepatectomy and did not develop PHLF, which may bias clinical representativeness for outcome-related analyses.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - explanation: Outcomes (FLR/FLR% and candidate categorization) are assessed consistently across methods, but the manual reference standard and annotation/measurement protocol details (blinding, number of raters) are not fully described in the main text.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - explanation: Reports downstream clinical metrics (correlation/AUC) but omits core segmentation metrics (e.g., Dice/HD) for the five segmentation tasks, limiting transparency about segmentation quality.

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Develops and externally validates a deep learning-based system (DL-FLRVS) that automates multi-structure liver segmentation from contrast-enhanced CT to compute FLR/FLR% and support candidate categorization for major hepatectomy, with direct comparison to hepatic VCAR and human doctors.
contribution_summary.novelty: Novelty is primarily clinical-workflow validation and comparative evaluation (DL vs a standard semi-automated workstation tool) for major hepatectomy planning across diverse liver conditions, rather than proposing a new segmentation architecture.
strengths: Independent external validation on a second hospital cohort (spatial generalization)
Clinically relevant endpoint evaluation (FLR/FLR% agreement and candidate categorization) rather than only technical segmentation feasibility
Comparison against both human doctors and a commonly used clinical tool (hepatic VCAR)
Uses appropriate statistical testing (ROC/AUC with CI, DeLong, McNemar, nonparametric tests, Bland–Altman)

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training set has 47 subjects (<50 threshold). Total dataset is 69 CTs, but the criterion specifies >50 for training.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Single-center in-house dataset from LMU Munich (Klinikum Großhadern) is clearly described, including scanner model and acquisition details.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Explicit split reported: 47 train / 11 validation / 11 test; rationale for split provided.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Annotation process described: bladder/rectum delineated by trained radiation oncologist; prostate contours redrawn under physician supervision per guidelines; stored as DICOM RT-struct point clouds and converted to masks.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset; data are not publicly available.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Network is described in detail (V-Net-based 3D U-Net, 5 levels, channels, kernel sizes, residual connections, normalization, activations, down/up-sampling, softmax + threshold).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Many training details are provided (optimizer with betas/epsilon, LR=1e-3, batch size=2, ~20k epochs, random search, augmentation parameters). However, some aspects are not fully reproducible (exact random search space/seed, stopping criterion specifics, and some implementation details).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Dice loss is specified and motivated by V-Net precedent, but there is limited explicit justification/ablation versus alternatives.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing and augmentation are clearly described: RT-struct to masks (plastimatch), resampling to 1mm isotropic, HU windowing [-150,150] and scaling, cropping to 128^3, and detailed augmentation (rot/shift/zoom/B-spline).

Q10_code_availability.justification: They provide a public V-Net code URL, but it is not clearly the exact training pipeline/configuration used for this study (likely adapted).

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses multiple geometric metrics (DSC, HDavg, HD95) and multiple dosimetric metrics (DVH parameters, gamma pass rate, conformity index).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Wilcoxon signed-rank test (p=0.05) for DVH differences; Pearson correlation with p-values reported.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares to prior SOTA via literature-reported numbers (Table 4) but not via direct re-implementation on the same dataset.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ DSC and HD metrics are reported; dosimetric parameters are also organ-specific (bladder/rectum/prostate).

Q15_limitations.justification: Limitations discussed: exclusion and failure on metal/fiducial artifacts, multi-physician contour variability, cropping effects on HD, need for human review, lack of public data.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient cohort is single-center and inclusion/exclusion is described (excluded hip implant and fiducial marker cases due to artifacts), but selection is not fully characterized (e.g., consecutive sampling, demographics) and exclusions may bias performance upward on cleaner scans.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Outcomes are assessed consistently using fixed test set and standardized metrics; however, evaluation is restricted to cropped 128^3 region and rectum evaluation is truncated to GT slices (reducing penalty for colon misclassification), which may bias geometric results.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low - Reports per-patient and per-organ geometric results, multiple dosimetric endpoints, and discusses a notable failure case; however, no external validation and no inference-time reporting limit completeness.

detailed_extraction.title: Dosimetric impact of deep learning-based CT auto-segmentation on radiation therapy treatment planning for prostate cancer

method.architecture_name: 3D U-Net (V-Net-based)

Systematic investigation of correlation between geometric segmentation quality (DSC) and dosimetric agreement (gamma/DVH).

augmentation: B-spline deformations (15x15x15 control grid; Gaussian shifts mu=0, sigma=30)
Intensity truncation to soft-tissue window [-150, 150] HU and rescaling to [-1, 1]

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that a V-Net-based 3D U-Net can achieve state-of-the-art pelvic organ segmentation on prostate CT and, crucially, quantifies the downstream dosimetric impact of using auto-segmentations for VMAT plan optimization.
contribution_summary.novelty: Shifts evaluation from purely geometric segmentation metrics to clinically relevant radiotherapy endpoints (DVH, gamma analysis, conformity index) and analyzes correlation between geometric and dosimetric agreement, showing they are not strongly coupled.
strengths: Clinically grounded evaluation: re-optimizes VMAT plans on auto-contours with controlled optimization settings and compares dose distributions.
Reports multiple geometric and dosimetric metrics, per organ and per patient, and includes statistical testing (Wilcoxon) and correlation analysis (Pearson with p-values).
Clear description of preprocessing, augmentation, and network architecture; provides hyperparameter values used in the final model.
weaknesses: No external validation; single-center dataset and patient data not shareable, limiting generalizability and reproducibility.
Training set size is modest (47) and excludes artifact-heavy cases; model fails on metal/fiducial artifacts (reported very poor DSC/HD).
Evaluation is constrained by cropping to 128^3 and rectum evaluation limited to GT slices, which may underestimate some errors (especially HD and colon misclassification).
Public code link is generic V-Net repository; full study-specific pipeline/configuration is not clearly released; inference time not reported.
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High relevance for radiotherapy workflows: results suggest auto-segmentation can often yield acceptable DVH agreement and target coverage, but gamma pass rates and a notable failure case indicate mandatory human review and caution for artifact-containing scans.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training dataset (Dataset 1) includes 88 subjects (kidney donors) with both CECT and NCCT; split yields >50 subjects for training (~57).

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Data sources and acquisition context are described: single-institution retrospective cohorts from UZ Leuven; additional test sets from PACS, PCCT scanner, and low-dose NCCT; scanner models and protocol summaries provided (with more in supplement).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Dataset 1 randomly split into train/val/test with explicit ratios 0.65/0.175/0.175; external test sets (2–4) used only for testing.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual delineation process described (3D Slicer SegmentEditor), exclusion of renal sinus fat, annotator expertise levels stated (medical student under expert radiologist supervision; external test sets annotated by expert radiologist).

Q5_external_validation.justification: Evaluated on multiple independent test sets not used for training: Test set 2 (institutional PACS CECT, not necessarily healthy), Test set 3 (PCCT 60/190 keV), Test set 4 (low-dose NCCT).

Q6_architecture_description.justification: 3D U-Net architecture described with residual units, encoder/decoder downsampling/upsampling, patch size, dropout, and separate identical models for CECT and NCCT; figure provided.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key training details reported: optimizer (Adam), learning rate (3e-4 constant), epochs (1500), batch size (8), patch size (96^3), framework versions (PyTorch 1.10, MONAI 1.0.1), CUDA 11.3, GPU (RTX 3090), inference sliding-window overlap (0.5).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Dice loss is specified, but justification/rationale for choosing Dice loss over alternatives is minimal (primarily stated as objective criterion).

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing and post-processing are clearly described: resampling strategy (NCCT to 1.5 mm isotropic), HU windowing per modality, min-max normalization, one-hot labels, augmentations, connected components, NC upsampling and validation-based threshold selection.

Q10_code_availability.justification: Code and trained weights are available only upon reasonable request; not publicly released with a persistent URL.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses DSC plus multiple additional metrics: mean percentage volume error, mean absolute error for axes, ICC with 95% CIs, Bland–Altman analysis, and inference time.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports ICC with 95% confidence intervals and p-values; includes Bland–Altman plots; reports CIs for DSC and statistical comparison vs TotalSegmentator (p < 0.001).

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Benchmarks against TotalSegmentator on Test set 1 and compares against ellipsoid volume estimation baseline; discusses related work (KiTS, other kidney segmentation studies).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Segmentation is for left and right kidneys, but results are primarily reported as overall/mean DSC and measurement errors; explicit separate Dice for left vs right kidney is not provided.

Q15_limitations.justification: Limitations discussed: single-institution training, protocol homogeneity, small external test sets, healthy-donor training cohort limiting generalization to diseased kidneys, lack of cortex/medulla segmentation, need for broader dose/protocol evaluation.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Training cohort is healthy kidney donors from a single institution/protocol; inclusion for external test sets is described but still institution-limited and small, which may limit representativeness and introduce spectrum bias.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - Outcomes assessed consistently with standardized metrics; manual annotations performed with defined tools and expert oversight; external test sets annotated by expert radiologist to validate against experienced labels.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low - Reports multiple relevant outcomes (segmentation, volume/axes errors, ICC with CIs, inference time) across all test sets and includes limitations; however, lack of per-kidney Dice may omit some granularity.

Derivation of clinically used quantitative descriptors (volume and 3 orthogonal renal axes) from segmentation via PCA-oriented minimal bounding box
Validation across acquisition variations: PACS CECT, photon-counting CT virtual monoenergetic reconstructions (60/190 keV), and low-dose NCCT

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces and validates two modality-specific 3D U-Net models that segment kidney parenchyma on CECT and NCCT and automatically compute clinically relevant renal volume and principal axes with human-level agreement.
contribution_summary.novelty: Beyond kidney segmentation, the work operationalizes automated extraction of standard clinical renal descriptors (volume and 3D axes) from CT for both contrast and noncontrast imaging, and tests robustness on PCCT monoenergetic reconstructions and low-dose NCCT.
strengths: Clear end-to-end pipeline: segmentation plus quantitative measurement extraction (PCA + oriented bounding box)
Separate models for CECT and NCCT with modality-appropriate preprocessing and post-processing
Multiple independent test sets including different scanner technology (PCCT) and low-dose imaging
Strong evaluation with ICC (95% CIs), Bland–Altman, and comparison to TotalSegmentator and ellipsoid baseline
Clinically motivated annotation choice: excludes renal sinus fat to better reflect functional parenchyma measurement needs
weaknesses: Training data from a single institution and largely healthy donor population; potential limited generalization to diseased kidneys
External test sets are relatively small (n=8–18–15)
No publicly released code/weights (available only upon request), limiting independent reproducibility
Per-kidney (left vs right) segmentation metrics are not explicitly reported
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High potential for clinical workflow support in renal morphometry (donor evaluation, longitudinal monitoring) due to fast inference and strong agreement with expert measurements; however, broader validation on diverse institutions and pathological kidneys is needed before deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training uses 89 manually contoured patients (>50). Augmentation expands samples but the underlying subject count is still adequate.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Training sources described (post-treatment 177Lu-octreotate SPECT/CT low-dose CT plus diagnostic CT and RT simulation CT; scanner models listed). Test set described as a Phase II 177Lu-PSMA-617 trial cohort with registry ID.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Clear separation: 89 patients for training; augmented to 712 with 640 used for training and 72 for validation; independent test cohort of 24 patients.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual kidney contours used as ground truth; test cohort contours were manually contoured and reviewed by a nuclear medicine physician. However, details such as number of annotators, inter-observer variability, and annotation protocol are limited.

Q5_external_validation.justification: External/independent validation performed on a separate prospective trial cohort (24 patients) distinct from the training cohort.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: 3D U-Net-like CNN described with 21 conv layers, 3x3x3 kernels, batch norm + ReLU, 2x2x2 max pooling, symmetric upsampling, skip connections via concatenation, 3-class output; input size 128x128x64; figures provided.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Some training details provided (300 epochs, framework versions, GPU, augmentation types, input resampling). Key hyperparameters are missing/unclear (optimizer, learning rate schedule, batch size, weight initialization, regularization).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Dice loss is used and explicitly justified to address class imbalance/background dominance and improve boundary sensitivity; discussion contrasts with prior approach.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing pipeline clearly described: rigid registration to reference using bony anatomy, cropping to kidney search volume, resampling to match network input, and detailed augmentation (with appendix reference). Postprocessing (upsample, smoothing, fusion, DICOM-RT export) also described.

Q10_code_availability.justification: Code/model not publicly released, but authors state the model and software may be distributed on request.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses Dice and mean distance-to-agreement; also reports volume differences and downstream clinical metric (renal absorbed dose difference).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Paired t-test used to compare dose estimates; p-values reported. No confidence intervals and limited statistical treatment for segmentation metrics.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: No quantitative comparison against other automated segmentation baselines (e.g., atlas/graph-cuts/other CNNs). Evaluation is primarily against manual contours.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Reports separate results for right and left kidneys (Dice, MDA, dose differences) and discusses outliers.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses failure modes and limitations: cystic kidneys underrepresented in training, occasional bowel inclusion, CT streak artifact, systematic over-segmentation (~7%) due to upsampling/smoothing; recommends contour review.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Training cohort is heterogeneous and not fully characterized with explicit inclusion/exclusion criteria; test cohort is clearly defined (Phase II trial) but overall patient selection details (consecutive vs selected) are limited.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Outcomes assessed consistently using the same metrics and manual contours as reference; however, annotation process details and blinding of reviewers are not described, and preprocessing (registration/cropping) could influence performance.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low - Key segmentation and clinical outcomes are reported (Dice, MDA, volume trend, dose comparison) and notable failure cases are discussed; however, lack of full per-case reporting in main text (moved to supplement) slightly limits transparency.

detailed_extraction.title: Deep Learning Renal Segmentation for Fully Automated Radiation Dose Estimation in Unsealed Source Therapy

method.architecture_name: 3D U-Net-like CNN (modified from Pazhitnykh et al.)
key_innovations: End-to-end clinical pipeline integrating rigid registration-based localization, 3D CNN kidney segmentation, and DICOM-RT export for PACS/workstation use
Use and explicit justification of Dice loss to mitigate background class dominance and improve boundary delineation
Demonstration of downstream clinical utility: automated kidney contours used to compute renal absorbed dose from voxel dose maps with no meaningful bias vs manual

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces and clinically integrates a 3D CNN kidney segmentation pipeline on low-dose non-contrast CT to enable fully automated renal dosimetry from post-therapy quantitative SPECT/CT.
contribution_summary.novelty: Less about proposing a new segmentation architecture and more about demonstrating a practical, PACS-integrated 3D deep learning workflow that produces DICOM-RT kidney structures and yields comparable renal absorbed dose estimates to expert manual contours in a prospective therapy cohort.
strengths: Independent test cohort from a prospective clinical trial (external to training set)
Clear preprocessing/localization strategy (rigid registration + cropping) enabling feasible 3D inference on limited hardware
Multiple evaluation endpoints including clinically relevant absorbed dose agreement
Explicit discussion of failure modes (cystic kidneys, bowel inclusion, streak artifacts) and systematic volume bias
weaknesses: No public code/model release (only available on request)
Incomplete reporting of training hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, batch size), limiting reproducibility
No quantitative comparison to alternative automated segmentation methods (atlas/graph-cuts/other CNN baselines)
Ground-truth annotation protocol and inter-observer variability not fully characterized
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High practical relevance for nuclear medicine dosimetry workflows: produces DICOM-RT structures integrated with hospital systems and shows small dose differences vs manual contours; however, contour review is still recommended, especially for cystic kidneys and artifact-prone scans.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses WORD with 100 training CT volumes (>50), plus 20 validation and 30 test.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset is clearly identified as the public WORD dataset; patient cohort and acquisition characteristics (resolution, spacing, slice count) are described.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Explicit split following WORD default settings: 100 train / 20 val / 30 test.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States scribble annotations for foreground organs and background in axial view are provided for training volumes; however, detailed annotation protocol (who annotated, QA, inter-rater, how scribbles were created) is not described.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset beyond WORD.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: TDNet is described: shared encoder + three decoders with dilation rates 1/3/6; different initializations; bottleneck dropout to auxiliary decoders; inference uses primary decoder.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides key training details: optimizer (SGD), momentum, weight decay, poly LR schedule, batch size=1, patch size [80,96,96], iterations 6e4, loss weights (alpha=10, beta=1) with ramp-up, preprocessing window/level and normalization, sliding-window inference.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Losses are well-defined mathematically (pCE, USPC with entropy-based weights, MPCC with class affinity KL); motivation is given (stability, reliability, class relations) but not deeply justified/compared theoretically.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is clearly described: HU window/level 400/50 and normalization to [0,1]; patch-based training and sliding-window inference; explicitly no post-processing.

Q10_code_availability.justification: They state baselines are implemented using an open-source codebase (WSL4MIS), but do not provide a repository for TDNet/this paper’s implementation.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports DSC, ASD, and HD95.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports paired t-test p-values (<0.05) versus the second-best method (DMPLS) and provides mean±std.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple prior scribble/weakly-supervised methods (pCE, TV, USTM, EM, DMPLS) and a fully-supervised upper bound.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides per-organ DSC (mean±std) for seven organs.

Q15_limitations.justification: Mentions future work on sparser labels and implicitly limited evaluation scope; however, discussion of concrete failure cases/limitations is brief and not systematic.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Uses a single dataset (WORD) with a predefined split and a specific cancer cohort; inclusion/exclusion criteria and selection process are not detailed beyond dataset description.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - Same experimental setting for all methods is claimed; consistent metrics (DSC/ASD/HD95) and standardized split; inference uses primary decoder only and no post-processing for fairness.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Strong quantitative reporting and ablations are provided, but no external validation and limited explicit failure-case analysis; code not released may limit verification.

detailed_extraction.title: Scribble-based 3D Multiple Abdominal Organ Segmentation via Triple-branch Multi-dilated Network with Pixel- and Class-wise Consistency

method.architecture_name: TDNet (3D U-Net backbone with triple-branch multi-dilated decoders)
key_innovations: Triple-branch multi-dilated decoder design (dilation rates 1, 3, 6) to produce complementary multi-scale predictions for scribble-supervised 3D multi-organ CT segmentation
Uncertainty-weighted Soft Pseudo label Consistency (USPC): ensemble-averaged soft pseudo labels rectified by voxel-wise entropy weights; KL consistency to each decoder output
Multi-view Projection-based Class-similarity Consistency (MPCC): enforces consistency of inter-class affinity matrices computed from multi-view (axial/coronal/sagittal) projections
loss_functions: Partially supervised cross-entropy on scribble voxels (pCE / Lsup)
USPC (uncertainty-weighted KL divergence between each decoder prediction and ensemble soft pseudo label)
MPCC (KL divergence between per-view class affinity matrices and their ensemble average)
Ramp-up weighting for unsupervised losses (alpha_t, beta_t)
method.learning_rate: Poly learning-rate schedule (exact initial LR not specified)
method.epochs: Not reported (trained for 6×10^4 iterations)
augmentation: Feature-level dropout/perturbation at bottleneck before auxiliary decoders (ratio in (0, 0.5))
Different decoder weight initializations (Kaiming/Xavier/Normal) as a perturbation source

names: WORD
dataset.ct_scanner: Not reported
dataset.slice_thickness: Slice spacing 2.5–3.0 mm (in-plane resolution 0.976×0.976 mm)
dataset.image_size: Each volume 159–330 slices; 512×512 per slice

results.inference_time: Not reported
results.gpu_memory: 11 GB (RTX 2080 Ti used; memory size stated)

implementation.training_time: Not reported

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces TDNet, a scribble-supervised 3D multi-organ CT segmentation framework that combines multi-dilated triple decoders with pixel-wise uncertainty-weighted pseudo-label consistency and class-wise affinity consistency.
contribution_summary.novelty: Compared to prior scribble-supervised approaches (often 2D and/or relying on hard pseudo-labels), this work (i) uses a 3D triple-decoder design with different dilation rates to generate complementary soft pseudo labels, (ii) rectifies pseudo-label supervision using voxel-wise entropy weighting, and (iii) regularizes multi-class structure via multi-view projected class-affinity consistency.
strengths: Strong experimental protocol on WORD with clear split and multiple metrics (DSC/ASD/HD95)
Demonstrates improvements over several established scribble/weakly-supervised baselines and includes ablation studies
Uses statistical testing (paired t-test) and reports mean±std
No post-processing and consistent inference choice (primary decoder) improves fairness of comparisons
weaknesses: No external validation; generalization beyond WORD is unknown
Limited description of scribble generation/annotation quality control and no inter-annotator analysis
No public release of the proposed method’s code/model (only mentions using an open-source codebase for baselines)
Limited explicit failure-case analysis; gallbladder and pancreas remain relatively low-performing
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Potentially clinically relevant because it reduces annotation burden (scribbles instead of dense labels) while achieving competitive multi-organ performance on abdominal CT; however, lack of external validation and limited robustness/failure analysis constrain immediate clinical deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: RAOS contains 413 CT scans (SetA used for training/internal eval: 220 train + 67 internal eval), exceeding the >50 training-subject threshold.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset is retrospectively collected from two institutions; scanners are described as multiple SIEMENS CT scanners with/without contrast; patient cohort characteristics and treatment strategies are summarized.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Split is described by clinical subsets: SetA (without surgery) used for training/internal evaluation (220/67), and SetB/SetC used as robustness test sets; cross-dataset evaluations are also reported.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual delineation by a senior radiation oncologist using MIM Software under RTOG guidelines, then reviewed by a second expert oncologist with consensus discussion, including special attention to resection cases.

Q5_external_validation.justification: External/cross-dataset evaluation is performed between RAOS and public datasets (BTCV, AMOS, AbdomenCT-1K), including RAOS→public and public→RAOS directions (AMOS).

Q6_architecture_description.justification: The paper benchmarks multiple known architectures (nnU-Net, 3D UX-Net, REPUXNET, nnFormer, Swin UNETR, TransBTS, UNETR) but does not re-describe their full architectural details (relies on cited implementations).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Framework (PyTorch 1.10) and hardware (8× V100) are given; however, training hyperparameters, schedules, and exact preprocessing are not detailed because default settings/pretrained models of each baseline are used.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss functions are not specified or justified; experiments use each baseline implementation's default settings.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing/post-processing are not explicitly described; the study indicates using default pipelines of the baseline implementations.

Q10_code_availability.justification: Code/dataset resources are provided via a public GitHub repository: https://github.com/Luoxd1996/RAOS.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses Dice (DSC) and Normalized Surface Dice (NSD, 2mm tolerance), and introduces an additional robustness metric: organ hallucination ratio for missing-organ cases.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean ± standard deviation for DSC/NSD per organ and per subset, but does not report hypothesis tests, confidence intervals, or significance testing between methods.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares seven state-of-the-art 3D segmentation methods under a unified benchmark setting and evaluates robustness across clinical subsets and across datasets.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ DSC and NSD are reported for SetA/SetB/SetC; hallucination ratios are also reported per missing organ type.

Q15_limitations.justification: Explicitly discusses robustness failures on post-surgery and organ-missing cases, including widespread hallucinations; concludes current SOTA is not clinically applicable for these corner cases and motivates future work.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient cohort is described (two institutions, abdominal lesions, latest scan per patient), but inclusion/exclusion criteria and consecutive sampling details are not fully specified; SetB is small (22) which may affect representativeness.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - Outcomes are assessed consistently using the same metrics (DSC/NSD) and the same evaluation protocol across methods; ground truth is generated with a standardized expert process.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low - Reports per-organ results across subsets and includes additional robustness metric (hallucination ratio); however, some implementation details (loss/preprocessing) are not reported because defaults are used.

detailed_extraction.title: Rethinking Abdominal Organ Segmentation (RAOS) in the clinical scenario: A robustness evaluation benchmark with challenging cases

method.architecture_name: Benchmark study (nnU-Net, 3D UX-Net, REPUXNET, nnFormer, Swin UNETR, TransBTS, UNETR)
key_innovations: Introduces RAOS: a clinically oriented abdominal CT multi-organ segmentation dataset with post-surgery and organ-missing corner cases
Defines clinical subsets (no surgery; surgery without organ missing; surgery with organ missing) to evaluate robustness under anatomical shifts
Introduces/uses an organ hallucination ratio metric to quantify false-positive segmentation of resected (missing) organs
Provides cross-dataset generalization evaluation between RAOS and public datasets (BTCV, AMOS, AbdomenCT-1K)

names: RAOS
BTCV
AMOS
AbdomenCT-1K
dataset.test_subjects: SetB: 22; SetC: 74 (used as robustness/generalization test sets after training on SetA)
dataset.ct_scanner: Multiple SIEMENS CT scanners; with/without contrast agents
dataset.image_size: Approx. 80k–84k 2D slices total; volumetric dimensions not specified

results.overall_dice: SetA mean DSC (nnU-Net): 86.65%; SetB mean DSC (nnU-Net): 84.12%; SetC mean DSC (nnU-Net): 80.55%

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces RAOS, a large-scale abdominal CT multi-organ dataset and benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate segmentation robustness on clinically challenging post-surgery and organ-missing cases, including quantification of organ hallucination.
contribution_summary.novelty: Unlike prior abdominal benchmarks that mainly reflect protocol/scanner domain shifts, RAOS targets anatomical domain shift from surgery (partial/whole excision) and formalizes evaluation of missing-organ hallucinations with a dedicated metric across multiple SOTA 3D methods.
strengths: Large, clinically motivated dataset (413 CTs) with broad organ coverage (17/19 organs) including underrepresented structures (colon, intestine, rectum, prostate, seminal vesicles, femoral heads)
High-quality annotation pipeline (expert manual delineation + senior review/consensus under RTOG guidelines)
Robustness-focused evaluation design (SetA training; SetB/SetC as progressively harder clinical corner-case test sets)
Comprehensive benchmarking across multiple strong 3D baselines and cross-dataset generalization analyses
Introduces/operationalizes organ hallucination evaluation for resected organs
weaknesses: Limited methodological reproducibility details (losses, preprocessing, hyperparameters) due to reliance on default baseline settings
No formal statistical significance testing between methods (beyond mean±SD)
SetB is small (22 cases), which may limit stability of estimates for that subset
Primarily a benchmark/dataset paper; does not propose a new segmentation model to address the identified failure modes
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High clinical relevance as it evaluates failure modes critical for radiotherapy planning (post-surgery anatomy and missing organs). Findings indicate current SOTA models can be unreliable in these scenarios (performance drops and frequent hallucinations), supporting the need for robustness methods before clinical deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses 281 CT volumes for training (>50) and 50 for validation; additionally evaluates on an external 150-case test set.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Training/validation data described as in-house clinical contrast-enhanced abdominal CT (portal venous phase) for gastric surgery planning; external test set from a different hospital/scanner/study (gastric cancer patients).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Random split of 331 cases into 281 training and 50 validation; external test set of 150 cases is separate and described as completely unseen.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States 'manually labeled' for 7 structures in training/validation; for external test set, labels were semi-automatically delineated by three trained researchers and confirmed by a clinician. Detailed annotation protocol/inter-rater agreement not provided.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Yes—tests on an independent dataset from a different hospital with 150 CT scans (liver/spleen/pancreas).

Q6_architecture_description.justification: 3D U-Net architecture described (analysis/synthesis paths, 4 resolution levels, 3x3x3 convs, 2x2x2 pooling/up-conv, skip connections, 1x1x1 output to 8 classes) and hierarchical two-stage setup explained.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides many training details (SGD, iterations, patch size, downsampling, batch norm, augmentation parameters, tiling/overlap strategy, dilation radius r=3). Missing key hyperparameters such as learning rate, momentum, weight decay, batch size, and stopping criteria specifics.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Uses weighted voxel-wise softmax cross-entropy with explicit weighting formula to address class imbalance; rationale is given (foreground/background imbalance), but no comparison/ablation vs alternative losses.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Clearly describes downsampling by factor 2, body-mask candidate region generation via thresholding + morphological operations, random sub-volume sampling from candidate regions, and augmentation (B-spline elastic deformations + rotations).

Q10_code_availability.justification: References an open-source 3D U-Net implementation (Caffe) and provides a URL, but does not provide full training scripts/configs for the hierarchical pipeline or exact experiment setup.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Primary metric is Dice; additionally reports candidate-region generation sensitivity/recall and false-positive rate vs dilation radius. No boundary metrics (e.g., HD/ASSD) for segmentation quality.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean/std/median/min/max Dice across cases, but no confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, or statistical significance analysis.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple prior methods (atlas/registration, RF+GC, 2D FCN approaches) and reports comparative Dice and runtime (with caveat about differing evaluation schemes).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides per-organ Dice for 7 organs on validation and for 3 organs on external test set, including summary statistics.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses GPU memory constraints requiring patch/tiling, class imbalance issues, difficulty for small/thin organs, and challenges in direct comparison due to dataset/evaluation differences; also notes overlap tiling trade-off with runtime.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient inclusion/exclusion criteria and sampling strategy beyond 'clinical CT for gastric surgery planning' and 'gastric cancer patients' are not fully specified; random split is stated but cohort definition details are limited.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - Outcomes are assessed consistently using Dice across validation and external test sets; inference procedure is described (tiling, overlap averaging).
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Strong reporting of Dice and summary stats, but limited reporting of additional clinically relevant metrics (e.g., surface distances) and limited detail on annotation protocol/variability; external test set only includes 3 labeled organs.

detailed_extraction.title: Hierarchical 3D fully convolutional networks for multi-organ segmentation

method.architecture_name: Hierarchical 3D U-Net (3D FCN) coarse-to-fine
key_innovations: Two-stage hierarchical coarse-to-fine training/inference using candidate regions to reduce class imbalance and focus on organ boundaries

Stage-1 body-mask candidate region (C1) via simple thresholding+morphology (~40% voxels) and Stage-2 foreground-dilated candidate region (C2) derived from Stage-1 predictions (~10% voxels) with high recall
Optional overlapping-tile probability averaging at inference to improve accuracy with moderate runtime increase
loss_functions: Weighted voxel-wise softmax cross-entropy (class weights based on inverse frequency within candidate region)
augmentation: 3D elastic deformations (B-spline; grid spacing 32 voxels; deformation sampled from N(0, sd=4))

names: In-house clinical contrast-enhanced abdominal CT (portal venous phase) for gastric surgery planning
External unseen abdominal CT dataset from different hospital/scanner/study (gastric cancer patients) with liver/spleen/pancreas labels
dataset.slice_thickness: 0.5-1.0 mm (train/val); 0.4-0.80 mm (test) (z spacing reported as voxel dimension range)
dataset.image_size: Each volume: 512x512 pixels per slice; 460-1177 slices (train/val) and 263-1061 slices (test). Voxel size ranges: train/val [0.59-0.98, 0.59-0.98, 0.5-1.0] mm; test [0.55-0.82, 0.55-0.82, 0.4-0.80] mm.

results.overall_dice: Validation Stage 2 (non-overlapping) mean across 7 organs: 0.792
results.inference_time: Per volume: ~1.4-3.3 minutes per stage (non-overlapping). With overlapping tiles in Stage 2: ~1.6-4.4 minutes per stage (depends on candidate region size).
results.gpu_memory: 12 GB (training GPU); 8 GB (testing GPU)

implementation.gpu: NVIDIA GeForce GTX TITAN X (12GB) for training; NVIDIA GeForce 1080 (8GB) for testing
implementation.training_time: 2-3 days for 200k iterations (Stage 1); Stage 2 trained for additional 115k iterations (time not separately stated).

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a two-stage hierarchical coarse-to-fine 3D U-Net pipeline that uses automatically generated candidate regions to reduce class imbalance and improve multi-organ abdominal CT segmentation, with external validation on an independent 150-case dataset.
contribution_summary.novelty: Rather than training a single 3D FCN on the full volume, the method uses (1) a simple body-mask candidate region and (2) a second-stage candidate region derived from dilated first-stage predictions, enabling the second network to focus on organ boundaries while maintaining >99% recall of foreground voxels.
strengths: Large in-house training set (281 train / 50 val) for 3D multi-organ segmentation
External testing on a different hospital/scanner dataset (150 cases), supporting generalization claims
Clear per-organ reporting and demonstration that small/thin structures benefit substantially (e.g., artery Dice 0.59→0.796; pancreas improved notably on external test 0.685→0.822)
No post-processing required; relatively fast inference compared to atlas-based methods
weaknesses: Incomplete reproducibility details (missing learning rate, batch size, momentum/weight decay, and full training configuration for the hierarchical pipeline)
Evaluation relies mainly on Dice; lacks boundary/surface distance metrics important for clinical usability
External dataset only labeled for 3 organs, limiting external validation for the full 7-organ task
Candidate-region generation depends on dilation radius choice (r=3), which may require tuning for other datasets
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Relevant for pre-operative planning and abdominal organ delineation in contrast-enhanced CT; the hierarchical approach improves difficult organs (notably pancreas) and runs in minutes per scan on a single GPU, but additional validation with broader cohorts, full organ label sets, and boundary metrics would be needed for clinical deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Large internal training set: 389 patients / 663 CT scans for training (UCLH), exceeding >50 subjects.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Sources clearly described: retrospective UCLH planning CT cohort (2008-2016) plus curated open-source TCIA subsets (TCGA-HNSC, Head-Neck Cetuximab) and PDDCA challenge dataset.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Explicit split by patient with counts for train/val/test; multiple scans per patient kept within the same subset; test set includes a primary adjudicated subset (19 patients/21 scans).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Annotation pipeline described in detail: radiographer segmentation + peer arbitration; for gold standard, oncologist review/correction; adherence to Brouwer Atlas; TCIA original labels rejected and re-labeled.

Q5_external_validation.justification: External testing performed on independent datasets from different sites/countries: TCIA test set and PDDCA test set.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Residual 3D U-Net with 8 levels described (down/up residual blocks, FC block, 21-channel output); figure and block-level description provided.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Training details provided: Adam, steps (120k), batch size (32 across 32 GPUs), LR schedule, L2 regularization, extensive augmentation parameters, model selection criterion.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss specified and motivated: regularised top-k-percent pixel-wise binary cross-entropy to address class imbalance and focus on hard pixels.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Inclusion/exclusion based on scan resolution/slice thickness described; some handling of spacing (exclude non-standard, subsample 1.25mm). However, full intensity normalization/cropping/resampling pipeline is not comprehensively detailed.

Q10_code_availability.justification: Full training code not released (proprietary components). Surface DSC implementation released and labeled TCIA subset released; thus partial availability.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses surface DSC (primary) and volumetric DSC (for comparability).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Figures include notched boxplots with 95% CI around medians; however, no formal hypothesis testing/p-values and limited reporting of confidence intervals for mean performance.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares to prior work via literature table of volumetric DSC (indirect, not same data/protocol). Also compares to radiographers on UCLH/TCIA. No direct head-to-head reimplementation on identical splits.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ results reported (surface DSC plots; volumetric DSC table with per-organ means and SD; additional per-patient tables in appendix).

Q15_limitations.justification: Limitations discussed: CT-only (no MRI/PET), omitted OARs, limited oncologist variability, protocol ambiguity (brainstem), external performance drops, lack of protected attributes for fairness.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient inclusion/exclusion criteria and cohort dates are clearly defined for UCLH and TCIA, but UCLH is single-centre and the primary expert-comparison test subset is a smaller adjudicated subset (21 scans) selected from the larger test pool, which may introduce selection effects.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - Outcomes assessed consistently using the same metrics and a clearly defined annotation/adjudication protocol; model and radiographers compared against oncologist-adjudicated ground truth; organ-specific tolerances derived from inter-observer variation.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low - Reports per-organ metrics across multiple datasets and discusses weaker organs; provides both the proposed metric and standard Dice for comparability; releases labeled TCIA subset enabling partial verification.

detailed_extraction.title: Deep learning to achieve clinically applicable segmentation of head and neck anatomy for radiotherapy

method.architecture_name: Residual 3D U-Net
key_innovations: Introduces surface Dice similarity coefficient (surface DSC) with organ-specific tolerances to better reflect clinical contour-correction effort
Large-scale training on routine clinical planning CT with protocol-conformant labels and expert adjudication for a clinically representative test set
Demonstrates generalization across external datasets (TCIA, PDDCA) and provides re-labeled TCIA subset publicly
method.learning_rate: Initial 1e-4; scaled by 1/2 at 24k steps, 1/8 at 60k, 1/64 at 108k, 1/256 at 114k (total 120k steps)
method.epochs: Not reported (training specified in steps: 120,000)
augmentation: Mirroring with p=0.5 (swap left/right labels accordingly)
Elastic deformations (control grid 100mm spacing; displacement SD 5mm; in-plane only; cubic B-spline interpolation)

names: UCLH (internal clinical planning CT)
TCIA (TCGA-HNSC, Head-Neck Cetuximab; re-labeled by authors)
PDDCA (MICCAI 2015 Head & Neck OAR challenge dataset)
dataset.total_subjects: UCLH: 486 eligible patients (838 scans) in cohort; used splits: 389 train, 51 val, 46 test; primary adjudicated test subset: 19 patients/21 scans. TCIA: 30 patients/31 scans used (6 val/7 scans; 24 test/24 scans). PDDCA: 15 patients/15 scans.
dataset.val_subjects: UCLH val: 51 patients (100 scans); TCIA val: 6 patients (7 scans)
dataset.test_subjects: UCLH test: 46 patients (75 scans) with adjudicated subset 19 patients (21 scans); TCIA test: 24 patients (24 scans) (some figures/tables refer to 28 scans); PDDCA test: 15 patients (15 scans)
dataset.ct_scanner: Not reported
dataset.slice_thickness: UCLH standard 2.5mm; 1.25mm scans subsampled; TCIA included only CT planning scans with 2.5mm slice thickness; PDDCA slice thickness/voxel height varies 2-3mm
dataset.image_size: Not reported; voxel spacing examples: UCLH ~0.976×0.976×2.5mm; TCIA in-plane 0.94-1.27mm; PDDCA axial 0.98-1.27mm in-plane and 2-3mm through-plane

challenging_organs: Brainstem (ambiguous CT boundaries; lower external performance)
Lens-Rt (indistinct borders in some TCIA scans; lower external performance)
Small glands/structures (e.g., lacrimal glands, cochleae) implied by lower volumetric DSC and class imbalance

results.primary_metric: Surface Dice similarity coefficient (surface DSC) at organ-specific tolerance τ
results.overall_dice: Not reported as a single overall mean; per-organ volumetric DSC and surface DSC reported across datasets
results.inference_time: Not reported
results.gpu_memory: Not reported

comparison_results.Radiographer_vs_Oncologist_UCLH: Surface DSC: model similar to radiographers across all 21 OARs (non-substantial differences within ±5% band in plots); volumetric DSC radiographer means reported in Table 1
comparison_results.Radiographer_vs_Oncologist_TCIA: Surface DSC: model similar to radiographers; model below radiographers for brainstem and right lens on TCIA as discussed
comparison_results.Prior_work: Indirect comparison via literature volumetric DSC table (Table 1) across many prior deep learning studies

implementation.framework: Not reported (mentions proprietary components; synchronous training across 32 GPUs)
implementation.training_time: Not reported

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Presents a residual 3D U-Net for multi-organ head-and-neck OAR segmentation on planning CT that achieves radiographer-level performance and introduces the clinically motivated surface DSC metric with organ-specific tolerances.
contribution_summary.novelty: Shifts evaluation toward clinically meaningful boundary agreement (surface DSC with tolerance derived from oncologist inter-observer variation) and validates against expert-adjudicated contours plus external datasets; also releases a re-labeled TCIA subset and surface-distance code.
strengths: Large, clinically representative internal dataset with clear inclusion/exclusion and patient-level splits
Strong annotation protocol with oncologist adjudication for gold standard and independent radiographer baselines
External validation on TCIA and PDDCA demonstrating generalization
Introduces and carefully implements surface DSC to better reflect contour-editing workload
Detailed training recipe (augmentations, optimizer, LR schedule) enabling approximate replication
weaknesses: Full model training code not released; framework and hardware specifics limited
