Preprocessing pipeline details (e.g., intensity normalization, cropping strategy) not fully specified beyond spacing-based exclusions
State-of-the-art comparison largely indirect (different datasets/protocols), limiting definitive ranking
Primary expert-comparison test set is relatively small (21 scans) compared to the full held-out pool
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High: targets radiotherapy planning workflow, evaluates against expert-adjudicated contours, and uses a metric aligned with clinical correction effort; however, deployment would still require prospective workflow/time-savings studies, broader multi-centre validation, and regulatory approval.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses two datasets: LCTSC (60 patients) and PCT (43 patients). Training is done via 5-fold cross-validation; at least one fold trains on ~48 (LCTSC) and ~34-35 (PCT). Overall dataset size is >50 total subjects, though PCT alone is <50.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset sources are clearly described as publicly available: 2017 Lung CT Segmentation Challenge (LCTSC) and Pancreas-CT (PCT) from TCIA, with citations.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Explicit 5-fold cross-validation described, including grouping (LCTSC: 5 groups of 12; PCT: 5 groups of 8 or 9).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Ground truth is implied to be the provided manual segmentations from LCTSC/PCT; however, the paper does not detail who annotated, how many annotators, quality control, or inter-rater variability.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No external validation on an independent dataset beyond the two public datasets used for cross-validation; no held-out external site/scanner evaluation.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described in detail (3D U-Net variant with residual blocks, instance norm, leaky ReLU, spatial dropout, skip concatenations, multi-scale outputs, and a separate fine-segmentation stage for esophagus/spinal cord).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides key hyperparameters (HU windowing, resampling, patch sizes, stride, optimizer Adam, initial LR=0.0005, LR halving rule, early stopping criteria). Missing details such as batch size, weight initialization, exact class weights for weighted Dice, and augmentation.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Weighted Dice loss is defined mathematically and motivated implicitly by segmentation needs/class imbalance, but there is no explicit justification/ablation comparing losses.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing and inference pipeline are clearly described: HU clipping/normalization, body-contour cropping, resampling/downsampling, patch extraction, sliding-window inference, overlap-mean fusion, resampling back, and fine-segmentation insertion.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is provided.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses Dice for segmentation and Relative Dose Error (RDE) for dosimetry evaluation; also reports runtime and Monte Carlo uncertainty (<0.5%).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports distributions via boxplots, medians, and mean±std (Table 1). No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance testing.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares segmentation DSC to LCTSC reference and 7 challenge contestant methods for thoracic organs. No comparable SOTA comparison for abdominal (PCT) segmentation; dosimetry compared to phantom-based VirtualDose workflow.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ Dice is reported for all organs in both datasets; per-organ RDE ranges are also reported for multiple organs.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations and failure-prone organs (esophagus/duodenum), limited training data, effect of abnormalities, low intra-abdominal fat, and limited organ coverage/populations.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - explanation: Uses public challenge/TCIA datasets with limited description of patient inclusion/exclusion and clinical diversity; cross-validation reduces but does not eliminate selection bias, and no external cohort is used.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - explanation: Outcomes (Dice, RDE) are computed consistently against provided ground truth; however, preprocessing choices (resampling, cropping) and the two-stage fine segmentation could differentially affect small organs, and no robustness testing across scanners/protocols is shown.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - explanation: Reports per-organ Dice and dose-error ranges and includes limitations; but lacks full reporting of some training details (e.g., batch size, augmentation, class weights) and does not provide full per-fold results or statistical significance.

detailed_extraction.title: A Method of Rapid Quantification of Patient-Specific Organ Dose for CT Using Coupled Deep-Learning based Multi-Organ Segmentation and GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo Dose Computing

method.architecture_name: 3D U-Net (residual, multi-scale outputs) + separate fine-segmentation networks for small/elongated organs
key_innovations: Streamlined pipeline coupling automatic multi-organ CT segmentation with GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo (ARCHER) organ dose computation
Two-stage approach: coarse multi-organ segmentation plus dedicated fine segmentation for esophagus and spinal cord using cropped higher-resolution volumes
Multi-scale output fusion (deep supervision-like) by producing probability maps at multiple decoder stages and upsampling/merging
loss_functions: Weighted Dice similarity coefficient loss (negative Dice; epsilon=1)
method.learning_rate: Initial LR=0.0005; halved if validation loss does not decrease for 30 epochs; early stop if no decrease for 50 epochs
method.epochs: Early stopping based on validation loss plateau (30-epoch LR schedule; stop after 50 epochs without improvement)

names: Lung CT Segmentation Challenge 2017 (LCTSC)
Pancreas-CT (PCT)
dataset.ct_scanner: Dose simulation uses a GE Lightspeed Pro 16 scanner model (for Monte Carlo dose computing); CT acquisition scanners for LCTSC/PCT not specified in this paper
dataset.slice_thickness: Resampling: LCTSC to 2x2x2.5 mm; fine segmentation to 1x1x2.5 mm; PCT resampled to 144x144x144 voxels (voxel spacing not specified)
dataset.image_size: PCT resampled to 144x144x144; patch sizes: LCTSC 96x96x96, PCT 128x128x128; fine segmentation patches 32x32x32

results.inference_time: Segmentation <5 s per patient; Monte Carlo dose computing (1e8 photons) <4 s per patient with <0.5% statistical uncertainty
results.gpu_memory: Segmentation GPU: 12GB (Titan X); dose GPU: 24GB (Titan RTX)

comparison_results.LCTSC_7_contestants_summary: Compared against 7 LCTSC contestant methods on 12 test cases; our method comparable for lungs/heart/spinal cord; esophagus better than all 7 contestants but below reference.

implementation.gpu: Nvidia GeForce Titan X (12GB) for segmentation; Nvidia Titan RTX (24GB) for ARCHER dose computing (reported in efficiency section)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates a fast end-to-end workflow that couples 3D deep-learning multi-organ CT segmentation with GPU-accelerated Monte Carlo dose computation to enable near real-time patient-specific CT organ dosimetry.
contribution_summary.novelty: Not primarily a new segmentation architecture; the novelty is the integrated, clinically-timed pipeline (seconds per case) combining automatic multi-organ segmentation (including a dedicated fine-segmentation step for small/elongated organs) with rapid GPU Monte Carlo organ dose calculation and showing reduced dose errors versus population-average phantom methods.
strengths: Uses public datasets (LCTSC, PCT) and reports per-organ Dice for 12 organs across thorax and abdomen
Clear description of preprocessing, patch-based training/inference, and a practical two-stage refinement for esophagus/spinal cord
Clinically relevant evaluation beyond segmentation: quantifies impact on organ dose estimation (RDE) and compares to phantom-based workflow
Very fast reported runtime (<5 s segmentation + <4 s Monte Carlo dose) with low Monte Carlo uncertainty (<0.5%)
weaknesses: No external validation on an independent cohort; generalization across institutions/scanners/pathologies is not established
Limited reporting for reproducibility (no batch size, no augmentation details, unclear weighting scheme details in weighted Dice)
Segmentation performance remains modest for difficult low-contrast/small structures (e.g., duodenum, esophagus)
No statistical significance testing or confidence intervals; abdominal SOTA comparisons are limited
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High potential relevance for CT dose reporting and prospective protocol optimization because it provides patient-specific organ masks and rapid Monte Carlo organ doses within seconds; however, clinical deployment would require broader validation across diverse scanners, protocols, and abnormal anatomies, plus robustness and QA procedures.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training uses >1000 expert-annotated CT volumes (well above >50 subjects criterion) plus an additional 50-volume independent test set.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Data described as collected CT volumes with varied protocols and an additional 50 volumes from clinical sites, but institutions/sites, acquisition details, and inclusion criteria are not fully specified; dataset is private.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Clear that 1000+ volumes are used for training and 50 unseen volumes for independent testing; however, no validation set description and no detailed split procedure (randomization, stratification) is provided.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States livers were delineated/annotated by human experts, but does not describe number of annotators, protocol, quality control, or inter-rater variability.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Uses an independent 50-case test set from clinical sites (external to training set), but still private and not a public benchmark; MICCAI-SLiver07 is discussed but not used for main evaluation.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: DI2IN generator and discriminator are described (encoder-decoder, skip/feature concatenation, deep supervision branches, bilinear upscaling, 3x3x3 kernels, LeakyReLU+BN, stride-2 downsampling; discriminator structure referenced in figure).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides optimizer (SGD), learning rate schedule, batch sizes, iteration counts, adversarial training schedule (kD, kG) and lambda; but omits key details such as momentum/weight decay, patching/cropping strategy (if any), initialization, and full discriminator layer-by-layer specification in text.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Defines segmentation loss (voxel-wise binary cross-entropy with deep supervision) and adversarial objective (GAN-style) with lambda; justification is mostly conceptual (higher-order potentials) without ablation/analysis of why BCE vs Dice/other losses.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Down-sampling to 3.0 mm isotropic is described; other common CT preprocessing steps (HU windowing/normalization, cropping, resampling method details) and augmentation are not described.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is provided.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice and average symmetric surface distance (ASD) with mean/std/max/median; also reports Dice min/median/std.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, or statistical significance analysis is reported.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against a prior learning-based method (Ling et al.) and internal baselines (DI2IN vs DI2IN-AN, 400 vs 1000+ training cases). Does not provide direct head-to-head evaluation against multiple contemporary public SOTA methods on a shared public benchmark.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ reporting is not applicable beyond liver-only; liver metrics are reported clearly.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses that CRF/graph-cut post-processing worsened results due to leakage on low-contrast fuzzy boundaries; however, broader limitations (generalization, annotation variability, failure modes) are not systematically analyzed.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient selection/inclusion/exclusion criteria and site distribution are not specified; dataset is private and may not represent the full clinical spectrum despite stated variability.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - outcomes are assessed with consistent metrics on an independent test set, but annotation protocol and consistency across annotators/sites are not described.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - reports key metrics and comparisons, but lacks statistical testing, detailed failure-case analysis, and evaluation on public external benchmarks; some implementation details (augmentation/normalization) are missing.

detailed_extraction.title: Automatic Liver Segmentation Using an Adversarial Image-to-Image Network

method.architecture_name: DI2IN (Deep Image-to-Image Network) + DI2IN-AN (adversarial training with discriminator)
key_innovations: 3D encoder-decoder segmentation network with multi-level feature concatenation (skip/bridge connections) and multi-branch deep supervision

Adversarial training using a discriminator on segmentation masks to encourage outputs closer to ground-truth distribution (higher-order shape/appearance constraints) without post-processing at inference
Demonstration of performance gains from scaling training data to >1000 annotated 3D CT volumes for liver segmentation
loss_functions: Voxel-wise binary cross-entropy (deep supervision branches + final output; weighted sum)
Adversarial loss term for generator (GAN objective; uses log(D(G(x))) variant)
method.optimizer: SGD (for DI2IN training; adversarial training alternates D and G updates)
method.learning_rate: 0.01 initially; divided by 10 after 100 iterations (for DI2IN pre-training)
method.batch_size: DI2IN: 4; adversarial training: D batch size 8 (kD=10), G batch size 4 (kG=1)
method.epochs: Not reported (iterations reported instead: DI2IN 200 iterations; adversarial training 100 overall iterations)

names: Private annotated CT dataset (1000+ volumes) for training
Private independent test set (50 volumes)
MICCAI-SLiver07 (mentioned; not used for main reported results)
dataset.total_subjects: 1050+ (1000+ train + 50 independent test; exact total not specified)
dataset.slice_thickness: Inter-slice distance varies from 0.5 mm to 7.0 mm (before resampling/downsampling)
dataset.image_size: Not reported; volumes down-sampled to 3.0 mm isotropic resolution

results.inference_time: < 1 second per case (GPU; discriminator not used at inference)

implementation.training_time: DI2IN pre-training: 200 iterations; adversarial training: 100 overall iterations (kD=10, kG=1). Wall-clock time not reported.

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a 3D adversarially-trained image-to-image segmentation framework (DI2IN-AN) for fast, accurate liver segmentation from CT without post-processing at inference.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines a 3D encoder-decoder with deep supervision and skip concatenation with GAN-style adversarial supervision on segmentation masks, and demonstrates benefits of training on an unusually large (>1000) expert-annotated CT dataset for liver segmentation (for its time).
strengths: Large-scale training dataset (1000+ CT volumes) relative to typical liver segmentation datasets in 2017
Adversarial training improves boundary/shape plausibility and reduces ASD/max errors compared to non-adversarial baseline
Very fast inference (<1s/case) since discriminator is not used at test time
Reports both overlap (Dice) and surface distance (ASD) statistics including dispersion and extrema
weaknesses: Private datasets limit reproducibility and comparability; no evaluation on public benchmark for main claims
No statistical significance testing or confidence intervals
Incomplete reporting of preprocessing (HU normalization/windowing) and data augmentation; no validation split described
No public code/model release
Limited failure-case analysis beyond noting CRF/graph-cut leakage issues
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Potentially high due to fast inference and strong accuracy on a diverse private multi-protocol dataset; however, lack of public external validation and limited reporting of clinical deployment considerations (robustness across scanners/sites, calibration, QA) reduce immediate translational confidence.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses 42 DECT torso volumes with ~30 for training per split (5:1:1). This is below the >50-subject training threshold, though cross-validation is performed.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset source and acquisition site are clearly stated: University Hospital Erlangen; Siemens SOMATOM Force dual-source DECT; adult patients with contrast agent details.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Split methodology is described (random 5:1:1 into 30 train / 6 val / 6 test) and 8-fold cross-validation is reported.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States ground truth was generated by experts 'in an inter-observer way' but does not detail number of annotators, protocol, adjudication, or quality control.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset; only internal cross-validation on the same 42 cases.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Describes a two-stage cascaded 3D U-Net (coarse ROI then fine segmentation) and multi-channel inputs, but lacks full layer-by-layer specification (filters, depths, patch sizes) beyond referencing Roth et al.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key training hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, batch size, epochs, augmentation, patch sampling) are not provided; relies on referenced implementation without sufficient details for reproduction.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Uses softmax with weighted voxel-wise cross-entropy; rationale is implicit via citation to prior work but not explicitly justified for class imbalance/organ sizes in this paper.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is clearly described: alpha blending of low/high kV to mixed image; binary body mask via skin-contour thresholding; stage-1 ROI mask used to constrain stage-2.

Q10_code_availability.justification: States use of an open-source implementation by Roth et al. (Caffe) but does not provide a direct URL for the exact code/config used for this DECT study.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Evaluation uses Dice only (via VISCERAL tool). No additional metrics (e.g., HD, ASD, precision/recall) are reported.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean, standard deviation, min, and max Dice across folds, but no confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical comparisons.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Provides a comparison table to SECT results from Roth et al., but explicitly notes datasets differ and results are not directly comparable; no direct baseline on the same DECT dataset.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Reports Dice per organ (liver, spleen, right kidney, left kidney) including summary statistics.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations and failure variability (e.g., one fold deviation, heterogeneous diseases, user-defined alpha, limited organs and small dataset) and suggests future improvements.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient inclusion is described broadly (adult patients with different indications) but selection criteria, exclusion criteria, and disease distribution are not defined; random split is mentioned but not stratified.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - outcomes are assessed with a consistent metric (Dice) and a standard tool, but annotation protocol details and blinding are not described; only one metric may mask boundary errors.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - per-organ Dice is reported with summary stats, but other relevant outcomes (HD/ASD, runtime, ablations, direct baselines on same dataset) are not reported.

detailed_extraction.title: Towards Automatic Abdominal Multi-Organ Segmentation in Dual Energy CT Using Cascaded 3D Fully Convolutional Network

method.architecture_name: Cascaded 3D U-Net (two-stage 3D FCN)
key_innovations: Application of a cascaded coarse-to-fine 3D FCN (3D U-Net) to dual-energy CT (DECT) abdominal multi-organ segmentation
DECT preprocessing via clinically used alpha-blended mixed images and study of alpha impact on segmentation accuracy
Transfer learning: fine-tuning a general SECT-trained multi-organ model for DECT segmentation
augmentation: Not specified (future work suggests using alpha as augmentation)

names: Clinical torso DECT dataset (University Hospital Erlangen)
dataset.ct_scanner: Siemens SOMATOM Force dual-source CT system (Stellar detector); 70 kV and Sn 150 kV with Sn filter
dataset.slice_thickness: 0.6 mm (z spacing reported as 0.6 mm)
dataset.image_size: 512x512 pixels; 992-1290 slices per volume; voxel size approx [0.6895-0.959, 0.6895-0.959, 0.6] mm

results.gpu_memory: 11 GB (GPU memory of GTX 1080 Ti)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a cascaded coarse-to-fine 3D U-Net approach with SECT-to-DECT fine-tuning for automatic abdominal multi-organ segmentation on clinical dual-energy CT.
contribution_summary.novelty: Early work applying cascaded 3D FCNs to DECT multi-organ segmentation, leveraging alpha-blended mixed images and analyzing how the DECT mixing weight (alpha) affects organ-wise segmentation performance.
strengths: Uses a clinically acquired DECT dataset with detailed acquisition parameters
Employs a proven cascaded 3D FCN design (ROI localization then refinement) suitable for large 3D volumes
Investigates the impact of DECT alpha blending on segmentation accuracy and notes organ-dependent optimal alpha
Reports per-organ Dice with variability statistics (SD/min/max) and cross-validation
weaknesses: Small dataset (42 cases) with no external validation
Limited reproducibility due to missing training hyperparameters and missing direct code/config link
Evaluation relies primarily on Dice only; no boundary metrics or runtime/inference reporting
No direct comparison to alternative methods on the same DECT dataset (only indirect SECT comparison)
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: demonstrates feasibility of automated DECT organ segmentation (useful for downstream DECT applications like context-sensitive reconstruction/material decomposition), but limited dataset size, lack of external validation, and incomplete reporting reduce readiness for clinical deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training set includes 340 CT volumes (>50), with 377 total subjects.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Source and acquisition context described: private clinical abdominal CT volumes in portal venous phase, pre-operative scans for gastric surgery planning (Aichi Cancer Center Hospital collaboration implied by affiliations).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: A random split of 340 training and 37 testing patients is reported, but no validation set is described and no details on randomization/stratification or multiple runs.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Ground truth creation described: manual establishment using semi-automated tools (graph-cuts, region growing) in Pluto software.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset is reported.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is identified as a 3D U-Net-type FCN (similar to Çiçek et al.) with 64×64×64 subvolume input and 8 output maps, but full layer-by-layer details (channels, depth, kernel sizes) are not provided in the text.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Some training details are given (Keras/TensorFlow, Adam optimizer, initial learning rates 0.001 and 0.01, 10,000 iterations, batch size 3 subvolumes), but key hyperparameters are missing (Adam betas/epsilon, weight decay, learning-rate schedule, initialization, exact sampling strategy, stopping criteria).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Dice-based loss is clearly defined (continuous Dice) and the study explicitly motivates/targets class-imbalance weighting variants (uniform, inverse frequency, inverse squared frequency) and analyzes their interaction with learning rate.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Downsampling by factor 4 and random cropping of 64×64×64 subvolumes are described; however, intensity normalization/windowing, resampling to isotropic spacing, and post-processing are not described.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is reported.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Only Dice similarity coefficient is reported; no additional metrics (e.g., HD95, ASD, precision/recall) are provided.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or variance estimates across subjects are reported.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares internal variants (loss weighting schemes and learning rates) but does not benchmark against external state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-class Dice scores are reported for background and each of the 7 organs.

Q15_limitations.justification: Limitations/discussion include: no data augmentation, sensitivity to learning rate/weighting interaction, possible non-convergence at 10,000 iterations for some settings, and suggestions for future improvements.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient selection criteria and inclusion/exclusion are not detailed beyond being portal venous phase pre-operative gastric surgery planning scans; random split is mentioned but no stratification or multi-center diversity is described.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - Outcomes (Dice) are assessed consistently on a held-out test set, but evaluation protocol details (e.g., full-volume reconstruction procedure specifics, any thresholding, handling of missing organs) are limited and no external validation is performed.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Per-organ Dice is reported, but only a single metric is provided and no statistical dispersion or failure-case quantification is included; post-processing and some training details are omitted.

detailed_extraction.title: On the influence of Dice loss function in multi-class organ segmentation of abdominal CT using 3D fully convolutional networks

method.architecture_name: 3D U-Net (3D fully convolutional network, U-Net-type)
key_innovations: Systematic analysis of multi-class Dice loss weighting schemes (uniform vs inverse frequency vs inverse squared frequency) for multi-organ 3D CT segmentation
Empirical demonstration of strong interaction between class-balancing weights and initial learning rate affecting convergence and final Dice
loss_functions: Multi-class continuous Dice loss (L_MD) with class-frequency-based weights: uniform (Wu), inverse frequency (Ws), inverse squared frequency (Wq)
method.learning_rate: Initial learning rate μ ∈ {0.001, 0.01} (no schedule reported)
method.epochs: Not reported (trained for 10,000 iterations)

names: Private clinical abdominal CT dataset (portal venous phase; pre-operative gastric surgery planning)
dataset.image_size: Each volume: 460–1177 slices; in-plane 512×512; volumes downsampled by factor 4; training crops 64×64×64

results.gpu_memory: 8GB (GTX 1080)

implementation.training_time: ≈2 days per model for 10,000 iterations

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that class-frequency weighting in multi-class Dice loss can substantially help or harm 3D multi-organ CT segmentation depending on the initial learning rate, highlighting a strong coupling between loss weighting and optimization settings.
contribution_summary.novelty: Rather than proposing a new architecture, the paper isolates and studies optimization behavior of weighted multi-class Dice losses in a 3D U-Net setting, showing that commonly used class-balancing weights may fail without appropriate learning-rate tuning.
strengths: Large private dataset for the time (340 training CT volumes) with multiple abdominal organs
Clear experimental factorization: three weighting schemes × two learning rates
Per-organ Dice reporting enables organ-specific interpretation
Ground truth process described (semi-automated tools with manual establishment)
weaknesses: No external validation and no comparison to other published segmentation methods on the same data
Only Dice metric reported; no boundary metrics or runtime/inference evaluation
Limited reproducibility details (no full architecture specification, missing several hyperparameters, no code release)
No validation set described; random split only, with no cross-validation or repeated trials
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: targets clinically relevant abdominal organ segmentation (including vessels) and uses real pre-operative portal venous CT; however, lack of external validation, limited metric reporting, and missing deployment details (inference time, robustness/failure analysis) reduce immediate clinical translation.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training set is 33 CTs total (25 training + 8 additional training), which is <50 subjects; additionally, some organs have fewer annotations (mandible 25, left SMG 26, right SMG 21).

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Uses the public PDDCA / MICCAI 2015 Head and Neck Auto-Segmentation Challenge dataset (v1.4.1) and describes origin (subset from RTOG 0522 trial) and imaging characteristics.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Clear split per challenge: 33 training images used for training; 15 testing images used for evaluation.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States all nine structures are manually segmented by experts for high quality/consistency; notes which structures are missing in some training cases.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset beyond the challenge test set.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: LocNet and SegNet are both 3D U-Nets; paper details layer operations (3x3x3 conv + BN + ReLU, 2x2x2 pooling, up-conv, skip connections), total conv layers, and binary output head.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides key settings (Adam with recommended parameters, cross-entropy, batch size 1, 200 iterations over training images, input sizes, resampling/cropping, bounding-box sizes, GPU). Missing learning rate value, weight initialization, augmentation details, and exact definition of '200 iterations over training images' (epochs/steps).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Uses cross-entropy for both stages but provides limited justification for this choice (no comparison to Dice/focal losses or class-imbalance discussion).

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Clearly describes resampling to 1mm isotropic, automatic cropping strategy with organ-group-specific margins, downsampling for LocNet, fixed bounding-box sizes per organ, and postprocessing steps.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release reported.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports DSC, 95% Hausdorff distance (95HD), PPV, and sensitivity (SEN).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean ± standard deviation across test cases, but no hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, or significance tests.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple MICCAI 2015 challenge methods (VU, UC, IM, UB) and also reports comparisons to Tong et al.; includes baseline strategies (joint localization, downsampling, sliding-window).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides per-organ DSC and 95HD (and PPV/SEN) in tables.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limited training data/overfitting risk, low CT soft-tissue contrast, localization errors propagating to segmentation, and memory constraints/downsampling effects; shows good vs bad qualitative examples.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - explanation: Uses a predefined challenge dataset (reduces investigator-driven selection), but the dataset itself is a curated subset from RTOG 0522 chosen for adequate image quality and minimal tumor overlap, which may limit representativeness; inclusion/exclusion criteria beyond this are not controlled by authors.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - explanation: Outcomes are assessed consistently using the same gold-standard masks and standardized metrics on the fixed test set; however, per-organ separate models and organ-specific bounding-box sizes could introduce organ-dependent performance differences by design.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - explanation: Reports per-organ metrics and comparisons; however, no external validation and no full disclosure of all training hyperparameters/augmentation may limit transparency; still, key outcomes (DSC/95HD) are comprehensively reported.

detailed_extraction.title: Organ at Risk Segmentation in Head and Neck CT Images Using a Two-Stage Segmentation Framework Based on 3D U-Net
detailed_extraction.venue: IEEE Access (as indicated by manuscript template; exact volume/issue not provided in text)
detailed_extraction.doi: 10.1109/ACCESS.2017.Doi Number (placeholder shown; exact DOI not provided in text)

method.architecture_name: Two-stage 3D U-Net (LocNet + SegNet), per-organ models
key_innovations: Two-stage decomposition: coarse localization (bounding box) followed by fine segmentation within localized target volume
Dedicated per-organ cascaded models (one LocNet + one SegNet per OAR) to simplify learning and mitigate memory/FOV constraints
Sliding-window maximization on LocNet output to robustly place a fixed-size bounding box
Empirical comparison against common large-volume handling baselines (downsampling-only, sliding-window segmentation, joint localization)
method.learning_rate: Not reported (uses Adam default/recommended parameters; exact LR not specified)
method.epochs: Not explicitly reported; training terminated at 200 iterations over the training images

names: PDDCA / MICCAI 2015 Head and Neck Auto-Segmentation Challenge dataset (v1.4.1)
dataset.ct_scanner: Not reported
dataset.slice_thickness: Inter-plane spacing 1.25–3.00 mm (before resampling); resampled to 1 mm isotropic
dataset.image_size: Original: 512x512 per slice; 76–263 slices. After resampling: in-plane 389–650, slices 226–416; then cropped to 384x384x224; LocNet input 96x96x56.

results.overall_dice: Not explicitly reported as a single mean across organs; best per-organ DSC reported up to 0.93 (mandible)
results.inference_time: ≈6 s per OAR per subject (≈2 s forward + ≈4 s LocNet postprocessing); ≈108 s for all 9 OARs
results.gpu_memory: Not reported

comparison_results.VU [34]: Reported per-organ DSC/95HD; generally worse 95HD than proposed method for 7/9 organs
comparison_results.UC [32]: Reported per-organ DSC only; generally lower DSC than proposed method for most organs
comparison_results.IM [33]: Partial metrics; proposed method competitive/better where comparable
comparison_results.UB [11]: Reported per-organ DSC/95HD; proposed method better 95HD for most organs
comparison_results.Tong [23]: Reported per-organ DSC/95HD; proposed method better 95HD for most organs, but Tong better for chiasm DSC/95HD in their table

implementation.training_time: ≈30 hours total to train 18 networks (2 per organ x 9 organs)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a per-organ two-stage 3D U-Net pipeline that first localizes each head-and-neck OAR with a learned bounding box (LocNet) and then segments within that ROI (SegNet), improving accuracy—especially boundary accuracy (95HD)—under GPU memory constraints.
contribution_summary.novelty: Compared to single-stage whole-volume segmentation or naive downsampling/sliding-window approaches, the paper operationalizes a learned localization-to-segmentation cascade with fixed-size organ-specific ROIs and demonstrates strong MICCAI 2015 challenge performance, particularly on 95HD for most organs.
strengths: Clear two-stage design addressing field-of-view and memory limitations in 3D CT segmentation
Strong boundary metric performance (95HD) on the MICCAI 2015 challenge test set; ranks first for 7/9 organs in 95HD per their comparison
Comprehensive per-organ reporting with multiple metrics (DSC, 95HD, PPV, SEN) and qualitative examples
Includes ablations/baselines for alternative large-volume strategies (downsampling-only, sliding-window, joint localization)
weaknesses: Small training set (33 CTs) with missing labels for some organs in training; no external validation
Per-organ training requires 18 separate models, increasing training/maintenance complexity and inference time for multi-organ use
Limited reproducibility details (no explicit learning rate, augmentation, or full training schedule); no code release
Cross-entropy loss without explicit handling/justification for severe class imbalance in small OARs (e.g., chiasm), where DSC remains low
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: The method targets radiotherapy planning OAR delineation and achieves competitive accuracy and reasonable runtime (~1.8 min for 9 OARs). However, lack of external validation, reliance on multiple per-organ models, and weaker performance on very small structures (notably chiasm DSC ~0.45) limit immediate clinical deployment without further validation and workflow integration.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: ADPK dataset has 203 CT volumes with kidney masks (used in 3-fold CV), exceeding >50 training subjects; auxiliary liver dataset has 176 CT volumes.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: ADPK dataset source is described as taken from Muto et al. (2017) clinical study; liver dataset described as a separate set of 176 abdominal CTs with liver masks; states no patient overlap.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Uses 3-fold cross-validation due to limited data; mentions ~1/4 used for validation, but does not provide exact fold composition counts or a held-out test set.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Ground-truth masks are referenced (kidney masks from prior clinical study; liver masks available), but the paper does not detail who annotated, protocol, inter-rater agreement, or quality control.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset; only internal 3-fold cross-validation on the ADPK dataset.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described in detail: shared encoder with task-specific decoders, conv block composition (two 3x3x3 conv + BN + ReLU), pooling, upconvolutions, skip connections, and rationale for increased depth/receptive field.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides optimizer (Adam), base learning rate (0.001), epochs (~100), voxel resampling (1.5 mm), cropping size (~144x250x250), and augmentation (rotation/scaling). Missing key details such as batch size, weight initialization, exact depth/channel counts, LR schedule, regularization, and patch sampling strategy.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Motivates dice loss for class imbalance and explains instability; proposes and formally defines bootstrapped cross-entropy (top K=10% hardest voxels) with rationale for inter- and intra-class imbalance.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is clearly described: resampling to 1.5 mm isotropic spacing, z-cropping to fit memory, and augmentation (rotation/scaling).

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is provided.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice for segmentation and also reports clinical metric: mean absolute percentage TKV error (3.86%).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance analysis across folds/methods.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against an implemented 2D FCN baseline based on Sharma et al. (2017) and against single-task 3D FCN; however, does not compare to multiple contemporary SOTA 3D methods beyond these baselines.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Reports mean Dice of left and right kidneys combined (mean of left+right). Does not provide separate left vs right Dice, nor per-structure results beyond kidney (liver task not evaluated in results table).

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses failure modes/limitations: cases with >5% TKV error (unique cases/small kidneys), difficulty distinguishing renal vs hepatic cysts, dice loss instability when crops contain no kidney voxels, and dataset size limitations.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient inclusion/exclusion criteria and cohort characteristics are not detailed; dataset is taken from a prior study but selection process for this work is not fully specified.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - evaluation uses internal cross-validation without a fixed held-out test set; cropping strategy may affect consistency, and details of how crops are selected at inference are not fully specified.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - reports Dice and TKV error and qualitative examples, but lacks full reporting of fold-wise variance, statistical testing, and does not report liver-task performance despite using it for multi-task training.

detailed_extraction.title: Computation of Total Kidney Volume from CT images in Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease using Multi-Task 3D Convolutional Neural Networks

method.architecture_name: Multi-task 3D FCN (3D U-Net-like)
key_innovations: Multi-task learning with shared 3D encoder and task-specific decoders to leverage an auxiliary liver segmentation dataset to improve ADPK kidney segmentation under data scarcity
Use of bootstrapped cross-entropy (hard-voxel mining; K=10% hardest voxels) as an alternative to Dice loss for class imbalance in 3D segmentation
loss_functions: Multi-task loss = mean of task losses (kidney task + liver task)
method.learning_rate: 0.001 (base learning rate; schedule not reported)
method.epochs: ~100 epochs (for multi-task: one epoch parses entire ADPK and liver datasets)

names: ADPK dataset (from Muto et al., 2017)
Liver dataset (institutional; details not fully specified)
dataset.slice_thickness: 0.5–5 mm (ADPK dataset; liver dataset slice thickness not reported)
dataset.image_size: Cropped volumes roughly z=144, y=250, x=250 after resampling to 1.5 mm isotropic

challenging_organs: ADPKD kidneys (large variation in size/shape/position; often non-contrast)
Kidney vs liver boundary when renal and hepatic cysts are in contact

implementation.gpu: Reedbush-L (SGI Rackable C2112-4GP3/C1102-GP8) HPC system; specific GPU model not reported

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a multi-task 3D U-Net-like FCN with a shared encoder (kidney ADPKD + auxiliary liver segmentation) and demonstrates clinically relevant TKV estimation accuracy from CT (mean absolute percentage error 3.86%).
contribution_summary.novelty: Uses multi-task learning across different organ datasets with inconsistent background definitions by splitting at the decoder, and evaluates bootstrapped cross-entropy (hard-voxel mining) as a simple alternative to Dice loss for 3D class imbalance.
strengths: Large ADPK cohort for this niche task (203 CTs) and clear motivation tied to clinical threshold (5% TKV change)
3D modeling substantially improves over 2D baseline (≈10% Dice gain)
Multi-task setup is practically motivated for data scarcity and shows qualitative improvements in difficult cases (renal vs hepatic cyst contact; contrast-enhanced kidneys)
Reports a clinically meaningful downstream metric (TKV error), not only segmentation overlap
weaknesses: No external validation; only internal 3-fold CV and no held-out test set
Limited reproducibility details (batch size, channel counts, exact depth, inference/cropping strategy, implementation framework)
No statistical significance testing or uncertainty estimates across folds
Liver-task performance is not reported, despite being central to the multi-task claim
No code/model release
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Potentially clinically relevant for ADPKD monitoring because mean absolute TKV error (3.86%) is below the 5% threshold cited for clinical decision-making; however, lack of external validation and incomplete reporting of failure rates in key subgroups limits immediate generalizability.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses 130 CT volumes with multi-organ labels for training/experiments (plus 10 manually labeled for lung/bone evaluation), exceeding the >50-subject criterion.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Source is clearly stated: anonymized CT volumes from the public LiTS challenge dataset; authors describe additional manual/semi-automatic labeling and some cropping to remove artifacts.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Mentions training/validation curves and a validation set, but does not clearly report the exact split sizes, selection procedure, or whether splits are patient-level and fixed.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual/semi-automatic annotation described for liver/kidney/bladder using ITK-SNAP; lung/bone labels largely generated by morphology with only 10 cases manually corrected for evaluation. Inter-rater info and detailed protocol are not provided.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Performs external evaluation on the LiTS challenge test set for liver segmentation (no additional training), reporting multiple LiTS metrics.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Provides a clear 3D GoogLeNet/Inception-style FCN description, including input/output sizes, decimation factor, lack of skip connections, and a layer table with filter sizes/strides and module sequence.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Gives many implementation details (resampling, crop size, augmentation types, batch size=4, inference tiling/averaging, hardware), but omits key training hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate schedule, epochs/iterations to convergence, weight decay, etc.).

Q8_loss_function.justification: IOU loss is mathematically defined, motivated for class imbalance, compared against weighted cross-entropy, and analyzed (false positive/negative penalty behavior; relation to Dice; family of IOU losses).

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing and postprocessing are described in detail: Gaussian smoothing + resampling to 3mm^3, random subvolume sampling for training, overlapping tiling with averaging for inference, nearest-neighbor resampling back; augmentation pipeline is specified.

Q10_code_availability.justification: Public code is provided for the GPU augmentation library (cudaImageWarp). The paper states the trained model and dataset 'will be made publicly available' but does not provide links for them in this version.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice for multi-organ experiments and multiple additional metrics on LiTS test set (VOE, RVD, ASSD, MSSD, RMSD, global Dice).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance analysis are reported.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against internal baselines (cross-entropy vs IOU; with/without augmentation) and against morphology labelers; provides contextual comparison to LiTS leaderboard but not a direct head-to-head reimplementation of SOTA multi-organ methods.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Reports per-organ Dice for lung, liver, bone, kidney, bladder (and 'other') across training schemes.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses limitations: morphology can outperform NN for distinctive organs; morphology can fail catastrophically; coarse 3mm^3 resolution and subvolume tiling; not top-tier on LiTS; no skip connections; general trade-offs.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient inclusion/exclusion criteria and exact split construction are not fully specified; some scans were cropped to remove artifacts, which may introduce unquantified selection/processing bias.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - outcomes are assessed consistently for the neural network, but ground truth quality varies by organ (manual/semi-automatic for some, morphology-generated for others) and only 10 cases have manual lung/bone evaluation labels.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - key outcomes (per-organ Dice, LiTS metrics) are reported, but missing statistical uncertainty and incomplete training hyperparameters may limit interpretability; dataset/model release is promised rather than fully documented here.

detailed_extraction.title: CT organ segmentation using GPU data augmentation, unsupervised labels and IOU loss

method.architecture_name: 3D GoogLeNet/Inception-style Fully Convolutional Network (FCN)
key_innovations: GPU-accelerated 3D data augmentation pipeline using GPU texture sampling + pipelined transfers
Weak/unsupervised label generation for lungs and skeleton using thresholding + morphology accelerated by 3D FFTs
Use and analysis of multi-class IOU (Jaccard) loss for class-imbalanced segmentation; comparison to weighted cross-entropy and relation to Dice
augmentation: Random 3D affine warping (rotation, scaling, shearing, reflection, generic affine) with trilinear interpolation for images and nearest-neighbor for labels
Random occlusion/cropping by zeroing a rectangular prism region
Additive Gaussian noise with per-example sigma sampled from a uniform range
Random CT window/level intensity mapping to [0,1] with random a,b limits

names: LiTS (Liver Tumor Segmentation Challenge) training data (used to build a 5-organ dataset)
LiTS challenge test set (external evaluation for liver)
dataset.image_size: Original CTs vary; resampled to 3 mm^3. Network input subvolume: 120×120×160 voxels at 3 mm^3.

challenging_organs: bone (skeleton) (too tedious to annotate; morphology used; NN underperforms morphology)
bladder (noted as particularly affected by cross-entropy boundary overestimation)

results.inference_time: 5.98 s per volume (LiTS liver test evaluation)
results.gpu_memory: 2429 MB (reported for LiTS liver test evaluation)

implementation.gpu: NVIDIA GeForce Titan X Pascal (4 GPUs used for augmentation speed experiments)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that a relatively simple 3D FCN can achieve competitive multi-organ CT segmentation when paired with fast GPU-based 3D augmentation, weakly generated labels for hard-to-annotate organs, and an IOU-based loss tailored to class imbalance.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines (1) a practical GPU texture-sampling implementation for 3D augmentation with pipelined transfers, (2) morphology/FFT-based unsupervised label generation for lungs and skeleton to expand training labels, and (3) a detailed motivation/analysis of IOU loss behavior versus weighted cross-entropy for imbalanced segmentation.
strengths: Addresses annotation scarcity via weak/unsupervised label generation for large structures (lungs, skeleton)
Clear, well-motivated augmentation pipeline and measured GPU speedups (2.6–8.1× vs SciPy CPU baseline)
IOU loss is explicitly defined, analyzed, and empirically shown to improve Dice and boundary tightness vs weighted cross-entropy
External validation on LiTS test set with multiple metrics and runtime/memory reporting
weaknesses: Incomplete reproducibility: key training hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, training duration) and exact data splits are not fully specified
Ground truth quality is heterogeneous (manual/semi-automatic for some organs; morphology-generated for others), and lung/bone evaluation uses only 10 manually labeled cases
Limited SOTA comparison for multi-organ segmentation (mostly internal baselines and morphology; LiTS comparison is leaderboard-context rather than controlled head-to-head)
Coarse 3 mm^3 resampling and subvolume tiling may limit fine boundary accuracy and small-structure performance
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: the method is fast (~6 s/volume for liver on LiTS) and memory-efficient, and multi-organ output is clinically useful for downstream tasks; however, coarse resolution, limited reporting of robustness across scanners/protocols, and mixed-quality labels (especially for bone/lung training) reduce immediate clinical readiness without further validation and standardized ground truth.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses KiTS19 with 210 training cases (and 90 held-out test in the challenge), exceeding the >50 training-subject threshold.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset source and provenance are described: University of Minnesota nephrectomy cases (2010–2018) as released in KiTS 2019.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Challenge split (210 train/90 test) is clear, and authors mention creating their own train/validation split from the 210 training cases, but exact split ratio/selection procedure is not specified.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Annotation process is described: transverse-plane annotation with longitudinal subsampling (~50 annotated slices per patient) and contour interpolation for excluded slices (per KiTS19 description).

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset beyond KiTS19 (test set is from the same challenge distribution).

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described in detail: asymmetric 3D encoder-decoder with residual blocks, bilinear upsampling, skip/add connections, and an attention-driven boundary stream; input/output and key layers are specified.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides several training details (crop size, sampling probabilities, optimizer Adam, LR schedule, epochs=300, batch size=8, resampling), but omits some reproducibility-critical specifics (e.g., weight decay, exact train/val split, random seeds, full augmentation list).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Losses are defined (Dice for main and boundary streams + weighted BCE for boundary) and imbalance motivation is given for BCE, but there is limited deeper justification/ablation for the overall loss design.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing and data handling are clearly described: intensity scaling/clipping, isotropic resampling to 1mm, crop-based training with biased sampling, and resampling back to original resolution.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is provided in the paper.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice-based metrics (kidney Dice, tumor Dice, composite Dice). No additional non-Dice metrics (e.g., HD95/ASSD) are reported.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance analyses are provided.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Mentions challenge ranking (9th among 100 participants) and provides portal 'approximate scores' for their submissions, but does not present direct quantitative comparisons against named baseline/SOTA methods in tables.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides separate results for kidney (foreground kidney+tumor) and tumor, plus composite average.

Q15_limitations.justification: No explicit discussion of failure cases, limitations, or error analysis.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient inclusion is defined by the KiTS19 cohort (nephrectomy cases at a single institution over 2010–2018), but the paper does not detail any additional inclusion/exclusion criteria for the authors' internal train/val split.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - outcomes are assessed consistently using the KiTS19 evaluation protocol (kidney Dice, tumor Dice, composite Dice) with standardized preprocessing and inference steps described.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - reports the main challenge metrics and final test-set scores, but lacks broader metrics, statistical analysis, and detailed failure-case reporting; also does not fully specify internal validation split details.

detailed_extraction.title: 3D Kidneys and Kidney Tumor Semantic Segmentation using Boundary-Aware Networks

method.architecture_name: Boundary-Aware 3D encoder-decoder CNN (U-Net-like) with attention-gated boundary stream
key_innovations: Dedicated boundary stream/branch to emphasize edge information for kidney/tumor segmentation
Attention-gated fusion in the boundary stream to compute boundary-focused feature maps
Edge-aware supervision via Dice loss on boundary outputs plus weighted BCE to address boundary/non-boundary imbalance
Inference improvements using test-time augmentation and 5-model ensembling
method.learning_rate: Initial LR 5e-5 with polynomial decay: alpha = alpha0 * (1 - e/Ne)^0.9
augmentation: Test-time augmentation (TTA) at inference (specific transforms not detailed)

names: KiTS 2019 (Kidney Tumor Segmentation Challenge)
dataset.image_size: After resampling to 1x1x1mm: ~512x512 axial, ~400–800 slices (inf-sup)

segmented_organs: kidney (kidney+tumor foreground in one output channel)
challenging_organs: kidney tumor (lower Dice than kidney; implicitly more difficult)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a 3D boundary-aware encoder-decoder segmentation network with an attention-gated boundary stream and edge-aware losses for kidney and kidney-tumor segmentation in CT.
contribution_summary.novelty: Adds an explicit boundary-focused decoding stream (with attention gates) supervised by edge-aware objectives (Dice + weighted BCE) to improve delineation of organ/tumor edges compared to a standard 3D encoder-decoder.
strengths: Strong performance on KiTS19 test set (kidney Dice 0.9742; tumor Dice 0.8103) with competitive challenge ranking
Clear description of core architecture components and training setup (crop size, sampling strategy, LR schedule, epochs)
Practical inference enhancements (TTA + 5-model ensemble)
weaknesses: No external validation beyond KiTS19; generalization to other institutions/scanners is untested
Limited evaluation metrics (Dice only) and no statistical analysis
No ablation studies or explicit failure-case/limitations discussion to isolate the benefit of the boundary stream/attention
No public code/model release, reducing reproducibility
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Relevant to preoperative planning and quantitative assessment of renal tumors; however, clinical translation would require validation on multi-center datasets, robustness analysis across acquisition variability, and reporting of boundary/shape metrics and failure modes.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training sizes are small for aorta and liver (10 training volumes per fold in 2-fold CV; total 20 each). Lung has 36 training volumes. None exceed >50 training subjects; however, multiple patch crops per volume greatly increase the number of training samples.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Public datasets are clearly named and cited: VISCERAL (aorta), SLiver07 (liver), and AAPM 2017 Lung CT Segmentation Challenge (lung).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Split methodology is described: aorta/liver use 2-fold CV with per-fold counts (10 train, 2 eval, 8 test); lung uses organizer split (36 train/24 test).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: No description of how ground-truth segmentations were produced (annotator expertise, protocol, inter-rater agreement). They rely on challenge/public labels without detailing annotation process.

Q5_external_validation.justification: They evaluate on multiple independent public datasets (aorta, liver, lung), which supports generality, but there is no true external validation for the same task/domain (e.g., training on one cohort and testing on a separate unseen cohort for the same organ) and no cross-dataset generalization experiment.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture and modifications are described in detail (anisotropic separable convs; ZU-Net/ZV-Net v1/v2; where 1D convs are inserted; instance normalization; patch strategy).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides optimizer (SGD momentum 0.9), learning-rate candidates and schedule, epochs (6/4), initialization, framework, GPU/CPU, and patch sizes/strides. Missing key details such as batch size, weight decay, exact LR chosen per experiment, and some implementation specifics (e.g., padding, sampling balance).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Uses softmax cross-entropy and defines it; however, there is limited justification for choosing CE over Dice/IoU losses given class imbalance (a central motivation).

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing and augmentation are described: per-volume max normalization to [0,1], patch generation (Patch-512 512x512x8 with stride 1 train / 8 test; Patch-128 baseline with specified strides), and rotations about Z.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is provided.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Primary evaluation uses foreground IoU (Jaccard) only; Dice, HD, ASD, etc. are not reported.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance analysis are reported.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against strong baselines (vanilla 3D U-Net and V-Net) and ablates patch strategy (training U-Net/V-Net on Patch-512). Does not compare to broader SOTA methods (e.g., nnU-Net, H-DenseUNet, DSN, etc.) on the same datasets.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Reports results per organ/task (aorta, liver, lung) with separate tables and fold-wise values for aorta/liver.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discussion mentions issues addressed and implies constraints (GPU memory, limited Z-depth patches, small datasets). However, limitations are not systematically analyzed (e.g., failure modes, robustness, generalization, annotation noise).

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient inclusion/exclusion criteria and cohort characteristics are not described; random shuffling is mentioned for 20-case datasets, but no details on representativeness or potential leakage beyond the stated splits.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - Evaluation uses consistent IoU metric and defined splits, but patch-based inference details (e.g., how overlapping predictions are fused, thresholding) are not fully specified; also different patching strategies between baseline and proposed method can confound performance attribution (partly addressed via Patch-512 ablation).
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Reports IoU and training-time/parameter counts, but omits common complementary metrics (Dice/HD), lacks statistical testing, and does not provide full hyperparameter selections (e.g., final LR, batch size), which may selectively emphasize favorable outcomes.

detailed_extraction.title: Z-Net: an Anisotropic 3D DCNN for Medical CT Volume Segmentation

method.architecture_name: Z-Net framework integrated into 3D U-Net and V-Net (ZU-Net, ZV-Net; v1 and v2 variants)
key_innovations: Replace isotropic 3D convolutions with anisotropic spatial separable convolutions: 2D conv in XY (e.g., 3x3x1 or 5x5x1) plus 1D conv along Z (1x1xD) to capture inter-slice context with reduced memory/compute
Patch strategy that preserves full XY field-of-view: crop only along Z to 512x512x8 (Patch-512) to reduce discontinuities and class imbalance from small isotropic patches
Two integration modes: (v1) replace each 3D conv with 2D+1D; (v2) use 2D convs and insert a single 1D conv before each down/up-sampling block (often better and lighter)
method.learning_rate: Tested {0.1, 0.05, 0.01, 0.005}; choose best. Schedule: halve after first epoch; if training >4 epochs, further divide by 10.
method.epochs: Aorta: 6 epochs; Liver: 4 epochs; Lung: 4 epochs
augmentation: Rotations about Z axis: aorta/liver 90/180/270 degrees; lung 180 degrees

names: VISCERAL (aorta)
SLiver07 (liver)
Lung CT Segmentation Challenge 2017 / AAPM 2017 thoracic autosegmentation challenge (lung)
dataset.train_subjects: Aorta: 10 per fold; Liver: 10 per fold; Lung: 36
dataset.val_subjects: Aorta: 2 per fold (evaluation set); Liver: 2 per fold (evaluation set); Lung: not specified (no explicit validation set reported)
dataset.test_subjects: Aorta: 8 per fold; Liver: 8 per fold; Lung: 24
dataset.image_size: Typical CT volume size described as 512x512xL with L>400; proposed training patches are 512x512x8 (Patch-512); baseline patches 128x128x64 (Patch-128)

challenging_organs: aorta (explicitly discussed as small target with severe class imbalance under small-patch training)

implementation.training_time: Seconds per 100 iterations (Table II): 3D U-Net 70.36s; ZU-Net v1 67.97s; ZU-Net v2 67.02s; V-Net (3 down-sampling) 157.4s; ZV-Net v1 82.08s; ZV-Net v2 80.11s.

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces Z-Net, a framework that decomposes 3D convolutions into anisotropic 2D (XY) + 1D (Z) operations and pairs it with full-FOV XY patching (512x512x8) to improve CT organ segmentation while reducing parameters and training time.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines (i) anisotropic spatial separable convolution design tailored to CT anisotropy and memory limits with (ii) a patching strategy that preserves full axial field-of-view (cropping only along Z) to mitigate discontinuities and class imbalance typical of small isotropic 3D patches.
strengths: Clear architectural idea with two integration modes and ablation comparing patching effects (Patch-128 vs Patch-512 and baselines trained on Patch-512)
Demonstrates consistent IoU gains, especially for small structures (aorta), and reports parameter and training-time reductions
Uses multiple public datasets across different organs, supporting broader applicability
weaknesses: Small training cohorts for aorta and liver (10 cases per fold) and no description of ground-truth annotation process
Evaluation limited mainly to IoU without Dice/HD and without statistical significance testing
No comparison to broader contemporary SOTA segmentation pipelines (e.g., nnU-Net) and no public code release
Post-processing and inference stitching/aggregation details are not fully specified, which affects reproducibility
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Potentially useful for intra-operative navigation and pre-operative planning where fast, memory-efficient 3D segmentation is needed; however, clinical readiness is limited by small training cohorts, lack of external clinical validation for each organ, and limited reporting of robustness/failure modes.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training sets are small: CHAOS CT uses 20 training subjects; 3D-IRCADb1 uses 10 training subjects. Both are <50.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Both datasets are clearly identified and referenced: CHAOS CT (grand-challenge/Zenodo) and 3D-IRCADb-01 (IRCAD). Basic acquisition stats are provided (Table 1).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Splits are explicitly described: CHAOS has official 20 train / 20 test; 3D-IRCADb1 is split 10 train / 10 test.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Ground truth availability is described (CHAOS provides training labels; test labels hidden; 3D-IRCADb1 labels created by clinical experts). However, detailed annotation protocol (number of annotators, inter-rater agreement, QA) is not provided.

Q5_external_validation.justification: They evaluate on two public datasets with different characteristics (healthy vs mostly tumorous livers), which provides cross-dataset evidence. However, there is no explicit 'train on A, test on independent B' external validation; each dataset is trained and tested within itself.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architectures are named and sourced (NiftyNet U-Net/V-Net/DenseVNet; original DeepMedic) and ensemble combiners are mathematically defined. But full architectural/training details (depth, channels, patch sizes, etc.) are not fully specified in the paper (delegated to external implementations).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: General experimental protocol is described (train 4 models, smooth probability maps, apply combiners, threshold at 0.5, evaluate). However, key training hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, epochs, batch size, augmentation) are not reported, limiting full reproducibility.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Losses are partially specified (U-Net cross-entropy; DenseVNet probabilistic Dice; V-Net uses its own loss; DeepMedic not specified). There is no strong justification beyond using default/vanilla settings.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Post-processing is clearly described (3x3x3 smoothing; thresholding). Preprocessing of CT intensities/resampling/cropping is not described; only a prior foreground probability is computed for the product rule.

Q10_code_availability.justification: Code for the ensemble pipeline is publicly available (GitHub). Metric code is also provided (separate GitHub).

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses four metrics: Dice, RAVD, ASSD, MSSD.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Performs statistical testing with normality checks (Lilliefors) and paired t-test or Wilcoxon signed-rank; reports win/loss/no-difference tables.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple strong baseline architectures (U-Net, V-Net, DenseVNet, DeepMedic) and shows ensembles outperform components. However, it does not benchmark against contemporary SOTA challenge-winning methods (e.g., nnU-Net, LiTS winners) on the same datasets.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Only liver is segmented, so per-organ reporting is not applicable beyond liver; results are reported per dataset and per method, but not per sub-structure.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses overfitting on small datasets, calibration sensitivity for heterogeneous ensembles, and computational considerations; notes no calibration was performed and suggests future work.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient selection criteria are not detailed beyond using the public datasets (CHAOS healthy livers; 3D-IRCADb1 includes many tumorous cases). No inclusion/exclusion criteria or demographic breakdown are provided; 3D-IRCADb1 split is an author-defined 10/10 without stating randomization/stratification.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Outcomes are assessed consistently using the same metrics and protocol across methods. However, CHAOS test evaluation is via an online server; repeated submissions could enable implicit tuning ('peeking'), though authors state they did not tune on test results.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low - They report multiple metrics, training and test results, and provide full result tables via GitHub. Some training details are missing, but outcome reporting appears comprehensive for the stated experiments.

detailed_extraction.title: Basic Ensembles of Vanilla-Style Deep Learning Models Improve Liver Segmentation From CT Images

method.architecture_name: Heterogeneous ensemble of 3D CNN segmenters (3D U-Net, DeepMedic, V-Net, Dense V-Networks) with non-trainable fusion (majority vote/average/product/min-max)
key_innovations: Demonstrates that a simple 'vanilla' heterogeneous ensemble of off-the-shelf 3D segmentation networks (no bespoke tuning) improves liver CT segmentation over individual models
Systematic comparison of basic non-trainable ensemble combiners (majority vote, average, product with prior, min/max) for 3D liver segmentation
Shows ensembles reduce (but do not eliminate) overfitting relative to individual models on small 3D datasets
Includes statistical testing framework to compare ensembles vs individual models across multiple metrics
loss_functions: V-Net loss (as implemented in NiftyNet; not detailed in paper)

names: CHAOS (CT task)
3D-IRCADb-01 (3D-IRCADb1)
dataset.slice_thickness: CHAOS: 2.00–3.20 mm; 3D-IRCADb1: 1.60–4.00 mm
dataset.image_size: In-plane 512x512; slices per volume CHAOS: 78–294 (avg 160); 3D-IRCADb1: 74–260 (avg 141)

challenging_organs: Region around vena cava superior (illustrated as difficult sub-region)

results.overall_dice: Best reported test Dice: 0.953 (CHAOS CT test, Average ensemble); 0.920 (3D-IRCADb1 test, Average ensemble)

implementation.framework: NiftyNet (TensorFlow-based) for U-Net/V-Net/DenseVNet; DeepMedic framework for DeepMedic; MATLAB for smoothing (smooth3) and metric computation (also provides MATLAB/Python/Julia metric code)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Shows that a simple, non-trainable heterogeneous ensemble of off-the-shelf 3D CNN segmenters improves liver segmentation from CT over individual models on two public datasets, with statistical significance across multiple metrics.
contribution_summary.novelty: Rather than proposing a new architecture, the paper emphasizes reproducible 'vanilla' deployment: using default implementations of multiple established 3D segmenters and combining them with basic, non-trainable fusion rules to obtain consistent gains and reduced overfitting on small datasets.
strengths: Reproducibility-oriented: relies on public implementations and releases code
Evaluates on two public datasets with different characteristics (healthy vs many tumorous cases)
Uses multiple complementary metrics (overlap, volume, surface distances)
Includes statistical testing to support claims of improvement
Clear description of ensemble fusion rules and post-processing
weaknesses: Small training sizes (20 and 10 subjects) limit generalizability and increase variance
Training hyperparameters and preprocessing details are insufficiently reported (hard to fully reproduce training)
No explicit external validation protocol (e.g., train on one dataset, test on another) and no multi-center independent cohort
Limited scope: single-organ (liver) segmentation only; no analysis of lesion/tumor segmentation
No runtime/inference cost reporting, which is important for ensemble practicality
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: liver segmentation is clinically relevant and the method is easy to deploy (black-box models + simple fusion), but lack of robust external multi-center validation, limited reporting of preprocessing/training details, and missing runtime analysis constrain immediate clinical translation.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses 131 labeled CT scans (MSD Liver) for training/validation via 5-fold CV (≈104–105 train per fold) and additionally 1277 unlabeled CT scans from MSD tasks; training size clearly >50.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset sources are clearly identified and described: MSD Liver (multi-site, multiple countries, pathology details) and BTCV challenge test set (VUMC; voxel size ranges provided).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: 5-fold cross-validation described with explicit counts per fold (105/26; last fold 104/27), fixed validation composition across experiments; separate external test set of 30 BTCV scans.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Uses challenge-provided liver annotations (MSD and BTCV), but the paper does not detail who annotated, inter-rater variability, or annotation protocol beyond dataset references.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Yes—final evaluation includes an independent dataset (BTCV, N=30) reserved for evaluation of final models.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: F-Net multi-resolution input pyramid and pathways are described; WTA-CAE encoder/decoder and sparsity (top-5 activations) are specified; S-TL and S-MTL integration (scaling factor, tied weights) is detailed.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides optimizer (Adam), LR (3e-4 baseline; 8e-5 for fine-tuning), weight decay (3e-5), initialization (Xavier normal gain 0.01), LR scheduling/early stopping rules, epoch definitions, patch overlap, sampling strategy, and key hyperparameter choices (kernel size, patch size, batch sizes).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Losses are specified (Dice+CE for segmentation; Huber for reconstruction) and compared against focal/L2 with empirical justification, but lacks deeper theoretical justification for the final choice beyond performance/stability.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is clearly described: crop to non-zero region, resample to median spacing (isotropic to largest median axis), percentile-based clipping using foreground intensities, z-score normalization; inference aggregation and thresholding are also described.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is provided in the paper.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Evaluation uses Dice similarity coefficient (DSC) only; no additional metrics (e.g., HD95, ASSD) are reported.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean ± standard deviation and boxplots across folds/datasets, but no hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, or significance analysis.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against a strong internal baseline (F-Net from scratch) and ablations (S-TL vs S-MTL; fine-tuning schemes; unlabeled ratios), but does not benchmark against external SOTA methods on the same test set (e.g., nnU-Net) beyond discussion.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Single-organ task (liver) so per-organ reporting is inherently satisfied, but no sub-structure or per-case stratification is provided.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses key limitations: potential bias from including validation images in WTA-CAE training, computational cost, outlier behavior at n=32, supplementary unlabeled data mismatch and sampling issues, and that tuning favored baseline F-Net.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Uses public challenge datasets with limited control/description of patient selection; inclusion criteria are not defined by authors beyond dataset descriptions.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - Validation images DV are sometimes included in WTA-CAE training (for S-TL/S-MTL), which can inflate validation performance; test set remains independent.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - Main outcome (DSC) is consistently reported across experiments with mean±SD; however, only one metric is reported and no statistical significance testing is provided.

detailed_extraction.title: 3D medical image segmentation with labeled and unlabeled data using autoencoders at the example of liver segmentation in CT images

method.architecture_name: 3D F-Net + Winner-Take-All Convolutional Autoencoder (WTA-CAE) with Self-supervised Transfer Learning (S-TL) and Self-supervised Multi-task Learning (S-MTL)
key_innovations: Adapts Winner-Take-All convolutional autoencoder training to 3D CT volumes (top-5 activations per feature map) to learn sparse features from unlabeled data.

Two integration strategies for unlabeled data in 3D segmentation: (i) S-TL weight initialization of F-Net encoders from pretrained WTA-CAE encoders with magnitude scaling; (ii) S-MTL joint training with tied/shared encoder weights and combined loss.
Systematic study of performance vs. labeled set size and unlabeled:labeled ratio, including use of large auxiliary unlabeled CT datasets from other tasks where liver is visible.
loss_functions: Segmentation: Dice + Cross-Entropy (Dice-CE), L = L_Dice + L_CE
Autoencoder reconstruction: Huber (smooth L1) loss with δ=1
MTL combined: L_MTL = L_FNet + γ * mean(L_WTA-CAE)
method.learning_rate: Initial LR 3e-4 (baseline F-Net); fine-tuning LR 8e-5 for transferred layers; LR reduced by 20% on plateau using moving-average criterion until min 1e-7
method.batch_size: F-Net: 8 (baseline; patch size 72^3); WTA-CAE: 12; S-MTL constrained to F-Net batch 5 and WTA-CAE batch 8 due to GPU memory
method.epochs: F-Net: up to 1000 epochs (each epoch = 15 train minibatch iterations + 8 validation iterations) with early stopping; WTA-CAE: up to 500 epochs (epoch = 15 minibatches)
augmentation: Tested but not used in final experiments: random translation (≤10 px), rotation (≤15°), scaling (log scale factor in [-0.2, 0.2]); omitted due to minimal gains and +30% training time
