names: Medical Segmentation Decathlon (MSD) Liver (Task 03) - labeled training/validation
Beyond the Cranial Vault (BTCV) challenge - labeled external test
MSD supplementary unlabeled tasks: lung, pancreas, hepatic vessel, spleen, colon (train+test) - unlabeled
dataset.total_subjects: Labeled: 161 (131 MSD + 30 BTCV). Unlabeled supplementary: 1277 (plus unlabeled portion of MSD liver training depending on n).
dataset.train_subjects: Per CV fold: 104–105 labeled (from MSD liver); plus unlabeled UT_r = (105-n) from MSD liver fold and optionally +1277 supplementary unlabeled
dataset.val_subjects: Per CV fold: 26 (last fold 27) from MSD liver
dataset.ct_scanner: Not specified (BTCV described as various clinical scanners at VUMC; MSD from numerous clinical sites)
dataset.image_size: Not explicitly reported; patch-based training/inference with target patch size 72^3 and 4-resolution pyramid inputs

results.overall_dice: Best reported mean DSC on external test (BTCV): 0.908 (S-MTL, n=105 with supplementary unlabeled data). Without supplementary unlabeled data: up to 0.897 (S-MTL, n=105).

dice_per_organ.liver: Range across settings; e.g., BTCV test mean DSC: baseline 0.884 (n=105), S-TL 0.896 (n=105), S-MTL 0.897 (n=105); with supplementary unlabeled at n=105: S-TL 0.902, S-MTL 0.908

comparison_results.Baseline F-Net (from scratch): BTCV test mean DSC 0.707 (n=2) to 0.892 (n=32) to 0.884 (n=105)
comparison_results.S-TL (pretrain WTA-CAE, transfer + fine-: BTCV test mean DSC 0.741 (n=2), 0.897 (n=32), 0.896 (n=105); with supplementary unlabeled: 0.901 (n=32), 0.902 (n=105)
comparison_results.S-MTL (tied encoders, joint loss; best w: BTCV test mean DSC 0.828 (n=4), 0.883 (n=32), 0.897 (n=105); with supplementary unlabeled: 0.890 (n=32), 0.908 (n=105)

implementation.training_time: Approx. per model (single fold): baseline ~26h; S-TL ~26h plus WTA-CAE pretraining; S-MTL ~39h. Full 5-fold runs: baseline ~80h; S-TL ~130h; S-MTL ~195h.

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates that self-supervised feature learning from unlabeled 3D CT volumes via a sparse WTA convolutional autoencoder can modestly improve 3D liver segmentation when integrated through transfer learning or multi-task learning with an F-Net backbone.
contribution_summary.novelty: Applies and adapts Winner-Take-All CAE sparsity to 3D CT and evaluates two concrete integration mechanisms (scaled-weight transfer initialization and tied-weight multi-task training) under controlled labeled/unlabeled ratio experiments with external testing.
strengths: Uses an external independent test set (BTCV) in addition to cross-validation on MSD liver.
Clear methodological description of preprocessing, patch-based inference aggregation, training schedules, and ablation studies (fine-tuning depth, MTL resolution levels, unlabeled ratio).
Systematic exploration of labeled set sizes (n=2 to 105) and effect of adding large-scale unlabeled data (1277 scans).
Reports variability (mean±SD, boxplots) and discusses computational trade-offs between S-TL and S-MTL.
weaknesses: Single evaluation metric (DSC) only; no boundary metrics (HD95/ASSD) or clinical error characterization.
Potential validation leakage: validation images DV are included in WTA-CAE training for some settings, which can bias validation comparisons (though test set remains independent).
No comparison against contemporary SOTA segmentation frameworks on the same evaluation protocol (e.g., nnU-Net) beyond narrative discussion.
No public code/model release, limiting reproducibility despite detailed hyperparameters.
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: liver segmentation is clinically relevant, and leveraging unlabeled data is practical in real deployments; however, gains are modest (up to ~0.02–0.04 DSC depending on setting), evaluation is limited to DSC without boundary/robustness metrics, and computational cost is high, so additional validation and efficiency work would be needed for clinical translation.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: 120 patients total; training uses 90 labeled cases per fold (10-fold CV on 100 train/val cases) which exceeds >50 subjects for training.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Single-institution retrospective radiotherapy planning dataset from Washington University in St. Louis; IRB approval stated; scanner model and acquisition details provided.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Clear split: 120 cases -> 100 train/validation and 20 held-out test; 10-fold cross-validation on the 100 train/val (90 train, 10 val per fold). Semi-supervised configurations (60 labeled + 30 unlabeled; 90 labeled + 30 synthetic unlabeled) are described.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual contours used as ground truth; OARs drawn by the same technician; prostate contoured by two experienced radiation oncologists with consensus; CT fused with MRI for accurate prostate delineation; exclusion criteria (post-prostatectomy) stated.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset; all results are from the same institution with a held-out internal test set.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: S-net and D-net architectures are described (residual U-Net backbone, multi-scale pooling replacement, auxiliary classifiers, discriminator input design). PGGAN architecture and progressive training are also described.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key hyperparameters are provided (optimizers, learning rates, scheduler, batch size, iterations, loss weights, pretraining iterations, threshold T_semi). However, some details are missing for full reproducibility (e.g., exact layer/channel configurations for all blocks, weight initialization, exact augmentation probabilities, random seeds).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Losses are mathematically specified (adaptive weighted multi-class CE, adversarial BCE, semi-supervised pseudo-label loss with confidence threshold). Justification is partial: references prior work (Hung et al.) and ARPM-net weighting, but limited ablation/argumentation for specific choices (e.g., why T_semi=0.2 beyond citing prior work; why specific lambda values).

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing and inference tiling are clearly described: crop 512->384 then resize to 128x128, select middle 64 slices, train on random 16-slice subvolumes, inference by splitting into four 16-slice chunks and stacking. Traditional augmentations listed.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release reported.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses multiple metrics: DSC, AHD, ASHD, and relative volume difference (VD).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports p-values for comparison with ARPM-net (Table IV) and provides mean±std across folds; however, no confidence intervals and limited statistical testing across all comparisons.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against several baselines trained on their data (variants of residual U-Net) and discusses/quotes results from prior SOTA papers. Direct head-to-head comparison with external SOTA is limited because other methods were not evaluated on the same dataset (authors state lack of access to code/data).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ results are reported for all metrics across five organs (prostate, bladder, rectum, left/right femur).

Q15_limitations.justification: Explicit limitations discussed: adversarial training instability/mode collapse, delicate hyperparameter tuning, balancing S-net/D-net, and difficulty of generating annotated synthetic images.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - explanation: Patient cohort is described (intact prostate cancer planning CT; post-prostatectomy excluded) but broader inclusion/exclusion criteria and consecutive sampling strategy are not fully detailed; single-center retrospective selection may limit representativeness.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low - explanation: Outcomes (segmentations) are evaluated consistently using fixed metrics on a held-out test set and cross-validation; same preprocessing/inference strategy described for all models; however, semi-supervised settings introduce additional training data types (real unlabeled vs synthetic) which could affect comparability across experiments.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - explanation: Many outcomes are reported per organ and across multiple metrics, but some comparisons to external SOTA rely on reported numbers from other papers rather than re-evaluation on the same dataset; code not released, limiting verification.

detailed_extraction.title: Semi-supervised Semantic Segmentation of Prostate and Organs-at-Risk on 3D Pelvic CT Images

method.architecture_name: 3D Residual U-Net with auxiliary heads + adversarial discriminator; PGGAN for 3D CT synthesis
key_innovations: Semi-supervised adversarial segmentation for 3D pelvic CT using a discriminator confidence map to select reliable pseudo-label regions (thresholded) for unlabeled data
Discriminator input design uses voxel-wise product of CT volume and (predicted/GT) one-hot mask to avoid trivial discrimination on one-hot maps
Modified residual U-Net: replaces stride-2 max-pooling with multi-scale pooling layers (from ARPM-net) to reduce spatial information loss
Deep supervision via two auxiliary classifiers at intermediate scales (aux_2 and aux_4) whose upsampled outputs are weighted into the final prediction
PGGAN-based synthesis of additional unlabeled 3D CT volumes to augment semi-supervised training when real unlabeled data are limited
loss_functions: Adaptively weighted multi-class cross-entropy (voxel-wise) for labeled data
Adversarial loss (BCE) encouraging discriminator to classify predictions as real
Semi-supervised pseudo-label cross-entropy on voxels selected by discriminator confidence threshold (T_semi=0.2)
Discriminator BCE loss on real (CT*GT) vs fake (CT*pred) confidence maps
method.learning_rate: S-net: 5e-4 with polynomial scheduler (power=0.9); D-net: 1e-4 with polynomial scheduler (power=0.9)
augmentation: Random sampling along Z (random 16-slice subvolume selection)

names: Institutional prostate cancer radiotherapy planning pelvic CT dataset (Washington University in St. Louis)
dataset.ct_scanner: Philips Brilliance Big Bore 16-slice CT scanner (85 cm bore)
dataset.slice_thickness: 1.5 mm
dataset.image_size: Original: 512x512 per slice (0.97 mm spacing), 100-200 slices; processed to 64x128x128 per patient; training subvolumes 16x128x128

results.inference_time: ≈10 seconds per patient (100–200 CT slices) for five organs

comparison_results.3d_res_Unet (90 labeled): Prostate DSC 0.85; Bladder 0.95; Rectum 0.84; Femur_L 0.97; Femur_R 0.97
comparison_results.3d_res_Unet_aux (90 labeled): Prostate DSC 0.86; Bladder 0.95; Rectum 0.86; Femur_L 0.97; Femur_R 0.97
comparison_results.3d_res_Unet_aux_adv (90 labeled): Prostate DSC 0.87; Bladder 0.96; Rectum 0.87; Femur_L 0.97; Femur_R 0.97
comparison_results.3d_res_Unet_aux_adv (60 labeled): Prostate DSC 0.84; Bladder 0.92; Rectum 0.83; Femur_L 0.95; Femur_R 0.96
comparison_results.Our method semi-supervised (60 labeled +: Prostate DSC 0.86; Bladder 0.96; Rectum 0.86; Femur_L 0.97; Femur_R 0.97
comparison_results.Our method semi-supervised (90 labeled +: Prostate DSC 0.90; Bladder 0.96; Rectum 0.87; Femur_L 0.97; Femur_R 0.97

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a semi-supervised adversarial framework for 3D multi-organ pelvic CT segmentation that leverages unlabeled (real or GAN-synthesized) CT volumes via discriminator-guided pseudo-labeling to reduce annotation needs and improve performance.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines (i) discriminator confidence-map thresholding for semi-supervised 3D segmentation, (ii) a discriminator input formulation (CT × mask) to prevent trivial discrimination, and (iii) PGGAN-based 3D CT synthesis to supply additional unlabeled data, within a modified residual 3D U-Net with multi-scale pooling and auxiliary deep supervision.
strengths: Clear internal evaluation protocol with held-out test set and 10-fold cross-validation on train/val
Per-organ reporting across multiple clinically relevant metrics (DSC, AHD, ASHD, VD)
Demonstrates reduced reliance on labeled data (60 labeled + 30 unlabeled achieves near 90-labeled performance)
Single-network end-to-end multi-organ segmentation (avoids multi-network ROI pipelines)
Fast inference (~10 s/patient) suitable for radiotherapy workflow support
weaknesses: No external validation; single-center dataset limits generalizability
No public code/model; partial reproducibility
Comparisons to some SOTA methods are not on the same dataset (relies on reported numbers)
Adversarial training stability and hyperparameter sensitivity acknowledged; may hinder deployment robustness
Synthetic data are unlabeled; paper notes difficulty of generating paired synthetic images and annotations
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High potential for radiotherapy planning (prostate/OAR contouring) due to multi-organ output and fast inference; however, lack of external validation and potential domain shift across scanners/protocols mean additional multi-center testing and calibration would be needed before clinical adoption.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses LUNA16 with 700 CT scans for training in the main split (700 train / 176 test), which is well above 50 subjects.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Public datasets are clearly named and referenced: LUNA16 and VESSEL12 (Grand Challenge links provided).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Explicit split reported: 876 scans used from LUNA16 with 700 for training and 176 for testing; VESSEL12 (20 scans) used for testing. No validation set is clearly defined.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States datasets include segmented ground truth and notes issues (holes/nodules) in LUNA16 masks, but does not describe who annotated, protocol, or quality control.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Evaluates on an independent external dataset (VESSEL12) after training on LUNA16 (though external test set is small: 20 scans).

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described with encoder/decoder structure, RRCU/DRRCU modules, dilation, inception-like downsampling, filter configurations, parameter counts, and activation (sigmoid).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides framework (Keras/TensorFlow), GPU, learning rates, batch size=1, and a custom training strategy (5 scans for 5 epochs repeated 500 iterations). However, key details are missing/unclear (exact epochs for full training, weight decay, initialization, patching vs full-volume, exact optimizer settings beyond LR, early stopping, validation protocol).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Defines Soft-DSC and Exponential Logarithmic Loss with weights and gamma; rationale is implied (class imbalance) but not deeply justified or ablated.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Clearly describes resampling to fixed 256x512x512 by repeating/selecting slices along z-axis and normalizing each scan to [0,1]. No post-processing used for proposed method.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model link provided.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports DSC and Soft-DSC (two closely related overlap metrics). No boundary/distance metrics (e.g., HD95/ASSD) or volumetric error metrics are reported.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance analysis reported.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against V-Net and Extended V-Net with reported Soft-DSC on VESSEL12; also discusses training size differences.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Single-organ task (lungs); results are organ-specific by definition (lung Dice/Soft-DSC).

Q15_limitations.justification: Explicitly discusses limited generalization due to small external test set and failure on COVID-19 infected lungs when trained on LUNA16.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Uses public datasets with predefined inclusion, but the paper does not clearly define patient selection criteria beyond using 876/888 LUNA16 scans, and uses a nonstandard training selection strategy (randomly sampling 5 scans from first 100 repeatedly) that may introduce sampling bias.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - Evaluation is consistent within datasets, but resampling by repeating/selecting slices may alter anatomy; lack of a clearly defined validation protocol and limited external testing (20 scans) may inflate perceived performance.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Reports mainly Soft-DSC and limited baselines; lacks statistical analysis, boundary metrics, and detailed failure case quantification, which may omit relevant outcomes.

detailed_extraction.title: R2U3D: Recurrent Residual 3D U-Net for Lung Segmentation

method.architecture_name: R2U3D (Recurrent Residual 3D U-Net); variant with DRRCU (Dynamic-Recurrent Residual Convolutional Unit)
key_innovations: Recurrent Residual Convolutional Unit (RRCU) integrated into a 3D U-Net for volumetric lung segmentation
Dynamic-Recurrent Residual Convolutional Unit (DRRCU) with variable recurrent depth per level to better utilize compute/memory
Integration of Squeeze-and-Excitation residual module inside DRRCU to model channel interdependencies
Inception-like strided convolution down-sampling module (multi-kernel) as an alternative to max-pooling for early downsampling/contrast stretching
Use of dilation in encoder and recurrent units
loss_functions: Exponential Logarithmic Loss (ELL): (-ln(DSC))^gamma + weighted cross-entropy with logits (wDSC=0.8, wWCEL=0.2, gamma=0.3)
method.learning_rate: 0.001 for 400 iterations then 0.0001 for 100 iterations (as described for the iterative training strategy)
method.epochs: Custom schedule: repeatedly train on 5 randomly selected scans for 5 epochs, repeated for 500 iterations (exact total effective epochs over full dataset not directly comparable/fully specified)

names: LUNA16
VESSEL12
dataset.total_subjects: LUNA16: 888 total scans (876 used); VESSEL12: 20 scans
dataset.image_size: Resampled to 256x512x512 (z,x,y) for all volumes

challenging_organs: lungs with infection/high attenuation patterns (discussed as challenging; COVID-19 infected regions cause failures)

results.gpu_memory: 12 GB (GPU: RTX 2080 Ti)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Proposes R2U3D, a recurrent-residual 3D U-Net (with a dynamic recurrent SE-enhanced variant) that achieves high lung segmentation accuracy on LUNA16 and external VESSEL12 CT datasets, including strong performance when trained with only 100 scans and no augmentation.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines recurrent residual units in a 3D U-Net with (i) a dynamic-depth recurrent design (DRRCU) and (ii) an embedded squeeze-and-excitation residual module, plus an inception-like downsampling block and dilation, targeting efficient volumetric feature propagation under compute constraints.
strengths: Clear architectural innovations (RRCU/DRRCU + SE) and parameter counts reported
External testing on VESSEL12 in addition to LUNA16
Demonstrates competitive performance even with limited training data (100 scans) and no augmentation
Acknowledges domain shift issues (COVID-19 infected lungs) and discusses generalization
weaknesses: No public code/model; limited reproducibility details (validation protocol, full training schedule, several hyperparameters)
Evaluation relies mainly on Soft-DSC; lacks boundary metrics (HD95/ASSD) and statistical testing
External validation dataset is very small (20 scans), limiting confidence in generalization claims
Preprocessing resampling by repeating/selecting slices may distort anatomy and is not compared against standard resampling/interpolation approaches
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Potentially useful as an automated lung mask generator to reduce downstream computation and annotation burden in CT workflows (e.g., nodule analysis), but clinical readiness is limited by lack of robust multi-center validation, limited reporting of failure modes on diseased lungs, and absence of deployment-oriented metrics (runtime, robustness, calibration).

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training datasets exceed 50 subjects for liver (245) and kidney (210); spleen dataset is smaller (41) but overall study uses 627 volumes across tasks.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Sources are clearly described: internal Zhejiang University hospital liver dataset; public MSD Spleen, KiTS19, and LiTS (external validation). Acquisition phases and some resolution details are provided.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Split methodology is clear via cross-validation: 5-fold for liver and kidney, 3-fold for spleen; fold sizes are reported.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Internal liver dataset states manual annotation by an experienced radiologist; public datasets imply provided annotations. However, detailed annotation protocol (number of annotators, inter-rater agreement, QA) is not described.

Q5_external_validation.justification: External validation performed for liver: models trained on internal liver dataset evaluated on LiTS (131 scans) with reported metrics.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: BA-Unet architecture is described with encoder/decoder units, convolution units, bottleneck blocks, attention blocks, pooling/upsampling, and final sigmoid output.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Training details include framework (PyTorch), GPU, cross-validation, label ensembling (EMA with alpha=0.1), loss formula, optimizers (Adam then SGD), learning rates and decay schedule, epochs (3+17), batch size (1), and BN freezing.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Dice loss is specified with formula and smoothing constant, but there is limited explicit justification beyond suitability for segmentation/overlap.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is detailed: organ-specific HU windows, clipping/normalization, slice selection, resizing to fixed shapes, bounding-box generation (+5 pixels), pseudo-mask generation via k-means fusion and morphological/CC filtering; inference thresholding is described.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release or URL is provided.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses multiple metrics: Dice (DSC), Jaccard, Recall, Precision; reports mean and standard deviation.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports variability (standard deviation across folds / cases) but no hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, or significance tests.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against baselines (BA-Unet trained on bbox labels, GrabCut, BA-Unet trained on GrabCut pseudo-labels) and discusses comparison to Ma et al. weakly supervised benchmark; limited direct comparison to broader SOTA fully supervised 3D methods on the same splits.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ results are reported (separate experiments for liver, spleen, kidney) including external liver validation; not a single averaged multi-organ score only.

Q15_limitations.justification: Explicit limitations discussed: blurred boundaries (e.g., liver) and boundary noise leading to false positives; notes room for improvement.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Internal liver cohort inclusion/exclusion criteria and patient selection process are not specified; public datasets are standard but internal dataset representativeness is unclear.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Outcomes are assessed consistently using provided ground truth and fixed metrics; however, preprocessing includes selecting only slices occupied by the target organ, which may simplify the task and could bias performance upward if not matched to real deployment.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Reports key overlap metrics and comparisons, but lacks full reporting of some potentially relevant outcomes (e.g., surface distance metrics, inference time, failure rate quantification) and no statistical significance testing.

detailed_extraction.title: Automatic CT Segmentation from Bounding Box Annotations Using Convolutional Neural Networks

method.architecture_name: BA-Unet (3D U-Net variant with bottleneck blocks and attention)
key_innovations: Pseudo-mask generation from bounding-box annotations using k-means clustering (multiple k values fused) plus morphological operations and connected-component filtering
Iterative/temporal label ensembling using exponential moving average (EMA) of model outputs to refine pseudo-labels during training
BA-Unet architecture: attention-gated skip connections that additionally consult features from other-dimensional encoders (pooled features / higher-level encoder features) during decoding
method.optimizer: Adam (first 3 epochs) then SGD with exponential learning-rate decay (remaining 17 epochs)
method.learning_rate: Adam lr=1e-4 for 3 epochs; then SGD initial lr=1e-3 with exponential decay (decay_rate=0.94, decayed_step=100)

names: Internal liver dataset (First Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine)
Medical Segmentation Decathlon (MSD) Spleen
KiTS19 (Kidney Tumor Segmentation Challenge 2019) - kidney organ
LiTS (Liver Tumor Segmentation Challenge) - external validation
dataset.slice_thickness: Internal liver: 5 mm constant; MSD spleen: slice distance 1.5–8 mm; KiTS: 0.5–5 mm; LiTS: z spacing 0.45–6.0 mm
dataset.image_size: Axial 512×512 for internal liver/MSD/KiTS; resized volumes: liver/kidney 40×512×512, spleen 31×512×512; KiTS and LiTS have variable slice counts before resizing/cropping

challenging_organs: spleen (small dataset size and noisy images)

results.gpu_memory: 24 GB (RTX 3090); batch size 1 due to memory limits

contribution_summary.main_contribution: A weakly supervised 3D CT organ segmentation pipeline that trains a 3D U-Net variant using only bounding-box annotations by generating and iteratively refining pseudo-masks.
contribution_summary.novelty: Combines (i) k-means-based pseudo-mask generation from bounding boxes (with multi-k fusion and morphology/CC filtering) and (ii) iterative EMA label ensembling, together with a modified attention 3D U-Net (BA-Unet) that consults additional encoder features during decoding.
strengths: Demonstrates strong segmentation accuracy from weak supervision across three organs
Clear preprocessing and training procedure with cross-validation and reported variability
Includes external validation (liver on LiTS), supporting cross-domain generalization
Provides baseline comparisons (bbox-only training, GrabCut, GrabCut pseudo-label training) showing benefit of proposed pseudo-mask + iterative refinement
weaknesses: No public code/model release, limiting reproducibility
Limited statistical rigor (no confidence intervals or significance testing)
Task setup includes extracting only slices occupied by the target organ and resizing to fixed depth, which may not reflect full-volume clinical deployment
No boundary/surface distance metrics (e.g., Hausdorff/ASSD) and no inference-time reporting
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Potentially useful for reducing annotation burden in CT organ segmentation workflows (bounding boxes are cheaper than voxel-wise labels), with promising accuracy and external validation for liver; however, deployment readiness is limited by lack of full-volume evaluation details, missing runtime analysis, and absence of prospective/real-world validation.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Total dataset is 60 kidney ROIs (30 train/30 test), but only 4 labeled training images are used for supervision; training size is adequate in total volume but labeled training subjects are far below >50.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Source is described as abdominal enhanced CT from patients undergoing laparoscopic partial nephrectomy (LPN) surgery at an affiliated hospital; however, it is a private dataset with limited acquisition details (e.g., scanner/vendor not provided).

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Clear split: 60 ROIs with half for training and half for testing; episodic training described (first 2000 iterations on labeled set, remaining iterations use unlabeled queries).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States that 4 training images and all 30 test images are 'fine labeled' for cortex/medulla, but does not describe annotator expertise, protocol, inter-rater agreement, or QA.

Q5_external_validation.justification: No evaluation on an independent external dataset; only internal test set from the same private cohort.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture and modules are described: Cycle Prototype Learning (forward/reverse), Bayes Weakly Supervised Module, and Fine Decoding Feature Extractor with encoder/decoder blocks, pooling, skip connections, group norm, and cosine-similarity prototype matching.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides key hyperparameters (SGD lr=0.001, momentum=0.9, batch size=1, 20k iterations, θ schedule, ω), ROI size, and episodic sampling; missing framework, weight initialization, augmentation, and some implementation specifics.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Uses cross-entropy for query and support losses and a weighted sum; rationale is partially explained (query learns from pseudo-labels; support enforces consistency), but no deeper justification vs Dice/focal/etc.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Reports ROI cropping to 160×160×200 and CT spacing/thickness; BWSM prior extraction uses filtering and subtraction between CTA/CTU. However, intensity normalization/resampling and postprocessing are not fully specified.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release reported.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice and Average Hausdorff Distance (AHD) for medulla and cortex.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean ± standard deviation for metrics, but no confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, or significance testing for comparisons.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against SegNet, U-Net, and PANet; comparison set is limited and not clearly tailored to 3D renal compartment segmentation SOTA (and baselines may not be optimized for the same setting).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides separate Dice and AHD for medulla and cortex.

Q15_limitations.justification: Discusses challenges (unclear boundaries, thin structures, anatomy variation, small labeled dataset) and reliance on weak supervision; limited explicit failure-case analysis.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - cohort inclusion/exclusion criteria and patient selection process are not defined; private single-source dataset with ROI-based sampling may not represent broader populations.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - evaluation is consistent on a fixed internal test set with labeled ground truth, but support set is drawn from the same 4 labeled training images and no external validation is performed; annotation protocol details are missing.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - key outcomes (Dice/AHD) are reported with per-class breakdown, but missing external validation, missing runtime/hardware/framework details, and limited reporting of failure modes may bias perceived performance.

detailed_extraction.title: CPNet: Cycle Prototype Network for Weakly-supervised 3D Renal Compartments Segmentation on CT Images

method.architecture_name: CPNet (Cycle Prototype Network) with FDFE backbone
key_innovations: Cycle Prototype Learning (CPL) with forward (support→query) and reverse (query→support) processes to enforce consistency regularization and improve generalization under noisy pseudo-labels
Bayes Weakly Supervised Module (BWSM) leveraging cross-period prior (CTA vs CTU) to generate/correct pseudo-labels via Bayes-style fusion of prior and network likelihood
Fine Decoding Feature Extractor (FDFE) encoder-decoder with skip connections to preserve fine-grained details for thin renal compartment structures
loss_functions: Total loss: L = θ * L_query + L_support (θ=1 during supervised initialization; θ=0.1 during pseudo-label training)
method.learning_rate: 0.001 (fixed, not reported as scheduled)
method.epochs: Not reported (20,000 iterations episodic training)

names: Private LPN surgery abdominal enhanced CT dataset with cross-period CTA/CTU
dataset.ct_scanner: Not reported
dataset.image_size: Kidney ROIs cropped to 160×160×200; in-plane pixel size 0.59–0.74 mm^2; z-spacing 0.5 mm

challenging_organs: kidney cortex (thin structures, unclear boundary with extra-renal tissues)

results.overall_dice: 0.787 (Avg Dice, CPNet-Total)
results.inference_time: Not reported
results.gpu_memory: Not reported

implementation.training_time: Not reported (20,000 iterations; episodic sampling; batch size 1)

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces CPNet, a weakly supervised 3D renal cortex/medulla segmentation framework that combines cycle-consistent prototype learning with Bayes-corrected pseudo-labeling from cross-period CT priors and a detail-preserving decoder.
contribution_summary.novelty: Novel combination of (i) forward/reverse cycle prototype learning for consistency regularization in 3D segmentation, and (ii) Bayes fusion of cross-period CTA/CTU-derived priors with network likelihood to improve pseudo-label quality, plus (iii) a decoder design emphasizing thin-structure detail.
strengths: Addresses label scarcity explicitly (only 4 labeled training ROIs) using weak supervision and pseudo-labeling
Per-compartment reporting (cortex vs medulla) with Dice and AHD
Ablation-style comparisons (CPNet-BWSM, CPNet-FDFE, CPNet-Total) showing module contributions
Uses cross-period imaging prior (CTA/CTU) in a principled fusion approach to reduce pseudo-label noise
weaknesses: Private dataset with limited acquisition/annotation protocol details; no inter-rater variability or QA described
No external validation; generalizability beyond the single cohort is unclear
Limited reproducibility details (no framework, no augmentation, no code release)
No explicit failure-case analysis or robustness evaluation across scanners/sites
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Potentially relevant for pre-/intra-operative planning in laparoscopic partial nephrectomy by delineating renal compartments, but clinical translation is limited by lack of external validation, private data, and incomplete reporting of deployment constraints (runtime, robustness across sites).

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Primary dataset has 240 training CT volumes (>50). MICCAI 2015 has 33 training cases (smaller), but the study includes a large 307-case dataset for development/ablation.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Sources are explicitly stated: TCIA Head-Neck Cetuximab (46) and McGill (261) for the first dataset; MICCAI 2015 Head and Neck Auto Segmentation Challenge dataset (48) for benchmarking.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: First dataset split is clearly reported (240 train / 20 val / 47 test, random grouping). MICCAI 2015 uses the provided split (33 train, 10 offset test for reporting, 5 onsite).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: First dataset: annotations for 24 OARs provided by experienced radiologists with quality control. MICCAI 2015: manual segmentations available for training and offset test sets.

Q5_external_validation.justification: They evaluate on a second public dataset (MICCAI 2015) in addition to their collected dataset, which functions as cross-dataset validation; however, it is not a true external validation where the model trained on one dataset is tested on a fully independent dataset without re-optimization (they state they optimize on MICCAI training and report on MICCAI offset test).

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described with three blocks (2D conv block, coarse 3D block with ResSE, fine 3D block with HDC) and an encoder-decoder structure; ablation variants are defined.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key training details are provided (PyTorch, Adam, batch size 2, input resize 256×256×48, LR schedule). However, several reproducibility details are missing (number of epochs/early stopping, weight decay, patch sampling vs full-volume memory strategy beyond resize, normalization/windowing, augmentation, exact HDC dilation rates/placement, random seeds).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss is clearly defined (focal + average Dice) with explicit formulas and weights; justification is mainly high-level (class imbalance) and largely follows prior work rather than providing deeper empirical justification.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is partially described: whole-volume input resized to 256×256×48. No details on HU clipping/windowing, resampling to physical spacing, intensity normalization, or cropping strategy; post-processing not described.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is provided in the paper.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses DSC and 95% Hausdorff distance (95HD).

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports statistical significance with paired t-tests on DSC values (p < 0.05) for ablation comparisons.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple baselines/variants and reports benchmarking against published results for MICCAI 2015 (AnatomyNet, FocusNet, SOARS, SCAA, and MICCAI 2015 best results).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides per-organ DSC for MICCAI 2015 (9 OARs) and per-organ DSC for 10 small organs on the first dataset; indicates full 24-organ results are in supplementary material.

Q15_limitations.justification: Partially discussed: acknowledges slightly worse mean DSC than SOARS and SCAA and notes those methods are more computationally intensive/complex. Limited discussion of failure modes, generalization, and clinical workflow constraints.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient inclusion/exclusion criteria and acquisition heterogeneity are not described; first dataset is a mix of two sources but selection process is unclear beyond random split.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - Outcomes are assessed with standard metrics, but details of consistent preprocessing, label harmonization across sources, and evaluation protocol (e.g., resampling effects) are limited; MICCAI comparison uses numbers copied from papers (not re-implemented), which may introduce comparability issues.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Per-organ results for all 24 OARs are deferred to supplementary material; limited reporting of runtime, failure cases, and negative results; no external generalization experiment clearly reported.

detailed_extraction.title: A Novel Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network for Accurate Organ Segmentation in 3D Head and Neck CT Images

method.architecture_name: OrganNet2.5D (U-Net-like encoder-decoder hybrid 2D/3D CNN)
key_innovations: Hybrid 2D+3D convolution design: 2D convolutions in early/late layers to mitigate anisotropic CT resolution effects on edge/low-level features, with 3D convolutions in middle layers for semantic context
Coarse 3D block augmented with ResSE (squeeze-and-excitation residual) units for feature recalibration
Fine 3D block using Hybrid Dilated Convolution (HDC) modules to enlarge receptive field while avoiding gridding artifacts
Reduced downsampling (two total: one 2D in-plane + one 3D) to preserve small-structure detail while still expanding receptive field via HDC
loss_functions: Combined loss: L = L_focal + lambda * L_avgdice (lambda=1.0)
method.learning_rate: Step decay: initial 0.001, reduced by 10× every 50 epochs until 0.00001

names: Head-Neck Cetuximab (TCIA) collection
Martin Vallières (McGill) head-and-neck dataset
MICCAI 2015 Head and Neck Auto Segmentation Challenge dataset
dataset.image_size: Resized to 256×256×48 (whole-volume input)

results.overall_dice: 84.6% ± 1.9 (first dataset, mean over 24 OARs); 81.3% (MICCAI 2015, mean over 9 OARs on offset test)
results.gpu_memory: 11G GPU used (GTX 1080Ti); memory consumption not quantified

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces OrganNet2.5D, a hybrid 2D/3D U-Net-like model with HDC-based receptive-field expansion to improve multi-organ (including small OAR) segmentation in anisotropic head-and-neck CT volumes.
contribution_summary.novelty: Unlike prior whole-volume 3D approaches that apply uniform 3D convolutions despite anisotropic spacing, the paper explicitly mixes 2D convolutions for edge/low-level features with 3D convolutions for semantic context, and replaces DenseASPP-style dilation with HDC to expand receptive field while mitigating gridding artifacts under limited downsampling.
strengths: Addresses anisotropic resolution explicitly via 2D/3D hybridization
Targets small-organ preservation by limiting downsampling and using HDC for context
Provides ablation study isolating effects of 2D block and fine 3D/HDC block
Uses two metrics (DSC and 95HD) and includes statistical testing (paired t-test)
Benchmarks on MICCAI 2015 challenge dataset with per-organ reporting
weaknesses: No public code/model; incomplete reproducibility details (epochs, augmentation, intensity preprocessing, exact HDC configuration)
Limited discussion of failure cases and practical deployment considerations (runtime, robustness across scanners/protocols)
External generalization is not cleanly demonstrated (MICCAI results involve training/optimization on MICCAI training set rather than training on one dataset and testing on another)
Comparisons to SOTA are based on reported numbers from other papers (no unified re-implementation), which may reduce fairness
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Clinically relevant task (RT OAR delineation) with whole-volume inference design and improved small-structure performance; however, lack of runtime reporting, missing deployment details, and limited cross-institution generalization evidence constrain immediate clinical translation.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training uses >50 subjects when combining datasets (XCAT n=30 + CT-ORG n=111 in Exp-2). CT-ORG overall is 140 cases (9 PET-CT excluded; 20 holdout test).

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset sources are clearly named and cited: XCAT phantoms and CT-ORG (TCIA) with reference/DOI.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: XCAT uses 5-fold CV with random 60/20/20 train/val/test; CT-ORG uses a 20-case holdout test and excludes 9 PET-CT cases; Exp-1/Exp-2 splits are tabulated (Table 1).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Ground-truth availability is described (XCAT fully labeled 22 organs; CT-ORG labeled 7 organs). However, the annotation protocol (who annotated, QA, inter-rater, tools) is not described; pseudo-label generation is described at a high level.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Model trained on XCAT is tested on CT-ORG (different dataset), which functions as cross-dataset evaluation. However, Exp-2 includes CT-ORG in training, and there is no truly independent external dataset beyond CT-ORG/XCAT.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architectures are identified (3D U-Net and 3D DenseVNet) and patch-wise/sliding-window strategy is described, but full architectural details (layers, channels, depth, normalization, residuals) are not provided in this paper (referred to prior work).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key preprocessing and patch sampling are specified (resampling, HU window, normalization, patch size/count, GPU, training/inference time). But critical training details are missing (optimizer, learning rate, epochs, batch size, augmentation, exact hyperparameters).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss function is not reported and no justification is provided.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is clearly described: resample to 2.5×2.5×5 mm, HU window [-1000, 800], normalize to [-1, 1], extract 32 patches/volume of size 128×128×128; sliding-window inference noted.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is mentioned.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Only Dice similarity coefficient (DSC) is reported; no additional metrics (e.g., HD95, ASD) are provided.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance analyses are reported; only fold-1 results are shown.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares 3D U-Net against DenseVNet on the same data. However, broader comparison to multiple state-of-the-art multi-organ CT segmentation methods/benchmarks is not provided.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ DSC values are reported for XCAT (22 organs) and for CT-ORG organs where labels exist (Table 2).

Q15_limitations.justification: Limitations are explicitly discussed: weaker performance on difficult organs (pancreas, thyroid, gallbladder, stomach), pseudo-labeling can degrade performance, and only one CV fold reported due to compute.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient/scan selection criteria are only partially described (XCAT phantoms; CT-ORG excludes 9 PET-CT due to resolution differences; no further inclusion/exclusion criteria or demographics).
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - evaluation uses consistent DSC computation and fixed folds/holdout, but reliance on pseudo-labels for missing organs and reporting only fold-1 may bias perceived performance and stability.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - per-organ Dice is reported, but only one metric, only one fold, and missing training details may lead to selective/incomplete reporting of outcomes.

detailed_extraction.title: Quality or Quantity: Toward a Unified Approach for Multi-organ Segmentation in Body CT

method.architecture_name: 3D U-Net (selected after comparison with 3D DenseVNet)
key_innovations: Empirical study of label quality (fully labeled XCAT) vs label quantity (adding CT-ORG with pseudo-labels for missing organs) for multi-organ 3D CT segmentation
Pseudo-labeling of missing organs in CT-ORG using an XCAT-trained model, followed by joint training on XCAT+CT-ORG
Direct comparison of 3D U-Net vs DenseVNet under matched data/preprocessing for 22-organ segmentation on XCAT

names: XCAT phantom dataset
CT-ORG (TCIA)
dataset.total_subjects: XCAT: 50; CT-ORG: 140 total (9 PET-CT excluded; 20 holdout test; 111 used for training in Exp-2)
dataset.train_subjects: Exp-1: XCAT train n=30; Exp-2: XCAT train n=30 + CT-ORG train n=111
dataset.val_subjects: XCAT val n=10 (fold-1 example; 5-fold CV described)
dataset.test_subjects: XCAT test n=10; CT-ORG holdout test n=20
dataset.slice_thickness: Resampled to 5 mm in z (target spacing 2.5×2.5×5 mm); original thickness not reported
dataset.image_size: Patch size 128×128×128; full volume dimensions not reported

results.inference_time: <20 seconds per volume
results.gpu_memory: 24 GB (NVIDIA TITAN RTX)

comparison_results.3D DenseVNet: Compared qualitatively/graphically; numeric per-organ DenseVNet results not provided in text (Figure 2 indicates lower DSC than U-Net).
comparison_results.3D U-Net: Selected as superior; per-organ DSC reported in Table 2.

implementation.training_time: Approximately 48 hours

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Demonstrates, for 22-organ 3D CT segmentation, that adding more cases via pseudo-labeling (CT-ORG) can improve performance for organs with true labels across datasets but can degrade performance for organs relying on pseudo-labels, highlighting the tradeoff between label quality and data quantity.
contribution_summary.novelty: A focused, controlled study combining a fully labeled phantom dataset (XCAT) with a partially labeled clinical dataset (CT-ORG) using pseudo-label completion, and quantifying how this affects per-organ segmentation performance in a unified 3D U-Net framework.
strengths: Clear experimental design contrasting quality-only training vs quality+quantity with pseudo-labels
Per-organ reporting across a large organ set (22 organs) and cross-dataset testing on CT-ORG
Practical preprocessing and inference setup (3D, patch-based, sliding window) with runtime reporting
weaknesses: Key training details missing (loss, optimizer, LR schedule, epochs, augmentation), limiting reproducibility
Evaluation limited to Dice only; no boundary metrics or statistical testing; only fold-1 results reported
Pseudo-label quality not quantified; no ablation on pseudo-label confidence/selection; no external dataset beyond XCAT/CT-ORG
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: the approach targets multi-organ segmentation for virtual imaging trials and could support downstream tasks (phantom generation/dosimetry), but limited reporting (single fold, single metric, no clinical scanner details) and reliance on phantom data/pseudo-labels constrain immediate clinical generalization claims.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses 250 CT volumes total with a 70/10/20 split; training set is ~175 volumes (>50).

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset sources are clearly named and described: MICCAI-Sliver07 (RAW), LiTS17 (RAW), and 3Dircadb (DICOM/IRCAD). Slice count ranges and in-plane resolution (512x512) are reported.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Explicit split reported: 70% training, 10% validation, 20% testing (applied to 250 volumes).

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Ground truth is implied via benchmark datasets with liver masks, but the paper does not describe annotation protocol, annotator expertise, or quality control.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Evaluates on three public datasets, but they appear pooled into one split rather than training on one dataset and externally validating on a fully independent held-out dataset/site. No true cross-dataset external validation protocol is described.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is described at a high level (DeepMedic, two pathways, 7 layers, CRF layer), but lacks detailed layer-by-layer specifications (kernel sizes, feature maps, receptive fields) and exact modifications beyond dual-input usage.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key training details are missing (optimizer, learning rate, batch size, epochs, patch sampling strategy, regularization, early stopping). Framework details are vague (Python + MATLAB; DL library not specified).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Loss function is not reported and therefore not justified.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is described in detail: resizing, HU windowing [-100,200], normalization to [0,1], scaling to [0,255], EED anisotropic diffusion denoising, and creation of two resolutions (265x265 and 128x128). Postprocessing is via an in-network CRF.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release or URL provided.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice (DSC) plus accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance analysis reported.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Provides a DSC comparison table against several prior methods, but comparisons are limited (single metric, unclear if same datasets/splits, and no direct re-implementation).

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Single-organ task (liver). Reports per-dataset results (Sliver07/LiTS/3Dircadb) rather than per-organ (not applicable beyond liver).

Q15_limitations.justification: Mentions under-segmentation at liver boundaries and dependence on preprocessing; however, failure case analysis is brief and qualitative with limited examples.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Uses public benchmarks but pools multiple datasets; patient inclusion/exclusion criteria and whether splits are patient-wise per dataset are not fully detailed, raising concerns about dataset mixing and representativeness.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Medium - Evaluation metrics are standard and consistently defined, but lack of details on inference protocol (e.g., patch aggregation, thresholding) and no external validation increases risk that performance is optimistic for the chosen split.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Reports several metrics and per-dataset results, but omits key training details (loss, optimizer, schedule) and does not report uncertainty, runtime, or comprehensive error analysis.

detailed_extraction.title: Efficient Liver Segmentation with 3D CNN Using Computed Tomography Scans

method.architecture_name: DeepMedic (modified dual-pathway input)
key_innovations: Dual-pathway DeepMedic input using two versions of the same preprocessed CT volume (265x265 and downsampled 128x128) to incorporate multi-resolution context
Preprocessing pipeline tailored to liver HU range (windowing) plus EED anisotropic diffusion denoising to improve segmentation robustness and efficiency under limited compute
Use of a 3D fully connected CRF layer (as part of DeepMedic) to reduce false positives/negatives

names: MICCAI-Sliver07
LiTS17
3Dircadb
dataset.image_size: In-plane 512x512 originally; resized to 265x265 for training/inference; second pathway uses downsampled 128x128. Slices per volume: Sliver07 64-502, LiTS17 42-1024, 3Dircadb 74-260.

contribution_summary.main_contribution: An efficient liver segmentation pipeline for abdominal CT that combines liver-focused HU preprocessing and denoising with a dual-pathway 3D DeepMedic model (with CRF refinement) to achieve ~0.93 Dice across three public datasets.
contribution_summary.novelty: Primarily an application/engineering contribution: adapting DeepMedic with two-resolution inputs plus a detailed preprocessing pipeline (HU windowing + EED denoising + normalization) aimed at improving segmentation quality and reducing computational burden, rather than proposing a fundamentally new architecture.
strengths: Uses multiple public benchmark datasets and reports per-dataset performance
Clear and detailed preprocessing description; includes an ablation-style comparison (preprocessed vs unprocessed) showing large performance gains
Reports multiple metrics (DSC, ACC, SEN, SPE) and compares DSC to prior work
weaknesses: Key training details missing (loss, optimizer, learning rate, epochs, batch size), limiting reproducibility
No statistical analysis (CIs/p-values) and no runtime/inference efficiency measurements despite 'efficient' framing
No true external validation protocol (e.g., train on one dataset, test on another) and unclear handling of dataset heterogeneity when pooling
Limited qualitative failure analysis; boundary under-segmentation noted but not deeply investigated
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: liver-only segmentation is clinically relevant as a preprocessing step for tumor analysis, and the method uses public CT data with standard metrics; however, lack of external site validation, missing reproducibility details, and no deployment-oriented reporting (runtime, robustness across scanners/protocols) limit immediate clinical translation.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training sets are 28 (Pancreas-CT re-annotated subset) and 32 (BTCV), both <50 training subjects; however, datasets are public and commonly used.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset provenance is described: Pancreas-CT from NIH Clinical Center (TCIA) and BTCV from Vanderbilt University Medical Center; challenge context for BTCV is stated.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Explicit random splits are provided: Pancreas-CT 28/5/10 and BTCV 32/5/10 (train/val/test), and the same splits are used across experiments.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: Manual organ masks are from public datasets; boundary ground truth is generated from masks via erosion and subtraction, and this process is described.

Q5_external_validation.justification: Evaluated on two public datasets (Pancreas-CT subset and BTCV), but no true external independent validation beyond these benchmarks (e.g., different institution held-out dataset) is reported.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architectures and multi-task topologies are clearly described (TSOL vs TSD) with figures and baseline details (3D U-Net/UNet++/Attention-UNet, blocks, channels, activations, pooling/upsampling).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Key training details are given (Adam, initial LR=0.001 with 0.9 decay each epoch, batch sizes, validation model selection, λ grid search). Some details are missing/unclear (number of epochs, weight decay, patch sampling strategy beyond fixed 144^3 resampling, random seed, exact LR schedule definition per epoch).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Losses are specified (multi-class Dice + BCE for boundary with λ). Motivation is provided (penalize boundary errors), but no deeper justification/ablation for alternative losses (e.g., boundary-aware Dice, focal/Tversky) beyond grid search on λ.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing is detailed: ROI cropping using provided bounding boxes, resampling to 144×144×144, boundary generation via erosion; augmentation was tested (affine) and then omitted.

Q10_code_availability.justification: Code is publicly available with a GitHub URL.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Uses Dice, Average Hausdorff Distance, Recall, and Precision; also includes trimap Dice analysis near boundaries.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Reports mean ± standard deviation and states metrics are averaged over 5 runs, but no formal statistical significance testing (p-values) or confidence intervals are provided.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against strong baseline architectures (3D U-Net, 3D U-Net++, 3D Attention U-Net) and their multi-task variants, but limited/no direct comparison to other published SOTA methods on BTCV/Pancreas-CT leaderboards.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Per-organ Dice and per-organ Avg. HD are reported in Tables 3 and 4.

Q15_limitations.justification: Some discussion of constraints is present (e.g., boundary labels are generated, not annotated; UNet++ deep supervision omitted; augmentation not used; parameter/time trade-offs). Limitations are not systematically enumerated with clinical/generalization implications.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - patient inclusion is described at dataset level, but the study uses small random splits without reporting stratification, random seeds, or repeated cross-validation; potential split sensitivity exists.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - outcomes are assessed consistently using the same preprocessing, metrics, and fixed splits; however, boundary ground truth is algorithmically derived and may bias toward methods that align with that boundary definition.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Low/Medium - multiple metrics and per-organ results are reported, but lack of external validation and lack of statistical significance testing may overstate robustness; limited comparison to external SOTA may omit relevant baselines.

detailed_extraction.title: Improved Abdominal Multi-Organ Segmentation via 3D Boundary-Constrained Deep Neural Networks

method.architecture_name: 3D U-Net / 3D U-Net++ / 3D Attention U-Net with boundary-constrained multi-task learning (TSOL and TSD variants)
key_innovations: Auxiliary boundary prediction task to improve 3D abdominal multi-organ segmentation via hard-parameter-sharing multi-task learning
Two multi-task topologies explored: TSOL (shared encoder-decoder with task-specific output layers) and TSD (shared encoder with task-specific decoders)
Boundary supervision generated from organ masks (erosion + subtraction) and used only during training; inference uses organ head only
Boundary-focused evaluation using trimap Dice in bands around organ borders
loss_functions: Total loss: L = L_RS + λ L_BD (λ tuned by grid search 0 to 2 step 0.5)
method.learning_rate: Initial 0.001; decays by factor 0.9 after every epoch
augmentation: Affine random transformations were tried but not used in final experiments (no augmentation).

names: Pancreas-CT (TCIA; 43 re-annotated multi-organ subset)
BTCV (MICCAI 2015 abdominal challenge dataset)
dataset.image_size: Cropped ROI using provided bounding boxes; resampled to 144×144×144 voxels

results.overall_dice: Best mean Dice reported: 0.820 (Pancreas-CT, Att-UNet-MTL-TSOL) and 0.778 (BTCV, Att-UNet-MTL-TSD); baseline bests around 0.799 (Pancreas-CT, UNet) and 0.753 (BTCV, UNet).
results.inference_time: 44–89 ms per volume depending on model (e.g., UNet 44 ms; UNet-MTL-TSOL 53 ms; UNet-MTL-TSD 68 ms; UNet++ 63 ms; Att-UNet-MTL-TSD 89 ms).

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces a boundary-constrained 3D multi-task learning framework that jointly learns organ masks and organ boundaries to improve abdominal multi-organ CT segmentation across multiple 3D encoder–decoder baselines.
contribution_summary.novelty: Rather than proposing a new backbone, the paper systematically integrates an auxiliary boundary prediction task into standard 3D segmentation networks and compares two hard-sharing multi-task topologies (TSOL vs TSD), showing consistent boundary/near-boundary improvements with minimal overhead (especially TSOL).
strengths: Clear, simple auxiliary-task formulation (Dice + BCE) with explicit λ tuning
Evaluated on two public CT datasets with fixed splits and multiple runs (mean±std)
Reports multiple metrics and per-organ results; includes boundary-focused trimap evaluation
Open-source code provided, aiding reproducibility
TSOL variant adds negligible parameters while improving Dice/HD in most settings
weaknesses: Small training sizes (28 and 32) and no cross-validation; results may be split-sensitive
No true external validation beyond the two benchmark datasets
Boundary ground truth is synthetic (morphological erosion), which may not reflect clinically meaningful boundaries and may bias training
Limited comparison to broader SOTA methods/leaderboards; mostly compares to internal baselines
No formal statistical significance testing (no CIs/p-values)
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Moderate: improving boundary accuracy is clinically relevant for abdominal organ delineation (e.g., radiotherapy planning), and inference is fast; however, generalization to diverse scanners/protocols and real-world clinical distributions is not established due to limited training size and lack of external multi-center validation.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Training uses 2500+ CT scans across four datasets (TotalSegmentator 1204; ChestOrgan 292; HNOrgan 447; EsoOrgan 640) with additional tumor datasets (304 esophageal cancer, 625 lung cancer). Clearly >50 subjects for training.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset names and provenance are described: TotalSegmentator is public; ChestOrgan/HNOrgan/EsoOrgan are in-house/private; lung cancer tumor dataset is public; esophageal tumor dataset is private.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: For each dataset, 20% randomly chosen as independent test set; remaining used for training+validation with a 4:1 train:val ratio. For NAS, an additional 60/30/10 split is described.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: States organs are 'labeled/annotated' and tumor masks are segmented; however, the paper text provided does not detail who annotated, annotation protocol, inter-rater checks, or quality control (likely in supplement).

Q5_external_validation.justification: Includes at least one public dataset (TotalSegmentator) and evaluates across multiple datasets/domains, but does not present a clearly independent external validation cohort from a separate institution beyond the included datasets; much evaluation is on private data.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: Architecture is clearly described: frozen 'General Encoder' (nnU-Net backbone) + task-specific decoders added per step; decoder NAS over kernel types; block-wise KD-based pruning; body-part and anomaly-aware output merging.

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Provides key training details: preprocessing windowing, resampling, patch size, batch sizes, epochs per stage, learning rates for NAS/KD/fine-tuning, framework (PyTorch), GPU (A100), and nnU-Net configuration ('3d-full res' + moreDA). Some nnU-Net internal defaults are not fully enumerated but overall reproducible.

Q8_loss_function.justification: Uses standard nnU-Net DSC+CE for segmentation and MSE for KD; rationale is implicit/standard rather than explicitly justified with ablations or theory.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Clearly specifies HU windowing [-1024, 1024], resampling to 0.75×0.75×3.0 mm, patch size 128×128×64, and nnU-Net moreDA augmentation; merging module described as post-processing-like step.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model release is indicated in the provided text.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports DSC, HD95, and ASD across datasets and overall.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: No confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, or statistical significance analyses are reported in the provided text.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against multiple continual semantic segmentation baselines (MiB, ILT, PLOP, LISMO, RCIL) and an upper bound (separate nnU-Net per dataset). Re-implements several methods in nnU-Net for fairness.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Main paper reports per-dataset and overall averages; it states that detailed per-organ/group results are in supplementary materials, but they are not present in the provided text.

Q15_limitations.justification: Some limitations are indirectly discussed (dependence on strong base dataset/encoder; private datasets; rule-based merging; runtime +12%), but there is no dedicated, comprehensive limitations section.

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient selection/inclusion criteria and acquisition heterogeneity are not fully specified for private datasets; random 20% test split is described but cohort definition details are limited.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Outcomes are assessed with standard metrics and consistent preprocessing; however, partial-label datasets and rule-based merging could advantage the proposed setup, and annotation consistency across datasets is not described.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Strong aggregate results are reported with extensive comparisons, but per-organ breakdown and some implementation/annotation details are deferred to supplementary; no statistical uncertainty is reported.

detailed_extraction.title: Continual Segment: Towards a Single, Unified and Non-forgetting Continual Segmentation Model of 143 Whole-body Organs in CT Scans

method.architecture_name: nnU-Net (3D full-resolution) based continual multi-decoder framework
key_innovations: Architecture-based continual semantic segmentation for multi-organ CT: freeze a 'General Encoder' after step-1 and add task-specific decoders for new datasets to avoid catastrophic forgetting without replay.
Decoder optimization via neural architecture search (NAS) over convolution kernel types {1x1x1, 3x3x1, P3D (3x3x1 then 1x1x3), 3x3x3} at the block level.
Progressive decoder compression using block-wise teacher-student knowledge distillation to replace blocks with 1x1x1 projection blocks under a Dice-drop tolerance (reported with τ=1%).

Body-part and anomaly-aware output merging using auxiliary body-part regression-derived regions and tumor predictions to select per-voxel outputs from multiple decoders via entropy/confidence weighting.
method.optimizer: Not explicitly stated (nnU-Net default typically SGD; not specified in provided text)
method.learning_rate: NAS/KD: initial LR 0.01; fine-tuning after pruning LR 0.001; other stages not fully specified
method.batch_size: 2 for main training; 4 for NAS training
method.epochs: Step-1 (TotalSegmentator): 8000 epochs; each subsequent step decoder: 1000 epochs; NAS: 400 + 600 epochs; KD: 500 epochs; post-pruning fine-tune: 500 epochs
augmentation: nnU-Net 'moreDA' augmentation (exact transforms not enumerated in provided text)

names: TotalSegmentator (public)
ChestOrgan (private)
HNOrgan (private)
EsoOrgan (private)
Esophageal tumor dataset (private, auxiliary anomaly head)
Lung cancer tumor dataset (public, auxiliary anomaly head)
dataset.total_subjects: 2583 unique patients across 4 organ datasets; plus 304 esophageal cancer and 625 lung cancer patients for tumor head
dataset.train_subjects: Per dataset: 80% used for train+val; within that, train:val=4:1 (i.e., 64% train, 16% val of total per dataset). Exact counts per dataset not explicitly computed in paper text.
dataset.val_subjects: Per dataset: 16% of total (from the 80% train+val split) under the 4:1 ratio; NAS uses an additional 60/30/10 split of train+val for training/NAS/validation.
dataset.test_subjects: Per dataset: 20% independent test set (randomly chosen).
dataset.slice_thickness: Not directly reported; volumes resampled to 3.0 mm spacing in z (0.75×0.75×3.0 mm).
dataset.image_size: Training patch size 128×128×64 voxels; full image dimensions not reported.

segmented_organs: 143 total structures across datasets: 103 TotalSegmentator structures (26 major organs, 59 bones, 10 muscles, 8 vessels; face removed), 31 chest structures (with overlaps: esophagus, trachea, SVC, pulmonary artery), 13 head & neck OARs, and esophagus in advanced cancer domain (EsoOrgan).
challenging_organs: Not explicitly listed; paper notes domain/body-part gaps and diseased esophagus as challenging for merging.

results.overall_dice: 88.74% mean DSC over all 143 organs (final model; both orders A and B reported identical for proposed method)
results.inference_time: Approximately +12% runtime vs a single nnU-Net segmenting 103 organs (exact seconds not provided).

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces an architecture-based continual 3D CT multi-organ segmentation framework that scales to 143 organs by freezing a general encoder, adding/pruning task-specific decoders, and merging outputs with body-part and anomaly awareness to avoid catastrophic forgetting.
contribution_summary.novelty: Unlike prior CSS methods that update shared parameters with distillation/regularization (and forget), this work uses a frozen universal encoder with independent decoders per task plus NAS+KD pruning to keep a single-model footprint, and a rule-based body-part/tumor-aware merging to reconcile overlapping organs and pathology-domain shifts.
strengths: Large-scale setting (143 organs) across multiple body parts and domains, closer to real clinical deployment than prior small-organ CSS studies.
Strong empirical results: near upper-bound performance while dramatically outperforming CSS baselines under body-part/domain shifts.
Scalability addressed via decoder NAS and KD-based pruning, keeping total decoder size ~0.98× a single nnU-Net decoder.
Explicit handling of pathology/domain change via anomaly-aware merging (not just class-incremental learning).
weaknesses: No public code/model release, limiting reproducibility.
Ground-truth annotation details and quality control are not described in the provided text (likely in supplement), and much evaluation relies on private datasets.
No statistical uncertainty (CIs/p-values) reported.
Merging is rule-based and depends on auxiliary body-part regression and tumor segmentation quality; potential brittleness not fully analyzed.
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: High potential: supports incremental addition of new organ/tumor tasks without retaining prior patient data (privacy/storage aligned) and achieves near-specialist-model accuracy; however, reliance on private datasets, lack of external multi-center validation reporting, and rule-based merging dependencies should be addressed before clinical deployment.

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Institutional dataset has 60 patients (80% train/20% test), meeting the >50 threshold for total subjects but not clearly for training subjects alone (implied 48). BCTV training size is not reported.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Two sources are described: public BCTV MICCAI 2015 challenge dataset and an institutional abdominal CT dataset from 60 patients.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: Institutional split is described as first 80% training and remaining 20% testing; no validation set described. Split methodology for BCTV is not described.

Q4_ground_truth.justification: The paper states 'manual contours/target contour' but does not describe who annotated, how many annotators, quality control, or inter-observer variability.

Q5_external_validation.justification: They evaluate on a public dataset (BCTV) and an institutional dataset, but do not present a clear train-on-one/test-on-independent-external-cohort setup; BCTV appears to be used as an additional evaluation dataset rather than explicit external validation.

Q6_architecture_description.justification: AFA-MI is described in detail and is applied to nnU-Net and TT-Vnet, but the underlying network configurations are not fully specified (relying on prior publications).

Q7_reproducibility.justification: Some key settings are provided (resampling, patch size, overlap, mixup parameter, epsilon=0.003, r=0.1, loss weights), but core training details are missing (optimizer, learning rate, epochs, batch size, hardware/framework).

Q8_loss_function.justification: Uses Dice+CE (0.5/0.5) and adversarial noise maximizes this loss; rationale is standard for segmentation but not deeply justified beyond common practice.

Q9_preprocessing.justification: Preprocessing/inference pipeline is clearly described: resampling to 2×2×3 mm, intensity normalization to [-1,1], patch sampling (64×64×32 mm, 4 patches/scan/iteration), sliding-window inference with 80% overlap, resampling outputs back, softmax+argmax.

Q10_code_availability.justification: No public code/model link is provided in the paper.

Q11_multiple_metrics.justification: Reports Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) and Hausdorff Distance (HD) on the public dataset; DSC is also reported extensively for noise robustness experiments.

Q12_statistical_analysis.justification: Uses Mann-Whitney U-test and reports p-values for multiple organs/conditions.

Q13_comparison_sota.justification: Compares against strong baselines (nnU-Net and TT-Vnet) without AFA-MI, but does not compare to a broader set of published SOTA augmentation/robustness methods.

Q14_per_organ_results.justification: Provides per-organ DSC and per-organ HD (Table 1) and per-organ DSC across multiple noise levels (Table 2).

Q15_limitations.justification: Limitations are not explicitly discussed in a dedicated section, but the conclusion/future work implies limitations (need more networks, more metrics, and testing on artifact-heavy data).

risk_of_bias.selection_bias: Medium - Patient inclusion/exclusion criteria and acquisition details for the institutional cohort are not described; BCTV is public but split usage is unclear.
risk_of_bias.performance_bias: Low/Medium - Outcomes are assessed consistently with standard metrics (DSC/HD) and same test sets; however, lack of annotation protocol details and unclear validation strategy may affect consistency assumptions.
risk_of_bias.reporting_bias: Medium - Many per-organ results are reported, but key reproducibility items (training hyperparameters, full dataset details, code) are missing; also HD is not reported for the institutional noise experiments.

detailed_extraction.title: Deep Learning-based Multi-Organ CT Segmentation with Adversarial Data Augmentation

method.architecture_name: nnU-Net; Token-based Transformer V-Net (TT-Vnet) with AFA-MI augmentation
method.architecture_type: CNN (nnU-Net) and Transformer-based hybrid (TT-Vnet backbone) with adversarial feature augmentation
key_innovations: Adversarial Feature Attack for Medical Image (AFA-MI) augmentation applied to intermediate encoder features rather than only input-space perturbations
Multi-scale feature attacks across encoder blocks with constrained perturbation range (Sr) and feature-statistics injection (mean/variance matching)
Robustness evaluation under controlled Gaussian noise corruption at multiple noise levels
loss_functions: Adversarial objective: FGSM maximization of segmentation loss on intermediate features
augmentation: AFA-MI adversarial feature augmentation (FGSM on intermediate encoder features; epsilon=0.003; constraint ratio r=0.1; multi-scale constraints k in {0.1, 0.05, 0.025, 0.0125})
Noise robustness testing (not training augmentation): additive Gaussian noise with mean 0 and std in {0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01} applied to test images

names: BCTV (Beyond the Cranial Vault) MICCAI 2015 challenge dataset
Institutional abdominal CT dataset
dataset.total_subjects: Institutional: 60; BCTV: not reported in paper text
dataset.val_subjects: Not reported (no explicit validation set described)
dataset.ct_scanner: Not reported
dataset.slice_thickness: Not reported (volumes resampled to 2×2×3 mm spacing)
dataset.image_size: Patch-based: 64×64×32 (units stated as mm in paper; effectively voxel patch size after resampling)

challenging_organs: vena cava (lower DSC and higher HD relative to other organs on BCTV)
spinal cord (largest clean-image improvement on institutional dataset; suggests difficulty)

results.overall_dice: Public (BCTV): nnU-Net+AFA-MI DSC=0.85; TT-Vnet+AFA-MI DSC=0.86. Institutional (clean, N(0,0)): nnU-Net+AFA-MI average DSC=0.92.

implementation.training_time: Not reported

contribution_summary.main_contribution: Introduces AFA-MI, an adversarial feature-space data augmentation method for 3D multi-organ CT segmentation that improves accuracy and substantially increases robustness to noise when integrated into nnU-Net and TT-Vnet.
contribution_summary.novelty: Unlike standard augmentations or input-space adversarial training, AFA-MI perturbs intermediate encoder features via FGSM under constraints and injects perturbations through feature-statistics matching, using multi-scale feature attacks to encourage out-of-distribution feature robustness.
strengths: Plug-and-play augmentation demonstrated on two different segmentation backbones (nnU-Net and TT-Vnet)
Evaluated with both region (DSC) and boundary (HD) metrics on the public dataset
Includes statistical testing (Mann-Whitney U-test with p-values)
Robustness study across multiple controlled Gaussian noise levels shows large gains under severe corruption (e.g., average DSC 0.67 vs 0.48 at std=0.01)
weaknesses: Insufficient dataset/annotation details (no description of contouring protocol, annotators, QA, or BCTV split specifics)
Incomplete training reproducibility (optimizer, learning rate, epochs, batch size, framework/hardware not reported)
No external validation in the strict sense (e.g., train on one domain and test on a fully independent external cohort with fixed protocol)
No public code/model release
Limited comparison to other robustness/augmentation methods beyond 'no AFA-MI' baselines
contribution_summary.clinical_applicability: Relevant to radiotherapy OAR delineation workflows: improved robustness to noise/artifacts can reduce contouring failures in real-world CT. However, clinical translation is limited by missing annotation/protocol details, lack of external multi-center validation, and absence of deployment metrics (runtime, failure analysis).

Q1_dataset_size.justification: Uses 80 annotated CT volumes for experiments with 5-fold CV (64 train / 16 test per fold), exceeding the >50 training-subject guideline.

Q2_dataset_source.justification: Dataset is clearly identified as Medical Segmentation Decathlon (MSD) colon cancer CT dataset; acquisition site (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center) and scan parameters are described.

Q3_train_test_split.justification: 5-fold cross-validation is explicitly stated with per-fold counts (64 training, 16 test).
